File.Name: a220c_a216-066.jpg
Notes: 59508033 My perfect grandkids - Kelvin on the left, and Ottokar on the right - I think! It's easy to get these nearly-identical twins confused! (Portland, Oregon, 16 February 2010)

File.Name: a221a_a221-014.jpg
Notes:
59510600 Beautiful Coccora mushrooms - the Italian name means cocoon - Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma - growing in the woods today under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's growing with a bouquet of California Bedstraw (Gallium californicum) and other spring wild plants. These mushrooms are very pale - almost white. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2010)

I guess we brought Southern California weather to Oregon - it was sunny last week - and returned to rainy Oregon weather back home.

It's good to be back home among the mushrooms!
CommonNames: Coccora; Coast Live Oak; California Bedstraw
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Quercus agrifolia; Gallium californicum
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Quercus agrifolia; Gallium californicum

File.Name: a221b_a213-005.jpg
Notes:
59510296 This is a new Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera on a leaf of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) plant family. You can see its mighty sword-like proboscis. These insects look fast-moving, but they are really v_e_r_y s_l_o_w. I've never seen one actually catch a prey. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2010)

It's interesting that these are among the last and the first insects I find around our rainy season.

File.Name: a221c_a214-049.jpg
Notes:
59509833 Interesting new flowers of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2010)

These trees started flowering early this year, but they've finally caught up with nature.

File.Name: a222a_a214-042.jpg
Notes: 59537904 Beautiful new flowers of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The pistil and stamens are perfectly placed to move pollen from flower to flower on the foreheads of hummingbirds. The leaves of the plant make interesting tea, and you can suck on the flowers for a taste of sweet nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2010)

File.Name: a222b_a213-001.jpg
Notes: 59537426 This is a calyptrate muscoid fly - probably a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae. It's perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) about 10 feet from our compost pile. I think this is our most common "house fly". It's pretty enough in a photo, but I wouldn't want to see it walking across my dinner! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2010)
ScientificName: calyptrate muscoid; Pollenia; Calliphoridae; Venegasia carpesioides
associatedTaxa: calyptrate muscoid; Pollenia; Calliphoridae; Venegasia carpesioides

File.Name: a222c_a213-033.jpg
Notes: 59536753 Interesting Common Club Coral (Clavariadelphus pistillaris or kin) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. They are related to the beautiful coral mushrooms, but they don't branch like coral mushrooms do. Are they edible? My book says they are "Harmless. The taste and texture are reminiscent of stale rope." (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2010)
CommonNames: Common Club Coral; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: a223a_a213-034.jpg
Notes:
59569836 Here is another brilliant Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom in the woods growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2010)

It looks beautiful, and it has an interesting hot spicy taste if you just nibble. But as the name suggests, it is quite poisonous. Not "drop down dead" poisonous, but "wish you were dead" stomach upset. There is a rumor that parboiling destroys the toxins.

File.Name: a223b_a213-020.jpg
Notes:
59569550 This looks like a Cortinarius mushroom, but it has no sign of a cortina. I believe these are colorful Phaeocollybia fallax mushrooms in the woods, though I previously mis-identified them as P. olivacia. The violet gills are remarkable. It has an broadly umbonate cap, no apparent cortina, and a very long stem. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2010)

I have a photo showing the long stem at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/59155651
Edibility is unknown. I wouldn't mess with it.

File.Name: a223c_a221-010.jpg
Notes: 59569109 I call this the "brain mushroom" when I'm hiking in the woods with kids. It's a strange Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushroom in the woods. It's said to be edible, but I'm not tempted. This is a spring mushroom. It's a sign that our mushroom season will soon be over, but there's more rain expected tonight to extend the season. Let it rain! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2010)

File.Name: a224a_a211-071.jpg
Notes:
59603430 Here is a perfect new "Witch's Butter" (Tremella mesenterica or kin) mushroom growing right next to a slimy old fungus of the same sort. The fresh mushroom is decorated with tiny Springtail (order Collembia) insects. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2010)

I guess it's edible. My book says it's "harmless but flavorless" - so why bother? These gelatinous "mushrooms" are mostly water. They shrivel up to almost nothing when dry, but can revive quickly after a rain.

File.Name: a224b_a223-020.jpg
Notes:
59602916 I love these Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plants in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. This photo shows the whole plant. These native wildflowers are having a good year. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2010)

I have a close shot of the flowers at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/58985210

The flower heads are mostly red bracts, but you can see a few real flowers sticking out. This plant is a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) in this place.

The flower heads of these Indian Warrior plants are sold in the Internet "legal high" market. The effects are described as "sweet in aroma and tranquilizing" (Wikipedia), and they sell for quite a bit. There were probably $100s of dollars worth of the buds in the place where I found this plant. (I'm not telling where!)

I'd rather enjoy the flowers. This "wildcrafting" of native plants - especially these interesting root-parasites that can't be cultivated - bothers me!

File.Name: a224c_a211-066.jpg
Notes: 59602007 Interesting mushrooms - but tiny! I guess these are little Mycena mushrooms growing from the oak leaves in the woods. They key to Mycena capillaris in my mushroom book, but I reckon that name just stands in for a whole bunch of species that can only be identified under a microscope. Edibility is academic for a mushroom this small. The caps are decorated with tiny Springtail (order Collembia) insects. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2010)

File.Name: a225a_a214-043.jpg
Notes: 59646585 These flowers are tiny, but there are zillions of them in the mountains right now. They are the individual flowers of BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) plant family. The native shrubs are covered with the tiny flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2010)

File.Name: a225b_a214-059.jpg
Notes:
59646327 This is the gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They're not as common as the "oak apples" on Scrub or Valley Oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2010)

This one looks like a poppy seed roll!
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: a225c_a211-074.jpg
Notes:
59646125 These are the tiny bisexual winter galls of one of the tiny Cynipidae wasps of the order Hymenoptera) that also produce summer galls on our Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia shrubs in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2010)

They have a complicated life cycle. These winter galls produce male and female wasps which mate and lay eggs which produce unisexual summer galls. The summer galls hatch into only females that produce the bisexual galls that produce males and females, and the cycle repeats.

File.Name: a226a_a225-058.jpg
Notes:
59663950 Look close in the center of this photo!

I didn't look close for a moment, and I found one of my after-school Hike Club kids high up in a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) tree! Pretty cool, but don't tell the school insurance company! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2010)

I told this kid that he should consider Humboldt State University - where they climb the biggest trees, the redwoods!

These native pine trees have head-size cones. I wouldn't camp under a Coulter Pine tree!

File.Name: a226b_a225-048.jpg
Notes:
59663511 Like little monkeys - you can't keep the boys off the rocks!

Here are my after-school Hike Club boys at Knapp's Castle in the mountains near home. "The Castle" is the ruins of a mountain mansion that was destroyed by a wildfire in the 1930s. Only stone walls and chimneys remain, but the chimneys are a great challenge! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2010)

File.Name: a226c_a225-034.jpg
Notes:
59663351 The Grateful Dead used to sing, "We are the eyes of the world..." I think that song should be an anthem for photographers!

Here are my after-school Hike Club kids at Knapp's Castle in the mountains near home. "The Castle" is the ruins of a mountain mansion that was destroyed by a wildfire in the 1930s. Only stone walls and chimneys remain. Looking through the castle walls is like looking through giant spectacles - the eyes of the world. It's great fun to climb on the ruins, and the views of the Santa Ynez Valley are the best! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2010)

File.Name: a227a_a227-017.jpg
Notes:
59684238 Do you ever wonder what insects do in the rain?

This Bumblebee (family Bombus, order Hymenoptera) is taking shelter under a leaf of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The leaf is a good umbrella! (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2010)

It rained hard last night. The storm peaked at 4.5 inches (11.5 cm) per hour according to a neighbor's automated rain gauge on the web. We've had over 3.5 inches (9 cm) so far and it's still raining. It's Banana Slug weather!

I got this photo in the rain and wind today, so it's not quite sharp - but it's still pretty cool!
Locality:
9 cm) so far and it's still raining. It's Banana Slug weather!

I got this photo in the rain and wind today

File.Name: a227b_a227-027.jpg
Notes:
59683971 We're having Banana Slug weather!

This giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) has an interesting hitchhiker. Look at the tiny wasp (order Hymenoptera) in the upper right of my photo. In another photo, it seems to be ovipositing.

Do slugs have to worry about parasitic wasps too? I've never heard of a wasp predator of these magnificent slugs, but maybe this is it. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2010)

File.Name: a227c_a227-014.jpg
Notes: 59683617 Look close! This brilliant Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom is reflecting the woods from the rainwater collected in its cap. It's growing under - and reflecting - Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2010)

File.Name: a228a_a228-040.jpg
Notes: 59709154 Yum - a Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom from the woods for dinner! It's one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2010)

File.Name: a228b_a228-030.jpg
Notes: 59709020 Interesting Volvariella speciosa in the Pluteacea family growing in the weeds along the road. It looks like an Amanita with a "death cup" on the stem, but it doesn't have a ring and it has pink spores, though you can't see them in my photo of young caps. It's said to be edible - but be very careful you don't confuse it with a deadly Amanita mushroom! People have died making this mistake. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2010)

File.Name: a228c_a228-031.jpg
Notes: 59708697 I found this strange Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum or kin) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees today. It seems that the later we go in the mushroom season, the stranger the mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2010)

File.Name: a301a_a301-058.jpg
Notes:
59736995 Intense blue color of native Larkspur flowers - I guess Delphinium patens, but cp. D. parryi - in the Ranunculaceae plant family. This one is growing in the woods near Lake Cachuma, where I stopped for a hike on my way to school today. (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 March 2010)

We've had 33 inches (84 cm) of rain so far this rain-year. That's better than the 18 inches we got last year, but not nearly the 83 inches we got in 1982-3! It's 123% of normal. There's more rain expected tomorrow and Wednesday, and the frogs are singing. Keep it coming!
Locality: 84 cm) of rain so far this rain-year.  That's better than the 18 inches we got last year

File.Name: a301b_a301-053.jpg
Notes:
59735970 Sweet-smelling white flowers of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family growing near Lake Cachuma on my way to school. The individual flowers are tiny, but elegant - and there are zillions of them! The purple styles are remarkable. (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 March 2010)

It still feels like winter up here in the mountains, but it's definitely spring in the valley!

File.Name: a301c_a301-065.jpg
Notes:
59735614 Here is a beautiful flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. You have to lift them up to see their insides - I propped this one up with a stick. They're still going strong in the recently burned woods near Lake Cachuma on my way to school. (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 March 2010)

The perennial roots used to be collected locally for export to China as a medicinal herb.

File.Name: a302a_a301-050.jpg
Notes:
59763415 Here are beautiful mushrooms of the Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus). Unlike most of their kind, these mushrooms seem to have no latex when you cut the gills. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2010)

They are said to be edible and good, but I've never tried them. They have a mild odor instead of the strong maple syrup odor of the true Candy Cap (L. fragilis). They are growing on the edge of our garden, under a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family.

Our Internet service is being flaky tonight. I hope these photos appear.
Locality: L. fragilis). They are growing on the edge of our garden

File.Name: a302b_a301-052.jpg
Notes:
59762885 Under the sea, perhaps a group of sea anemones?

No, here is a beautiful cluster of the "fruticose" Gold Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus) growing on a living branch of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. The "gold eyes" are reproductive structures, but I'm not sure if they belong to the lichen or just the fungal part of the fungus-algae community that a lichen represents. (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 March 2010)

I don't know the local lichens very well. Here's a whole new world to discover. Maybe next year!

File.Name: a302c_a228-036.jpg
Notes:
59762038 There's new life in the woods! The plants with the long parallel-veined leaves are rare native Green Rein Orchids (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2010)

I saw many seedlings in this place two years ago. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/31120297

But it's curious that in the last drought year I didn't find any. There are at least 20 mature-looking plants this year. I hope they flower!

File.Name: a303a_a303-076.jpg
Notes:
59795037 I'm pretty sure this is a strange Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) growing from the fallen bits of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree on the ground in the woods today. I think it is the species Physarum virascens. Look how it's covering the leaf - stem and all. This is not typical fungus behavior. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2010)

Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part protist. Their old name is "Mycetozoa" - which literally means "fungus-animals". They are really animals, not fungi.

They live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying forest debris. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes. I think this is the end of the streaming stage.

They will end up as tiny little mushroom-like structures that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another.

See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/47730302

File.Name: a303b_a303-081.jpg
Notes:
59794311 To paraphrase William Burroughs, "Nobody owns life, but anyone who can find this mushroom owns death."

This is a beautiful but deadly poisonous Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom close to the garden. It's growing closest to a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree, but there's also a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the neighborhood. Look close - you can see the mushroom shedding her partial veil over the gills! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2010)

I always think of these deadly mushrooms as "she" not "it". They are elegant and beautiful, though this one is a little chunky.

File.Name: a304a_a227-012.jpg
Notes:
59825823 This must be "The Blusher" - an Amanita rubescens mushroom in the woods. They fruit in the fall, but they have a second fruiting in the spring. This one doesn't seem to have any volva at all, but it has definite "amanita vibes"! Apparently it is edible when cooked, but it is indigestible or even poisonous when raw. I haven't tried it. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2010)

This spring form of the mushroom seems much more colorful than the autumn form. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/57000838

File.Name: a304b_a227-007.jpg
Notes: 59825518 This well-named Yellow-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus xanthodermus) mushroom looks beautiful and edible, but it tastes terrible. It has a strong phenolic odor and taste, like an old fashioned doctor's office. Don't eat it! All parts of the mushroom stain bright yellow as you handle it, especially the very base of the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 27 February 2010)

File.Name: a304c_a303-071.jpg
Notes: 59825313 Here is another photo of the remarkable Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) growing on the fallen leaves and twigs of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree on the ground in the woods today. I think it is the species Physarum virascens. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2010)

File.Name: a305a_a304-126.jpg
Notes:
59855100 My after-school Hike Club kids yesterday at the "frog pond" on Kinevan Ranch near our old cabin in the woods. We found lots of frogs! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2010)

I used to bring my own kids out here 30 years ago when they were just 2 and 3 years old. We would sit by the pond and say "shush" and be very quiet - and then thousands of Pacific Tree Frogs (Pseudacris regilla) would start croaking in a deafening chorus!

File.Name: a305b_a304-109.jpg
Notes:
59854701 I took my after-school Hike Club kids yesterday to visit our old cabin on Kinevan Ranch across the highway from our new home. It's known as the "Chinamen's cabin" - the story is that it was first built by Chinese workers on the stage coach line that ran nearby in the 1860s. Julie and I used to rent this cabin when it was seeing better days about 30 years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2010)

Now, it's just another tumble-down cabin in the woods - but for me this will always be "The Cabin." Julie and I lived here for 10 years with no electricity or phone back in the 70s and into the 80s. We had artesian spring water, propane refigerator and hot water, Aladdin kerosene lamps, and a dangerous - but wonderful - wood-fire sauna. We had a large garden, along with goats, pigs, rabbits, ducks, chickens, and geese. I started learning local natural history here, we played music into the night, I built my first computer using wire-wrap and a propane torch to solder, and I started teaching. Our two kids started life here. So many memories in this tumble-down cabin that make it a glow for me now... Maybe every tumble-down cabin in the woods has this glow in memory for someone.

We moved a few miles away to our new house in 1983. The cabin has been abandoned for many years except for the deer mice and wood rats. Now it's a "Hanta Hotel" - with a threat of Hanta Virus from rodent droppings just breathing the air inside. Life goes on.

File.Name: a305c_a304-108.jpg
Notes: 59853910 Yesterday, I took my after-school Hike Club kids to our old cabin on Kinevan Ranch across the highway from our new home. Julie and I lived up this dirt road 30 years ago, and the place is full of memories for me. These trees along are mostly California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees in the Lauraceae plant family. It's a magical place! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2010)

File.Name: a306a_a305-140.jpg
Notes:
59882714 A perfect cluster of the well-named native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primulaceae plant family wildflower growing in the woods near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 March 2010)

It's been dark and gloomy today. Finally the rain is starting to fall...

File.Name: a306b_a306-006.jpg
Notes:
59882434 This is a lesson on mushroom anatomy...

Here are two caps of the handsome Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom. They have a "partial veil" that covers the immature gills. In the mushroom on the left, the veil is still closed - but on the right, it's turned into a ring or "annulus" on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2010)

These mushrooms look delicious, but they are poisonous to many people.

File.Name: a306c_a304-111.jpg
Notes: 59881896 One of my after-school Hike Club kids found this "Western Blue-tailed Skink (Eumeces skiltonianus on our hike last Thursday. Skinks look like true lizards, but they have finer scales. This one seems to have recently lost it's fine blue tail - maybe just a few minutes ago? It will regenerate it. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2010)

File.Name: a307a_a306-013.jpg
Notes: 59895750 I'm pretty sure this is a rusty-brown Cortinarius fulmineus or kin mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. You can see the yellow/orange-stained hairs on the stem that brand this species as a Cortinarius, and the bulbous base that places it in the subgenus Bulbopodium. Edibility is unknown - mainly because there are so many species that are very hard to tell apart. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2010)
ScientificName: Cortinarius fulmineus; Quercus agrifolia; Cortinarius; Bulbopodium
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius fulmineus; Quercus agrifolia; Cortinarius; Bulbopodium

File.Name: a307b_a306-008.jpg
Notes:
59895418 This is the Golden Milky Cap (Lactarius alnicola) mushroom in the woods growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree.

I've had three separate people ask me if these are chanterelles this week. No such luck! These mushrooms have true gills with sharp edges, whereas the chanterelles have blunt fold-like ridges which extend down the stem. They have a "latently acrid taste" - mild at first, but then extremely peppery. (I like to nibble!) (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2010)
CommonNames: Golden Milky Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: a307c_a306-021.jpg
Notes:
59895019 These are the same strange Slime Molds (Myxomycetes) growing from a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree on the ground in the woods that I show in a previous photo. I believe it is the species Physarum virascens. They have stopped streaming, and are now forming sessile sporangia. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2010)

For more info about these curious creature, see my recent photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/59795037

File.Name: a308a_a308-024.jpg
Notes:
59940596 Here is a full basket of delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms that we found at my Dad's house yesterday! They were growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in his backyard. They will become our dinner tonight - see my [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2010)

It's been an odd year for the Chantrelles. I've only collected them a few times up here in the mountains, but they are doing well in the foothills.

Chanterelles sell for $15-20 a pound in local markets. We found $100s of dollars worth at my Dad's yesterday! But I don't approve of "wildcrafting" any native mushrooms or plants for sale. Let people find their own damn mushrooms. This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings!

File.Name: a308b_a308-035.jpg
Notes:
59940142 Yum - here's bunch of delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms for dinner tonight! We went to visit my Dad yesterday, and we found a bonanza of tasty chanterelles growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in his backyard. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2010)

(Continued from my [Previous] photo...)

File.Name: a308c_a308-030.jpg
Notes:
59939882 Yum - here's bunch of delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms for dinner tonight! We went to visit my Dad yesterday, and we found a bonanza of tasty chanterelles growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in his backyard. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2010)

The first step to cooking chanterelles is to clean them by trimming without water to remove the gills and any (rare) bad spots. Then dump them into a dry hot frying pan on high heat to let them "stew in their own juices" with a bit of fresh pepper. It's remarkable how much juice they make on heir own. Let them cook down until the juice is almost gone. This concentrates the flavor.

See my [Next] photo for more instructions!

File.Name: a309a_a309-039.jpg
Notes:
59968449 It was too windy for photos today, but I went out anyway to look for new mushrooms that don't blow in the wind. I found this interesting Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family in a place protected from the wind. Maybe the fly is genus Bombylius? It has a mighty proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2010)

Very slowly, the insects are starting to come out! I've been seeing Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterflies on the wing, but no photos yet.

File.Name: a309b_a228-045.jpg
Notes:
59967878 Here is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae in the order Diptera) lapping pollen from a tiny flower of BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. If you look very close, you can see another tinier fly on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2010)

This fly is not quite sharp. It's always a problem when I transition from shooting mushrooms to insects. It's a completely different mindset, and totally different camera settings. Mushrooms don't move, so I go for very long exposures at low ISO and high f/ numbers. Insects do move, so I need faster exposures. I get confused, as with this photo at 1/40 sec @ f/25 @ ISO 200. Darn, I should have remembered to stop down for a sharper image!

File.Name: a309c_a308-029.jpg
Notes:
59967026 I captured the light from this tiny Wasp (Hymenoptera) resting in the sun on a cold morning. It's perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2010)

Very slowly, the insects are starting to come out!

File.Name: a310a_a310-075.jpg
Notes:
59999551 I found the first shy flower of beautiful Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) in the Liliaceae plant family on the lower slopes of Figueroa Mountain after school today. This is the whole plant. (Figueroa Mountain, 10 March 2010)

I had some time to kill before my board meeting with the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society, so I drove up the mountain despite the wind. These lily flowers have deep chocolate/burgandy colors - like that expensive 70% cocoa dark chocolate.

File.Name: a310b_a310-074.jpg
Notes:
59999401 I believe these are new flowers of Fremont's Death Camas aka Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family. As two of it's many common names suggest, it's quite poisonous. (Figueroa Mountain, 10 March 2010)

I've been confused about these flowers, and I give thanks to my naturalist friend Lynn Watson (http://natureshutterbug.com/) for setting me straight. The stamens are much shorter that the petals, so this must be Zigadenus fremontii and not Z. venenosus as I once thought.

I was confused because it's cousin Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus which grows close to home looks so different. This plant is much larger with more robust flowers that have greenish glands on the petals. I hope to get new photos soon - maybe this weekend - but see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/49343050

The south-facing slopes across the Santa Ynez Valley to the north (in our east-west mountains) and the north-facing slopes where I live are very different! This photo is across the valley.
Locality: http://natureshutterbug.com/) for setting me straight.  The stamens are much shorter that the petals
CommonNames: Fremont's Death Camas; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; fremontii; Liliaceae; Zigadenus fremontii; Z. venenosus; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; fremontii; Liliaceae; Zigadenus fremontii; Z. venenosus; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus

File.Name: a310c_a306-002.jpg
Notes: 59998876 Here are sporophytes of tiny Liverworts (Marchantia or Asterella?) - related to the mosses - on the ground still damp from recent rains. In some drier years, I never see these fruiting bodies. My first thought is surfing palm trees! (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2010)

File.Name: a311a_a311-081.jpg
Notes:
60026204 I believe this is an Afranius Duskywing (Erynnis afranius) butterfly in the woods today. It doesn't have the white hindwing fringe of our other common duskwings, and it lacks the markings of E. propertius. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2010)

I have today and Friday off from school. It's student conference time, and I don't do that any more. Wahoo!

There's been a change of shift in the woods. I did my usual morning walk and found few mushrooms. I guess the drying winds for the last two days did them in. But I did see insects and butterflies! I didn't get many photos, but there is a change. Maybe tomorrow...

File.Name: a311b_a311-084.jpg
Notes:
60025733 We had lizard weather today...

Here's a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) enjoying the sun to prove it! It's already starting to show hints of mating colors. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2010)

File.Name: a311c_a311-091.jpg
Notes: 60025543 Maybe these are shy Deer Mushrooms (Pluteus cervinus) growing from the wood of an old California Bay (Umbellularia californica) stump in the woods - they've grown here before. But I'm not sure. The spores look more brown than pink, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2010)
ScientificName: Pluteus cervinus) growing from the wood of an old California Bay (Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Pluteus cervinus) growing from the wood of an old California Bay (Umbellularia californica

File.Name: a312a_a312-119.jpg
Notes:
60051746 This is an uncommon Slender-pod Jewelflower (Caulanthus heterophyllus var. pseudosimulans) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's a fancy mustard. I don't think I've ever found it before. I found it growing near West Camino Cielo Road in the mountains, a few miles from the highway near the trail-head to the Playground. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2010)

This plant was rejected for CNPS status, but it's not common. There's only one Santa Barbara County record in the Jepson database. My flora reports it in 1929, 1930, 1956, and 1986. I'll send a report to the Santa Barbara Botanic Garden.

This is not the very rare Santa Barbara Jewelfower (Caulanthus amplexicaulis var. barbarae) re-discovered recently at Midland School on serpentine soil:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/34486353@N07/3441304607/in/set-72157618544570744/
ScientificName: Caulanthus heterophyllus; pseudosimulans; Brassicaceae; not; Caulanthus amplexicaulis var. barbarae
associatedTaxa: Caulanthus heterophyllus; pseudosimulans; Brassicaceae; not; Caulanthus amplexicaulis var. barbarae

File.Name: a312b_a312-102.jpg
Notes:
60050348 "Yes, we're open for business!"

This is a perfect Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a flower cluster of native Tauschia arguta in the Apiaceae plant family. "Why don't you land here for a taste?" (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2010)

I like to nibble on the seeds of this plant later in spring - they have a pleasant caraway taste ("kummel" in German!) - but I have no idea if they are considered "edible"!

File.Name: a312c_a312-127.jpg
Notes: 60049884 Here is a beautiful native Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. This plant is a fire-follower, but it actually follows any disturbance in the woods, including bulldozing. This one is growing on ground burned by the Jesusita Fire last spring. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2010)

File.Name: a313a_a312-131.jpg
Notes:
60080403 Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a native flower of Gambel's Phacelia (Phacelia viscida var. albiflora). This is an interesting flower. It's common in this place on the ocean side of the Santa Ynez Mountains on the way to town. It looks like a smaller Phacelia grandiflora. It's growing on ground burned by the Jesusita Fire last spring. (South slopes of the Santa Ynez Mountains, 12 March 2010)

I believe I have a photo of the species plant (Phacelia viscida var. viscida ) way back in the archives from 2003 - though it's misidentified in the caption:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/3738751

The species plants have blue flowers. They are more beautiful, but less common - at least in a fire year.

There's a curious silence about this native plant in the Jepson manual and at CalPhotos!
Locality:

File.Name: a313b_a312-147.jpg
Notes:
60079305 Here is an acorn of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family sprouting. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2010)

*Sign* It was a good acorn year, and there are hundreds of them putting down roots in the garden. They will become our newest "weed"!

File.Name: a313c_a313-007.jpg
Notes:
60078990 Here's an odd Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum or kin) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. You can see in this photo that it has a fluted stalk. Note the branching ribs on the outside of the cup. Edibility unknown - but I'm not really tempted. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2010)

It was breezy today after we had just a trace of rain last night. Just enough to keep up our record of raining Saturdays!

File.Name: a314a_a314-009.jpg
Notes:
60104856 Here is a beautiful new Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly resting (for the moment) on the ground today. I love the peaches 'n cream colors of these butterflies! This one is female - a bit less intensely colored than the male. They mostly fly up and down the trails without landing. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2010)

No wonder this one landed - it's tired, I chased it for about 10 minutes to get this photo!

File.Name: a314b_a314-027.jpg
Notes:
60104485 These native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) have been busy placing tiny Aphids (family Aphididae) in the order Homoptera on the new flower buds of the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae) plant family right outside our bathroom window. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2010)

I wonder where the ants get those aphids at the start of spring?
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: a314c_a314-008.jpg
Notes:
60104055 Here is a tiny wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) resting on a delicate native flower of native Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2010)

This Lithophragma cymbalaria has opposite leaves on its flower stalks and favors the sun, but we also have the similar Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine) with alternate leaves on the stem that favors the shade. These little differences help make our local plants interesting year after year!
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Lithophragma cymbalaria; Saxifragaceae; Lithophragma cymbalaria; Lithophragma affine
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Lithophragma cymbalaria; Saxifragaceae; Lithophragma cymbalaria; Lithophragma affine

File.Name: a315a_a315-040.jpg
Notes: 60133549 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in the woods with its prey in the order Hemiptera. The spider is resting on a leaf of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family. I got this photo in the woods near Lake Cachuma on my way to school today. (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 March 2010)
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Wild Cucumber; Chilicothe; California Manroot

File.Name: a315b_a314-016.jpg
Notes:
60132979 I found this small Robber Fly (Asilidae in the order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family growing in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2010)

I'm especially interested in flies right now because I'm working in my Dunn Middle School science fair project on "Families of Flies on San Marcos Pass" - or something like that. It's due in two weeks.

My project is really more display than "real science" - but I'm the semi-retired science teacher and I can do what I like! Basically I'm going through my 7,000 fotolog photos from the last (almost) 7 years, and pulling together all the photos of flies sorted to family in a simple database - about 450 photos so far. I think it will be an impressive slide show!
CommonNames: Robber Fly; Wild Cucumber; Chilicothe; California Manroot

File.Name: a315c_a314-018.jpg
Notes:
60131372 There aren't many butterflies in the woods yet, but soon there will be more! Here is a caterpillar snuggled into new leaves of an endemic native shrub of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family growing in the mountains near home. I don't know what sort of butterfly it will become. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2010)

This plant is named for the great naturalist Blanche Trask who studied the botany of the channel islands 100 years ago. Interesting lady! There's a collection of her writing on the web at:
   http://naturespeace.org/blanchetraskanthology.htm

File.Name: a316a_a316-062.jpg
Notes:
60160255 One of the hazards of photographing local wildflowers and insects is the venomous Southern Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus viridis helleri) - "la serpiente de cascabel". They look and sound nasty, and I always jump when I hear one rattling - it's such an electrifying sound. But I just give them plenty of room - except for the occasional close photo. Who would guess that they have such purple tongues! (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 March 2010)

This is an impressive snake. I count 14 rings on it's rattle, so it's been around a few years. That doesn't tell how old they are (they're not trees), but how many times they've shed their skin at a rate of several times a year.

I found this snake today on a trail near Lake Cachuma on my way to school. One group was hiking back down the trail and asked if there's another way out - there isn't - and another group on the other side turned back when they heard the snake rattle. Me? I went in close for the photo!

Rattlesnakes are no big deal - just don't step on them. They don't like that!

File.Name: a316b_a316-054.jpg
Notes: 60159143 This is a beautiful Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) resting on a grass leaf in the woods. I think it's a Pacific Forktail Damselfly (Ischnura cervula) - like a flying needle! It's munching on prey, but it's hard to make out in this photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 March 2010)

File.Name: a316c_a316-049.jpg
Notes: 60158720 Here are a mating pair of Bordered Plant Bugs (family Largidae in the insect order Hemiptera). They're going at it on a leaf of native Wild Cucumber (Marah fabaceus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family. I got all of today's photos in the woods near Lake Cachuma on my way to school. This has become a favorite photo spot. (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 March 2010)

File.Name: a317a_a317-070.jpg
Notes:
60186373 Strange pollen-dusted March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) - captured near the garden in the month of March by luck! I guess it's in the genus Bibio. It's perched on a dead branch of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus). I wonder where its been to get so dusted with pollen? (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2010)

Don't miss my new rattlesnake photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/60185338

File.Name: a317b_a317-071.jpg
Notes:
60185793 There are lots of flies that look by bees, but here's a bee that looks like a fly!

But it's definitely a wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) trying to warm up this morning on a leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It has four wings, not two as the di-ptera have. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2010)
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae; di-ptera
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae; di-ptera

File.Name: a317c_a316-061.jpg
Notes:
60185338 A snake in the grass!

Here's another photo of the venomous Southern Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus viridis helleri) - "la serpiente de cascabel" - that I featured yesterday. This photo shows its "rattler" tail. Now if I could just reach out and remove that dry grass stem blocking it's eye... but no, they don't like that!

I count 14 rings on the rattle, so it's been around a few years. That doesn't tell how old they are (they're not trees), but how many times they've shed their skin at a rate of several times a year. (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 March 2010)

I took these photos with my 105 mm macro lens on a tripod - the same lens I use for almost all my photos. And I cropped a bit - I'm not foolish! I took yesterday's photo at 4288 by 2848 pixels (what my Nikon D300 can do) and I cropped it down to about 1800 by 1800 pixels before reducing it down to fotolog size.

I was beyond striking distance - and even if the snake decided to get nasty, my camera and tripod were between us. No concerns.

Those people who were afraid to walk the trail - now that's just silly! This is one of God's creatures. He just doesn't want people messing with him, so he gives them fair warning to stand back. I can relate to that!

File.Name: a318a_a315-034.jpg
Notes:
60223354 Beautiful new flowers of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum), aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish, in the Liliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2010)

Our power was out all day and most of the night yesterday, so I haven't even loaded my new photos onto my computer yet. It's a good day to catch up on some flower photos I've been meaning to post.

File.Name: a318b_a312-097.jpg
Notes: 60223130 Beautiful yellow flower of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family growing in a place that was burned by the Gap Wildfire two years ago. It's a fantastic head-high (or more) native shrub that has flowers most of the year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2010)

File.Name: a318c_a312-093.jpg
Notes: 60222995 The Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) flowers in the Scrophulariaceae plant family are still going strong on the burned ground of the Gap Wildfire two years ago. This plant is a partial root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2010)

File.Name: a319a_a318-120.jpg
Notes: 60240740 Here are my after-school hike club kids on the upper lip of "Lizard's Mouth" in the mountains yesterday. It's a popular site for rock climbing and hanging out away from authority in the mountains near home. The rocks are fantastic! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2010)

File.Name: a319b_a318-083.jpg
Notes:
60240579 I did my study of insect galls for last year's science fair project - this year my subject is local flies - but I keep finding new galls in the woods!

This is an interesting gall on flowers of Common Fiddleneck (Amsinkia menziesii var. intermedia - aka A. intermedia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. You can see the normal flowers in the background. The plant is a native, but kind of weedy. If fact it takes over whole fields, and it is spreading around the world. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 March 2010)

I believe this photo shows the gall of the Fiddleneck Gall Nematode (Anguina amsinckia) that induces the plant to form floral galls that replace the flowers. Notice that there are no flowers on the galled plant in the foreground. I read on the Web that about 40,000 nematodes occur in an average 3/8-inch diameter gall!

The mind boggles... This might hopefully is a way to naturally control the weed. There's more info at:
   ucce.ucdavis.edu/files/repositoryfiles/ca3907p4-62885.pdf

(You have to cut-'n-paste the link.)

File.Name: a319c_a318-081.jpg
Notes:
60239900 Here is a little "true bug" (order Hemiptera) that I found on a native flower of Wild Cucumber (Marah fabaceus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family. It has four-segmented antennae, so I guess it is a Plant Bug in the family Miridae. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 March 2010)

I'm sure it's looking right at me!

This is probably the same kind of bug that became breakfast for the spider in my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/60133549
CommonNames: true bug; Wild Cucumber; Plant Bug

File.Name: a320a_a315-042.jpg
Notes:
60261973 Spring has sprung!

Today is the vernal equinox - it's officially spring in the northern hemisphere, and autumn south of the equator.

My photo for the event shows two tendrils of Wild Cucumber (Marah fabaceus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family - with flowers in the background - reaching out for a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. I wonder which tendril came first? (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 March 2010)

(My visual pun probably won't survive Google Translate!)
ScientificName: Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: a320b_a320-016.jpg
Notes:
60261068 Here is a tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae in the order Diptera) lapping pollen from a tiny flower of native California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2010)

This is a tight crop of a photo taken in deep shade today. That's probably me reflected on the thorax.

File.Name: a320c_a320-001.jpg
Notes:
60260696 *Sigh* These might be our last mushrooms of the season.

I believe they are the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox aka Pholiota praecox - or kin) in the Bolbitaceae mushroom family. They are growing on a pile of wood chips left over from our tree work last autumn. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2010)

We have a Mediterranean winter-wet, summer-dry weather pattern here in the Santa Barbara mountains. Today is the vernal equinox - the official first day of spring - and I can feel the dry summer season coming. It's bad for mushrooms, but good for insects. There's always something to photograph in our woods!

File.Name: a321a_a321-024.jpg
Notes:
60290786 I've been wondering why the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae) plant family in front of our house looks kind of droopy... I've been wondering why the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae) plant family in front of our house looks kind of droopy...

No wonder. Almost every flower cluster on this poor shrub - there are hundreds - has been infested by Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) carefully placed by native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera)! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2010)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Greenbark Ceanothus; Aphids; Ants

File.Name: a321b_a320-015.jpg
Notes:
60289454 Here is an uncommon native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae plant family. The buds are about ready to open. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2010)

This plant is common in Oregon - aka Oregon Grape - but it's uncommon in our mountains. I only find it in a few shady places where the sun never shines, and I've never seen a berry. I guess it's a remnant plant from wetter times in the past that's trying to hold on against climate change.

File.Name: a321c_a320-008.jpg
Notes:
60289177 Some little critter has been boring holes through the side of these Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2010)

It's kind of neat - you can see inside the flowers, like one of those candy "Easter eggs" with a scene hidden inside!

File.Name: a322a_a322-026.jpg
Notes:
60320586 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has caught his breakfast fly - maybe family Calliphoridae or Muscidae? The spider is perched on a leaf of Wild Cucumber (Marah fabaceus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 March 2010)

I've been working tonight on my DMS science fair project about "Interesting Flies on San Marcos Pass". I'm making progress - all photos are now printed! Next up is some writing and putting it all together in a poster.
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Calliphoridae; Muscidae; Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Calliphoridae; Muscidae; Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae

File.Name: a322b_a322-037.jpg
Notes: 60320283 Here is an interesting moth (Lepidoptera) that I found in the woods today. It's diurnal - a daytime moth - at least it was active at about 11:15 AM today. It's an "underwing"-type moth, with bright orange colors on it's hind wings that you can't see in my photo with wings closed. It's perched on a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family that is encrusted with interesting lichens. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 March 2010)

File.Name: a322c_a322-033.jpg
Notes:
60320091 This isn't the photo I was hoping to get. The darn Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) is one flower over, so you can't see it's long probiscis in the flower. The flowers are Common Fiddleneck (Amsinkia menziesii var. intermedia). These little flies are very camera shy! (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 March 2010)

I'll try again tomorrow.

File.Name: a323a_a323-046.jpg
Notes:
60347780 We had a swarm of Honey Bees (Apis mellifera in the order Hymenoptera) at school today! Here they are resting in the non-native Pine by Donna's classroom. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 March 2010)

I'll be cutting back on photos for a few days. Not that I I don't have enough new photos, but it's science fair crunch time! My project on "Interesting Flies on San Marcos Pass" is due on Friday. I hate deadlines, but they get me working...

File.Name: a324a_a323-041.jpg
Notes:
This sure looks like a Western Box Elder Bug (family Rhopalidae in the Hemiptera), but if so it's lost! There were lots of these "true bugs" on the new Blue Oak leaves (Quercus douglasii in the Fagaceae plant family) growing near Lake Cachuma yesterday. This one is looking at me! (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 March 2010)

I'm cutting back on photos for a few days. Not that I I don't have enough new photos, but it's science fair crunch time! My project on "Interesting Flies on San Marcos Pass" is due on Friday. I hate deadlines, but they get me working...
CommonNames: Western Box Elder Bug; Blue Oak

File.Name: a325a_a323-042.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful new flowers of Fremont's Death Camas aka Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's quite poisonous. I found this pretty flower in the woods near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 March 2010)

Up in the mountains we have its cousin Z. fremontii var. inezianus which is much larger with more robust flowers that have greenish glands on the petals. It's probably flowering now, but I haven't had time to find them.

The DMS Science Fair is tomorrow! I'm just about done with my project on "Interesting Flies on San Marcos Pass". I'm the semi-retired science teacher at DMS, but I've always done my own project, so I'll continue the tradition!
CommonNames: Fremont's Death Camas; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas

associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; fremontii; Liliaceae; Z. fremontii; inezianus

File.Name: a326a_a326-049.jpg
Notes:
60450212 Here is my Science Fair poster hanging in the usual place at Dunn Middle School. I did my project this year on "Interesting Flies on San Marcos Pass, Santa Barbara County, California". Along with the poster, I had a slide show with 520 photos of interesting flies I've taken over the years - most are my fotolog shots. It was a pretty fabulous show. I'll try to figure out how to post it on YouTube. Work done! (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2010)

I admit my project is more display than experiment - I have a great poster and a slide show with hundreds of my photos. I don't really have a question apart from the obvious "How many kinds of flies do we have" - but cataloging is the basis of all natural history, and natural history is surely the basis of all biology.

File.Name: a327a_a326-087.jpg
Notes:
60452977 This perfect Turkey Vulture (Cathartes aura) bird was grooming itself on the power pole close to our house. I got this photo from our upstairs porch. It didn't quite touch the two live wires with wings extended! You can see the crossbar that keeps them away from the two closer wires.

Sure - they feed on carrion, but I think of them as philosopher birds who ponder life and death, no hurry. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2010)

I'm off to Joshua Tree National Park in the California desert tomorrow morning for a week of backpacking with the DMS 8th graders! I hope to return with some photos of wildflowers - but the forecast is for snow on Wednesday night. Yipes!

New photos in a week.

File.Name: a327b_a326-082.jpg
Notes:
60452494 Lupines and poppies - what a glorious combination!

Here are native Sky Lupines (Lupinus nanus) and California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) growing together near the top of Figueroa Mountain. (Figueroa Mountain, 26 March 2010)

I had some time to kill before science fair started last night, so I took a drive up Figueroa Mountain...

File.Name: a327c_a326-100.jpg
Notes:
60451977 Lupines and poppies - what a glorious combination!

Here are Sky Lupines (Lupinus nanus) and California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) growing together near the top of Figueroa Mountain. It was too windy for any closer photos. (Figueroa Mountain, 26 March 2010)

I had some time to kill before science fair started last night, so I took a drive up Figueroa Mountain...

File.Name: a327d_a326-096.jpg
Notes:
60451751 Looking up at Grass Mountain on the western flank of Figueroa Mountain past a field of native Goldfields (Lasthenia californica). You can see a patch of Bigelow's Coreopsis (Coreopsis bigelovii) on the lower slopes of the mountain. (Figueroa Mountain, 26 March 2010)

I had some time to kill before science fair started last night, so I took a drive up Figueroa Mountain...

File.Name: a327e_a327-010.jpg
Notes:
60451356 How green is my valley!

The Santa Ynez Valley looks so green this year after decent rains this year! This is looking west across the south-facing slopes of Figueroa Mountain. The trees are Gray Pines (Pinus sabiniana) up close, Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) in the middle, and Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) in the distance. (Figueroa Mountain, 26 March 2010)

I had some time to kill before science fair started last night, so I took a drive up Figueroa Mountain...
CommonNames: Gray Pines; Blue Oak; Valley Oak
ScientificName: Pinus sabiniana; Quercus douglasii; Quercus lobata
associatedTaxa: Pinus sabiniana; Quercus douglasii; Quercus lobata

File.Name: a403a_a331-559.jpg
Notes: 60663643 Beautiful flowers of Desert Bluebells (Phacelia campanularia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. I only found a few of these beautiful inch-long native wildflowers hiding under the rocks in the desert. (Joshua Tree National Park, 31 March 2010)

File.Name: a403b_a328-018.jpg
Notes: 60663125 Brilliant Desert Paintbrush (Castilleja angustifolia) flowers in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It was growing at the base of a Desert Almond (Prunus fasciculata) shrub in the Rosaceae plant family. The desert shrubs are nursery plants for the wildflowers which find some shelter under them. The showy red "petals" of this flower are really leafy bracts. The real flowers are the greenish tubes that stick out from the red-tipped bracts and sepals. (Joshua Tree National Park, 28 March 2010)
ScientificName: Castilleja angustifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Prunus fasciculata; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Castilleja angustifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Prunus fasciculata; Rosaceae

File.Name: a403c_a328-035.jpg
Notes:
60662451 Odd little flowers of Rattlesnake Weed (Chamaesyce albomarginata) in the plant family Euphorbiaceae growing in a sandy wash in Joshua Tree National Park. The whole plant is only about 1/2 inch high, According to Wikipedia, it gets its name from its former use as a folk remedy for snakebites (as a poultice or brewed as a tea) - it probably doesn't work. (Joshua Tree National Park, 28 March 2010)

I got back from our school backpack trip to Joshua Tree on Friday. I spent Saturday sorting through the 1,429 photos I took - mostly school stuff - and narrowed it down to 730 keepers. Whew!

File.Name: a404a_a331-450.jpg
Notes:
60665418 Here is an astounding Desert Tortoise (Gopherus agassizii that we found while hiking in the desert at Joshua Tree last week! It's at least a foot long - 25 cm. Think about turtles and you probably think water, but this turtle lives on the water it gets from eating desert plants - not much. (Joshua Tree National Park, 31 March 2010)

I wanted to move it a bit to show it's face better, but I couldn't disturb it. One defense mechanism the tortoise has when it is handled is to empty its bladder. This leaves the tortoise in a very vulnerable condition. They should never be alarmed, handled, or picked up in the wild.

I got back from our school backpack trip across Joshua Tree National Park on Friday. I spent Saturday sorting through the 1,429 photos I took - mostly school stuff - and narrowed it down to 730 keepers. Whew!

File.Name: a404b_a330-361.jpg
Notes: 60664471 We found this perfect sun-bleached Coyote skull in the desert wash while hiking down from Ryan Mountain. (Joshua Tree National Park, 30 March 2010)

File.Name: a404c_a401-583.jpg
Notes:
60664316 Julie jokes that I can find mushrooms anywhere - maybe so!

Here is a "Desert Shaggy Mane" (Podaxis pistillaris) mushroom fruiting in the Joshua Tree desert last week. It looks like a gilled mushroom that has failed to open properly. The cap will remain closed to protect the developing spores from moisture loss. It's said to be edible when very young. (Joshua Tree National Park, 1 April 2010)

File.Name: a405a_a331-530.jpg
Notes: 60694322 DMS math teacher Noah during our solo activity at Joshua Tree last week - look in the lower left of my photo. Here's Noah taking in the scenery doing art - a great rock pile in the desert. (Joshua Tree National Park, 31 March 2010)

File.Name: a405b_a331-567.jpg
Notes: 60693958 A line of Mojave Yucca (Yucca schidigera) and Joshua Trees (Yucca brevifolia) at sunset. This was the night we were supposed to get snow on our backpack trip last week. Fortunately it didn't happen! (Joshua Tree National Park, 31 March 2010)

File.Name: a405c_a331-492.jpg
Notes: 60693379 Here is a Stink Beetle aka Stinkbug (genus Eleodes in the family Tenebrionidae of the Coleoptera) in the desert with his butt up and head down on some Joshua Tree granite. It's a pinacate beetle. When threatened or attacked, pinacate beetles can spew a brew of noxious chemicals from the posterior of their abdomen. It's the beetle equivalent of a skunk. From Wikipedia: "The name pinacate is Mexican Spanish, derived from the Nahuatl (Aztec) name for the insect, pinacatl, which translates as 'black beetle'." (Joshua Tree National Park, 31 March 2010)

File.Name: a405d_a331-385.jpg
Notes:
60691984 I guess this is a new Specklepod Milkvetch (Astragalus lentiginosus) in the Fabaceae plant family, with flowers. It doesn't yet have the distinctive speckled seed pods. It's growing in the desert at the base of some other shrub - like most of the early wildflowers I found. (Joshua Tree National Park, 31 March 2010)

I took these wildflower photos in Joshua Tree with my Nikon 18-200 mm zoom lens using only my monopod for support. Not bad - but not quite the quality I expect with my macro 105 mm lens and tripod.

File.Name: a405e_a331-480.jpg
Notes: 60691132 Tiny flowers of Wallace's Woolydaisy (Antheropeas wallacei) in the Asteraceae plant family growing on the coarse desert sand at Joshua Tree last week. The diminutive plants are only about 1/2 inch high and an inch across. (Joshua Tree National Park, 31 March 2010)

File.Name: a405f_a401-608.jpg
Notes: 60690667 Pretty flowers of native Brown-eyed Evening-primrose (Camissonia claviformis) in the Onagraceae plant family. We were a week or two too early for wildflowers on our backpack hike across Joshua Tree last week - it's still very cold in the desert! - though I found a few early bloomers. (Joshua Tree National Park, 1 April 2010)

File.Name: a406a_a406-047.jpg
Notes:
60719971 I guess this is a real Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) because of the patterned wings, the long distinct ovipositor, and the wonderful eyes. It's ovipositing on a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2010)

This photo may also solve a question I've had - what causes the galled flowers on Canyon Sunflower? See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/48320206

Tephritid flies lay their eggs inside plant tissue, and they are known to cause galls in other plants.

It's good to be home from the desert, but it's a short stay - Julie and I are flying off again tomorrow for spring vacation to Portland, Oregon to check in with the grandkids. More photos next week!
ScientificName: Tephritidae; Diptera) because of the patterned wings, the long distinct ovipositor, and the wonderful eyes. It's ovipositing on a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Tephritidae; Diptera) because of the patterned wings, the long distinct ovipositor, and the wonderful eyes. It's ovipositing on a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: a406b_a405-023.jpg
Notes: 60718819 Pretty Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) resting on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2010)

File.Name: a406c_a405-027.jpg
Notes:
60718309 Here's looking at the head of a Giant Crane Fly (family Tipulidae of the order Diptera). Despite the interesting mouthparts, I believe these adult flies don't eat at all. They mate and are done, though their larvae live on as "leatherjackets" in the soil or creeks. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2010)

It's good to be home after a week in the desert!

File.Name: a407a_a405-030.jpg
Notes:
60733574 I found this Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly (order Lepidoptera) perched on a branch in the woods. These butterflies are spotted gray underneath, but they really are blue on top. You can see hints of the blue color on the sliver of its upper wing that is showing. This butterfly is tilted about 30 degrees like a solar collector trying to get some heat from the sun on a cold day. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2010)

Julie and I are flying off later this afternoon for spring vacation to Portland, Oregon to check in with the grandkids. More photos next week!

File.Name: a407b_a405-036.jpg
Notes: 60733246 I found this perfect little bird skull in the woods. It's less than an inch long. I posed it on the trunk of a tree for this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2010)

File.Name: a407c_a405-016.jpg
Notes: 60733150 Just a little fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). Maybe it's a "root-maggot fly" in the Anthomyiidae family? (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2010)

File.Name: a413a_a413-019.jpg
Notes:
60907046 There are still mushrooms in the woods! I believe these are the Giant Gymnopilus (Gymnopilus spectabilis or kin) mushrooms growing on the wood chips left over from tree work last autumn. There are lots of them. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2010)

These mushrooms taste bitter. The mushrooms in the eastern US may contain psilocybin or psilocin, but apparently the west coast forms are "inactive".

We went to Portland, Oregon last week, and a funny thing happened on the way to the airport. I forgot my camera case in the car. By the time I noticed, it was too late to back through the security checks.

No problem - actually I was glad I didn't have my camera to get in the way. My grandkids are walking all over the place and being delightful!

You can see some fairly recent photos at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/roz_n_ewan/

File.Name: a413b_a413-007.jpg
Notes:
60906512 Here is a tiny Rove Beetle in the family Staphylinidae of the order Coleoptera hiding among the flowers of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2010)

It's easy to recognize these little beetles by their short wing covers that leave their abdomen exposed.

File.Name: a413c_a406-070.jpg
Notes:
60906168 Strange new flowers of native Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) in the Papaveraceae family- terrible common name! The foliage looks like poppy leaves, but the flowers are very different. I see faces... (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2010)

This is a fire-follower plant growing in a place in the mountains that burned two years ago in the Gap Fire.

File.Name: a414a_a414-026.jpg
Notes:
60934466 These amazing Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) don't quite land on flowers to feed - they hover like little helicopters with their wings in constant motion. Maybe this is genus Bombylius? It's working a new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Who knew that these flies have forked tongues?? (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2010)

My science fair project on "Interesting Flies on San Marcos Pass" is now history, but I keep finding interesting flies to add to the catalog!
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: a414b_a414-020.jpg
Notes:
60934180 This pretty little March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera - maybe genus Bibio) is holding onto a flower bud of a composite Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. These flies are slow fliers, so I could get close this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2010)

That's a spiny Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the upper left.
CommonNames: March Fly; Canyon Sunflower; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Bibio; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Rubus ursinus
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Bibio; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Rubus ursinus

File.Name: a414c_a414-023.jpg
Notes:
60933961 I'm pretty sure this pretty fly is a Snipe Fly (order Diptera, family Rhagionidae - probably genus Rhagio). It's resting on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2010)

Some of our snipe flies (genus Symphoromyia) are bad biters and lap blood, but I this isn't one of them.
Locality: genus Symphoromyia) are bad biters and lap blood
ScientificName: Diptera; Rhagionidae; Rhagio; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Symphoromyia
