File.Name: a415a_a415-065.jpg
Notes: 60957398 Here is a Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) in the woods near Lake Cachuma this morning. It may well be a little Katydid (family Tettigoniidae). It's still a nymph, but it's emerging from its previous husk - kind of like graduating from middle school with high school still to come. (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 April 2010)

File.Name: a415b_a415-062.jpg
Notes:
60956773 I spotted this tiny Tick (family Ixodidae) in the woods today perched on a blade of grass. It's just waiting (and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me! Ticks can spend weeks or months just waiting... like an extended moment that passes just like that! (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 April 2010)

I'm not up on my "Acarology" - the study of mites and ticks - so I'm not sure what kind of tick this is. Local ticks can carry Lyme Disease, but I'm not worried. If you get ticked, just pull the damn thing out! Use tweezers if you have to. The first sign of a tick bite is a persistent irritation. The bites usually swell up and hurt for a few days after you remove the tick. No big deal.

File.Name: a415c_a415-076.jpg
Notes:
60955968 Here is a pretty flower cluster of Fremont's Death Camas aka Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family. These plants are close to the variety inezianus that grows in the mountains with flowers that have greenish glands on the petals. It's quite poisonous. I found this pretty flower in the woods near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 April 2010)

Look very close to see the tiny ants that are getting nectar from the glands!
CommonNames: Fremont's Death Camas; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas

File.Name: a416a_a414-022.jpg
Notes:
60980134 This is just a fly (order Diptera) - but even flies have to groom themselves! This little fly is cleaning its eyes with special hairs on its front legs. I'm not sure, but I think it might be a "root-maggot fly" in the Anthomyiidae family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2010)

My science fair project on "Interesting Flies on San Marcos Pass" is now history, but I keep finding new interesting flies to add to the catalog!

File.Name: a416b_a406-042.jpg
Notes: 60979737 This is a tiny Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) perched on an immature flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden. These little flies have wonderful eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2010)
ScientificName: Tephritidae; Diptera) perched on an immature flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Tephritidae; Diptera) perched on an immature flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: a416c_a413-006.jpg
Notes: 60979505 Here is a tiny fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae) plant family. I guess it is a little Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2010)
CommonNames: fly; Greenbark Ceanothus; Pomace Fly; Small Fruit Fly

File.Name: a417a_a417-008.jpg
Notes:
61003108 This tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) has brought down a breakfast fly this morning. The unfortunate fly is a March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera - maybe genus Bibio). Oh baby, it's a wild world! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2010)

My photos today happen to be the first three photos I got this morning - at least they are the first three critters I photographed. It was a good start to the day!

File.Name: a417b_a417-006.jpg
Notes:
61002746 Perfect white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) - but why is it perched on this green leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family instead of hiding in a white blackberry flower? (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2010)

The spider is looking at me!

File.Name: a417c_a417-004.jpg
Notes:
61002562 I'm trying to do a "Gary photo"...

Here is an immature Katydid (family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera) - and its world. It's resting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2010)

File.Name: a418a_a418-054.jpg
Notes:
61032717 Pretty flowers of Yellow-throated Phacelia (Phacelia brachyloba) in the (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. This plant is a "fire follower" growing in a place that burned in the Gap Wildfire two years ago. Look in the lower left of my photo - there's a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2010)

It was breezy today as a new storm approaches - too windy for photos, though I got a few.

File.Name: a418b_a417-032.jpg
Notes:
61032191 I believe these are Giant Gymnopilus (Gymnopilus spectabilis or kin - maybe Gymnopilus ventricosus?) mushrooms growing on the wood chips left over from tree work last autumn. There are lots of them, though they really aren't that big - nothing like the 40 cm caps mentioned in my book. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2010)

In Japan, they are called the "Big Laughing Mushroom" - 'shrooms in japan and eastern North America contain psilocybin or psilocin, but apparently the west coast forms are "inactive". Que lastima!

File.Name: a418c_a418-064.jpg
Notes: 61031702 Here is a clutch of Pacific Tree Frogs (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) eggs just starting to hatch into tadpoles. This is a "pond" made from our old fiberglass bathtub in the garden. The adult frogs are still croaking tonight and laying eggs! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2010)

File.Name: a419a_a418-042.jpg
Notes:
61059582 This little Bumblebee (family Bombus in the order Hymenoptera) is working a flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2010)

Julie and I went to visit my Dad in L.A. today. Good visit, but tiring.

File.Name: a419b_a417-039.jpg
Notes: 61059425 I guess this is a Twice-stabbed Lady Beetle - interesting common name! - (genus Chilocorus of the family Coccinellidae of the order Coleoptera) - but I'm really not sure. It seems tiny for a ladybug, and there are other insects (e.g. Hyperaspis) that have the "black with two red spots" pattern. It's perched in a flower cluster of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2010)
ScientificName: Chilocorus; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Hyperaspis; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Chilocorus; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Hyperaspis; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: a419c_a415-061.jpg
Notes:
61059175 Look close! Here's a Giant Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera hanging in a bush of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. I guess it's a lady because of her swollen abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2010)

These flies seem so awkward when they wander into our homes, but they look at home here in the brush.

File.Name: a420a_a417-014.jpg
Notes:
61083633 Here are remarkable native flowers of Larkspur - I guess Delphinium patens, but cp. D. parryi - in the Ranunculaceae plant family. These are growing at the bottom of our driveway. They really are this brilliant. It's a fine way to start the day! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2010)

It's interesting that I rarely spot insects on these more elaborate native wildflowers. I wonder why?

Today's flowers are all about "the blues". Tomorrow I'll show a few of "the reds"!

File.Name: a420a_a417-017.jpg
Notes: 61083373 Here is the perfect flower of a native Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) wildflower in the Iridaceae plant family growing in the woods near home. Shouldn't it be named a yellow-eyed grass? This is the only native iris that grows near home. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2010)

File.Name: a420b_a418-046.jpg
Notes: 61083128 Here are the beautiful flowers of a native perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in the canyon. In this lupine, the flowers come in whorls. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2010)

File.Name: a421a_a417-028.jpg
Notes:
61108725 These are the brilliant red flowers of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing on the hillside across from our mailbox. The red "petals" are mostly leafy bracts, and the real flowers are the slender red tubes with protruding pistils. They are in the same genus as the Owl's Clover (Castilleja densiflora) two photos back, and have a similar strategy to get attention. It works! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2010)

We've had two days of unusual April rain and cold wind - we've gotten nearly an inch of rain so far from this storm! And this comes after 3 inches of rain last week while we were still in Portland, Oregon. Conditions are crummy for photos right now, but they're looking good for summer flowers.

Today's flowers are all more or less red. There's a bit of purple in the mix.

File.Name: a421b_a418-052.jpg
Notes: 61108318 Beautiful new flowers of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The pistil and stamens are perfectly placed to move pollen from flower to flower on the foreheads of hummingbirds. You can suck on the flowers for a taste of sweet nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2010)

File.Name: a421c_a415-069.jpg
Notes: 61108224 Here are the strange native flowers of Owl's Clover (Castilleja densiflora ssp. densiflora) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's now in the same genus as Indian Paintbrush (C. affinis) and has similar bright leafy bracts that make up most of the flower head. I've read that it gets it's name because of the Burrowing Owls (Athene cunicularia) that sometimes nest in the same grassy fields where it grows. I don't know about that. (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 April 2010)
CommonNames: Owl's Clover; Indian Paintbrush; Burrowing Owls
ScientificName: Castilleja densiflora; densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; C. affinis; Athene cunicularia
associatedTaxa: Castilleja densiflora; densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; C. affinis; Athene cunicularia

File.Name: a422a_a406-054.jpg
Notes:
61134442 These might be flowers of Hairy Sun Cups (Camissonia hirtella) in the Onagraceae) plant family - but I'm not sure. We have several species of Camissonia in our mountains, and I don't have time to look it up in my books tonight. New flowers keep appearing as the stem unfolds. The long flower stalks are really inferior ovaries that become seed pods. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2010)

I'm getting ready for the 10th anniversary of the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society on Saturday at the Sedgwick Reserve. I'm the president of the society, and I'm doing a macro photography workshop at the event. It will be a big gathering, and I'm busy!

Today's flowers are all yellow - the hardest color to capture!

File.Name: a422b_a406-050.jpg
Notes: 61134053 Elegant new flowers of native Chaparral Lotus (Lotus grandiflorus) in the Fabaceae) plant family. These plants are normally uncommon in the mountains, but they are doing well as fire followers in a place that burned two years ago in the Gap Fire. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2010)

File.Name: a422c_a406-066.jpg
Notes: 61133918 I believe these are the flower heads of Pacific Sanicle (Sanicula crassicaulis or kin) in the Apiaceae plant family. We have several native Sanicles in our mountains, and I always get them confused. They all have similar diffuse heads of tiny yellow flowers in characteristic round umbels. These are growing as fire followers in a place that burned two years ago in the Gap Fire. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2010)

File.Name: a423a_a422-124.jpg
Notes:
61160000 We found a few of these Springtime Amanita (Amanita velosa) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family at the Sedgwick Reserve yesterday. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 April 2010)

I don't find these mushrooms often, but we've had good late-season rain this year. The mushrooms are said to be edible and delicious - but heed all the usual cautions about eating amanitas!

File.Name: a423b_a422-115.jpg
Notes:
61159830 My after-school Hike Club kids running down a hill at the UCSB Sedgwick Reserve. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 April 2010)

For more info, see my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: a423c_a422-107.jpg
Notes:
61159773 I took my after school Hike Club kids to the UCSB Sedgwick Reserve yesterday. You can see Figueroa Mountain in the background. My ulterior motive was to scout out the hike I'm leading for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society tomorrow. I'll be doing a macro-photography workshop. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 April 2010)

I only had three kids - it was raining at lunchtime, and there's a school festival on Friday! But the rain held off, and we had a good time!

For more info about the Sedgwick Reserve, check out:
   http://sedgwick.ucnrs.org/

File.Name: a424a_a424-008.jpg
Notes:
61180718 There used to be simple cartoons called Droodles - started by Roger Price. There are examples at:
   http://www.droodles.com/

This photo is a natural droodle. Can you guess what it is? I'll give the answer in my next photo tomorrow! (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 April 2010)

I led a macro photography workshop for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society's 10th anniversary celebration today at the UCSB Sedgwick Reserve in the Santa Ynez Valley. It wasn't the best day for macro photos. It was foggy and breezy most of the morning, so we had to crank up the ISO and increase aperture. But we managed to get some decent photos - including this mystery photo!

File.Name: a424b_a424-004.jpg
Notes: 61180347 I believe this is a March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) trying to warm up on a foggy morning. If you look close, you can see fog drops on the fly. It's perched on a native wildflower of California Buttercup (Ranunculus californicus) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. This flower has been open for a while, and you can see the greenish developing seeds. (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 April 2010)

File.Name: a424c_a424-016.jpg
Notes:
61180092 This isn't the evil eye of Sauron!

I led a macro photography workshop for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society's 10th anniversary celebration today at the UCSB Sedgwick Reserve, and my friend Rick Mexico let us borrow his large Bearded Dragon (Pogona vitticeps?) lizard as a photo model. It was foggy all morning, and the chilly lizard was a perfect model for close shots, not moving very much. (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 April 2010)

The SYVNHS 10th anniversary bash was a great success and a lot of fun. *Whew*

File.Name: a425a_a425-038.jpg
Notes:
61203243 This is a tiny "Garbage line spider" (genus Cyclosa). It's an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2010)

The spider has just added a new prey on its web. Looking close at my orginal photo, the unfortunate prey seems to be a tiny Cantharidae beetle - with its abdomen hog-tied over it's head. I'm not sure if the spider is crowing for victory or alarmed by my presence, but it's plucking the strands of its web.

File.Name: a425b_a425-042.jpg
Notes:
61202631 This yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) was guarding the throat of this flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae) plant family - but I spooked it. Now it's guarding against me! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2010)

Like many of these spiders that are exposed, it has a vague "scary face" on its abdomen.

I don't give it much of a chance of catching a meal on this flower. I've noticed that the "fancy flowers" like this one don't get many insect visitors. Do they all usually self-pollinate?

File.Name: a425c_a424-023.jpg
Notes:
61202318 This is the "mystery insect" in my [Previous] photo.

Of course it is a dragonfly - actually a Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata). It's perched on blade of grass on a foggy morning. (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 April 2010)

We found many of them perched on grass blades during my macro photography workshop for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society yesterday. When you approach them for a photo, they always turn away to the far side of the grass blade - like my [Previous] photo! I tricked this one by holding my arm out to the left, so it came back around.

It was foggy and breezy - nothing to do except bump up the ISO and increase the aperture.

File.Name: a426a_a426-056.jpg
Notes:
61231953 Here's a lesson on flower pollination...

This is a pretty flower of Fremont's Death Camas aka Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. fremontii - with the flower glands yellowish) in the Liliaceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 April 2010)

The flowers start with the female pistils held up high and male stamens held low to entice an insect like this Convergent Lady Beetle - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - to come by to taste the sweet nectar from its glands and get dusted with pollen.

Then the flowers switch gears to stamens up and pistels down to collect the pollen previously deposited. This photo shows the latter mode, and it seems effective with this ladybug, which is about to transfer yellow pollen from its shell!

The lesson - flowers are often active in their pollination strategies!
CommonNames: Fremont's Death Camas; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas; Convergent Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; fremontii; Liliaceae; Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; fremontii; Liliaceae; Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: a426b_a425-046.jpg
Notes:
61231290 The cold icepick in the brain - like the death of Trotsky in Mexico...

(Though the word on Wikipedia is that "Trotsky was attacked in his home in Mexico with an ice axe (not an ice pick as is often stated)'. Does anyone else remember the 1960's movie Morgan!?)

I first thought these were a mating pair of March Flies (family Bibionidae, order Diptera), but I realize looking at the photo that this is a small Robber Fly (Asilidae) on top that has captured a March fly and is sucking it dry. Nature is sometimes tough. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2010)

File.Name: a426c_a424-007.jpg
Notes:
61230439 An ogre guarding the entrance to its treasure cave?

Nah, it's just a spider sitting in its web on a fog-drippy morning. The web looks like a Funnel Weaver (Agelenidae), but I guess from its face that this is a Funnelweb Mygalomorph (Dipluridae) - in the suborder Orthognatha. (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 April 2010)

File.Name: a427a_a427-080.jpg
Notes:
61255076 Here are a pair of tiny mating Fruit Flies (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden today, not too far from our compost pile. The flies are connected by the female's (on the bottom) ovipositor. This is a sweet moment that would go unseen without my camera. These little flies have speckled wings and wonderful eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2010)

It's raining tonight! We've been having regular late-season rains this year. Here in the Santa Barbara mountains, we have a Mediterranean winter-wet, summer-dry weather pattern. This rain is a blessing as we approach our 6 month summer drought.

File.Name: a427b_a426-062.jpg
Notes: 61254364 I found this handsome inch-long Beetle (order Coleoptera) working a flower of native Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Malvaceae) plant family. Here it is taking a moment off from feeding for personal grooming. The red "eye spots" are dummies - the real eyes are black and just in front. I have no idea what kind of beetle it is. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2010)

File.Name: a427c_a427-075.jpg
Notes:
61253364 This is looking eye to eye to eye to eye (and I and I) with a little pencil-erasor sized Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a dead oak leaf in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2010)

I wonder what it's like to BE such a spider? Does it have one continuous field of vision, or is it more like "now switch to camera 5"? Either way, it works. It's strange to make personal eye contact with any critter this small, but I'm sure the spider looked right at me and was aware of my presence.

File.Name: a428a_a427-073.jpg
Notes:
61279168 I guess this is "The Blusher" - an Amanita rubescens mushroom in the woods. This is a woodland scene with native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) and California Bedstraw (Galium californicum). It's interesting that this mushroom is so scaly on both stem and cap - other A. rubescens mushrooms in the woods now are quite smooth. All of these mushrooms develop reddish stains.

It's also interesting that this is one of the first mushrooms to appear in the fall, and also one of the last to appear in spring. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/56969291

Are these really the same mushrooms??

We had some rain last night! We've been having regular late-season rains this year. Here in the Santa Barbara mountains, we have a Mediterranean winter-wet, summer-dry weather pattern. This rain is a blessing as we approach our 6 month summer drought.

File.Name: a428b_a424-019.jpg
Notes: 61278461 We found a lot of these strange yellow mushrooms growing on wood chips under a Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) during my macro photography workshop at the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society 10th anniversary bash last Saturday. From the yellow colors and gelatinous cap and scaly stalk, I guess it's some kind of Pholiota mushroom - but I'm not sure. Don't eat it! (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 April 2010)

File.Name: a428c_a424-017.jpg

This appears to be an odd Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum or kin) mushroom in the woods - though this one lacks ribs and a stalk. It's growing on wood chips at the Sedgwick Reserve. (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 April 2010)

61304802 Native Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) on a odd flower in the garden. I believe it is "Mediterranean Spurge" (Euphorbia characias ssp. wulfenii) in the Euphorbiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2010)

Look close - this ant is blowing a bubble!

File.Name: a429b_a425-051.jpg
Notes:
61304605 "Been going up so long it looks like down to me!"
(After Richard Farina, of course.)

This is the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that has eaten its way as far up this stalk of native California Figwort (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae) plant family as it can. Now it's time to head back down to find another stalk to devour! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2010)

File.Name: a429c_a425-045.jpg
Notes:
61304251 Here is another "Blusher" (Amanita rubescens) mushroom in the woods. I went to pick it, and the stem broke because of worm tunnels - but it shows the characteristic reddish colors that these mushrooms develop. Of course you can also see the free gills, ring on the stalk, and "death cup" at the base of the stalk that make it an elegant Amanita. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2010)

This amanita is said to be edible when cooked, but it's easily confused with poisonous species. No thanks!

File.Name: a430a_a427-069.jpg
Notes:
61326468 Here is a new flower of Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus). It's a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae family. This may be our most elegant native wildflower. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2010)

It's been very windy for several days - too windy for photos. I hope it blows out by tomorrow when I have some time for new photos.

File.Name: a430b_a426-059.jpg
Notes:
61326113 Beautiful new flowers of Fremont's Death Camas aka Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. fremontii - with the flower glands yellowish) in the Liliaceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 April 2010)

The flowers start (on top) with both stamens and pistils held up. Then the stamens lower like merry-go-round carousel horses to transfer pollen. Then they rise and the pistils lower to receive pollen. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 April 2010)

File.Name: a501a_a501-022.jpg
Notes:
61349529 Here is a beautiful Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly resting (for a moment) on a leaf in the woods. I love the peaches 'n cream colors of these butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2010)

This butterfly is female, with yellow colors. My book says that the "females are white or yellow, with the percentage of yellow females varying from population to population, sometimes over small distances." This is probably the second brood in our mountains. They mostly seem to fly up and down the trails without landing. I chased this butterfly for quite a ways!

May 1 is May Day - it's the workers' day, but it also has deep roots as one of the cross-quarter days midway between vernal equinox and summer solstice. It's the old first day of summer, which explains why "Midsummer Night's Eve" is really the summer solstice - though it's the first day of summer by our calendar. It's interesting how the ancient holidays get recycled again and again.

Today was another windy day. Not quite gale force winds like we've been having, but still too windy for many photos. I hope tomorrow will be calm.

Notes: 61348211 This is an immature Katydid (family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera) on a leaf of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. "Welcome to my world!" (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2010)

File.Name: a502a_a501-008.jpg
Notes:
61374565 Here is a tiny "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) wrapping its newest prey. It's a small Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. It has an abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2010)

I looked up the species on BugGuide.com: "Cyclosa conica can be distinguished from the other species in this genus by the folded paramedian apophysis and one or two ventral macrosetae on coxa 4, and by the posteriorly tapered median septum." What???

*Sigh* it was another windy day. I made the best of it and started sorting my photos for my upcoming school slide show. There's a few busy weeks coming up: a hike for the local Natural History Society on May 15, the Dunn Middle School slideshow on May 27, and a public lecture on insect galls on June 3. My photo-take will suffer in the coming weeks!

File.Name: a502b_a502-027.jpg
Notes:
61373573 I wish this photo was sharper - it was windy, my tripod wasn't ready, my exposure settings were all wrong - all the usual excuses...

It's still pretty cool! Here is a mating pair of large Carpenter Bees (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the order Hymenoptera. They favored the new flowers of Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2010)

The female bees (on top) drill holes in the fascia boards of our house. I know them well. I always figured the white-faced male bees (on bottom) were a different species - but here they are. I guess the males don't figure in nest building.

61402800 I guess this is a Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera - maybe a Red-eared blister beetle, Lytta auriculata? The real black eyes are in front of those red spots on its head. This beetle is dusted with pollen from the flower of native Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Malvaceae) plant family that it's sitting on. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2010)

Blister Beetles include the infamous "Spanish Fly" (Lytta vesicaroria), but I don't believe these California species have those properties.

File.Name: a504a_a503-057.jpg
Notes:
61427626 Ladybug cocoon - this is the pupal stage. I found this critter on a native shrub of Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family.

Look close - you can see the discarded larval skin on the the upper end of the pupa!

Maybe it's the pupa of a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle - Coccinella septempunctata in the family Coccinellidae of the order Coleoptera? I found the adults near by. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2010)

Gardeners - protect these creatures! They will help control the aphids on your plants.

Notes:
61475700 Here is another photo of a Ladybug cocoon - this is the pupal stage. I guess it is the pupa of a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle - Coccinella septempunctata in the family Coccinellidae of the order Coleoptera. You can see the discarded larval skin on the the upper end of the pupa!

I apologize for not posting new photos last night. The power went out around 5:00 pm and didn't return until after noon the next day. Blame SoCalEdison!

File.Name: a506a_a506-098.jpg
Notes: 61476457 Here is a Giant Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera hanging on a leaf on the edge of the garden this morning. These large flies always remind me of wise old philosophers contemplating the infinite. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2010)

File.Name: a506b_a504-073.jpg
Notes:
61476272 This is a white-faced Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae in the order Diptera) grooming itself of a branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2010)

The flowers are done, but there are still aphids on them that were placed by the ants. These interesting flies have been ovipositing on the old flower clusters - laying eggs that will become larvae that will finish off the last of the aphids. I've been trying to get a photo of the flies ovipositing, but they're mostly too high in the shrub.

Look close at that fly grooming itself... its left hind leg is raised high, but its right hind leg is folded up under its abdomen to reach a spot that would be hard to scratch!

File.Name: a507a_a503-033.jpg
Notes:
61499306 Beautiful black-and-white Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae in the order Diptera) working a composite flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2010)

We have lots of these Syrphid Flies in the garden right now. They mostly come with black-and-white or black-and-gold colors, both big and small and tiny. I have no idea how many species they represent.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Canyon Sunflower; Syrphid Flies

File.Name: a507b_a505-082.jpg

61498981 "Luke, follow the honey guides!"

Here is a tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae in the order Diptera) resting on a petal of a remarkable yellow-orange Iris in the (Iridaceae) plant family growing in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2010)

It's not a "bearded iris". I seem to recall we got this flower from my mother, who believed it was a cultivar of native Douglas Iris (Iris douglasiana) - but I'm not sure. Douglas Iris flowers are usually blue.

If you know this flower, please let me know about it!

File.Name: a507c_a505-086.jpg
Notes:
61497814 I'm pretty sure this is a "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the order Hemiptera - but I won't try to guess at a species. It has five-segmented antennae. Many species are green, but not this one. This one is perched under a flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2010)

Heh - the green stink bugs smell like cilantro when you squish them! I left this one alone.

File.Name: a508a_a508-003.jpg
Notes: 61524616 Here is an uncommon Firefly beetle - family Lampyridae in the order Coleoptera - in the woods. Our fireflies don't glow at night, but we do have Glowworms (Phengodidae) that do! I've never gotten a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2010)

File.Name: a508b_a508-016.jpg
Notes: 61524032 Here is a female Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly (order Lepidoptera) perched on a leaf in the canyon. The males have the color. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2010)

File.Name: a508c_a508-021.jpg
Notes: 61523814 Here is a beautiful new flower of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) in the canyon. This is a different kind of wild blackerry in the Rosaceae plant family. They are common and almost weedy in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are scarce here in the Santa Barbara mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2010)

File.Name: a509a_a501-019.jpg
Notes:
There are still mushrooms in the woods! This is a late-season "Blusher" (Amanita rubescens) mushroom pushing up a "mushrump" of dry soil. This amanita is said to be edible when cooked, but it's easily confused with poisonous species. No thanks! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2010)

It's been windy all week, and today it was very windy, too windy for photos. Just as well - I've been working on my school slide show.

File.Name: a509b_a502-026.jpg
Notes: This is our new "worst weed" - Coast Live Oaks (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family sprouting from last year's acorns! The oaks had a good year, and the new sprouts are everywhere! (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2010)

File.Name: a509c_a508-017.jpg
Notes: This is an unusual "fasciated" flower spike of a native perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in the canyon. Fasciation is "caused by a mutation in the meristematic cells, bacterial infection, mite or insect attack, or chemical or mechanical damage." (Wikipedia) Whatever the reason, it causes flat stems and abnormal flowers. An herbicide was used nearby to clear vegetation around a power pole. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2010)

File.Name: a510a_a503-041.jpg
Notes:
61579502 I believe this is a Red-eared Blister Beetle (Lytta auriculata) in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera. The real black eyes are in front of those red spots on its head.

This beetle is perched on a branch of native Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2010)

Blister Beetles include the infamous "Spanish Fly" (Lytta vesicaroria), but I don't believe these California species have those nasty properties.

Gale force winds continue to blow over the pass tonight. I hope this wind event will soon be over - I'm running out of photos! (Though it lets me work on my school slide show, due on May 27!)
Locality: Though it lets me work on my school slide show
CommonNames: Red-eared Blister Beetle; Chaparral Honeysuckle; Blister Beetles; Spanish Fly
ScientificName: Lytta auriculata; Meloidae; Coleoptera; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae; Lytta vesicaroria
associatedTaxa: Lytta auriculata; Meloidae; Coleoptera; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae; Lytta vesicaroria

File.Name: a510b_a508-014.jpg
Notes: 61578105 Here is a female Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly (order Lepidoptera) perched on a leaf of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Labiatae plant family growing in the canyon. The males have most of the color, but this female has some. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2010)
ScientificName: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lepidoptera; Salvia mellifera; Labiatae
associatedTaxa: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lepidoptera; Salvia mellifera; Labiatae

File.Name: a510c_a503-049.jpg
Notes: 61577702 I believe this is the "Winecup Clarkia" Clarkia purpurea ssp. quadrivulnera in the Onagraceaeplant family. It's also called Farewell-to-Spring - a sign that our dry summer is approaching. Can you see the tiny Springtail (order Collembia) insect with dark eye-spots on the upper-most stamen? (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2010)

File.Name: a511a_a502-030.jpg
Notes:
61631624 This is a remarkable yellow-orange Iris in the (Iridaceae) plant family growing in the garden. I think we got this plant from my mother, who said it was a cultivar of Douglas iris (Iris douglasiana), which grows wild at Mount Tranquillon near Lompoc - but the native iris has blue flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2010)

It continues to be too windy for new photos, but maybe it's letting up tonight? I've been digging into the recent archives, and I'm ready for new photos!

File.Name: a511b_a501-024.jpg
Notes: 61630390 Here are the brilliant red flowers of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing on the hillside across from our mailbox. They're having a good year with Spring rains and cool weather. The red "petals" are mostly leafy bracts, and the real flowers are the slender reddish tubes with protruding pistils. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2010)

File.Name: a511c_a501-015.jpg
Notes:
61629327 Here is an interesting insect gall on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe this is the gall of a group of Gouty Stem Gall Wasps (Callirhtis quercussuttoni) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It looks like a potato!

This is the unisexual stage of this gall, but it will give birth to a generation of bisexual wasps that will produce small galls in leaf buds. Those galls will produce only females that will repeat the cycle. Who said biology is easy?! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2010)

I'm doing a public lecture on "Insect Galls of San Marcos Pass" for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society in a few weeks. I have to get ready for it!
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Gouty Stem Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhtis quercussuttoni; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhtis quercussuttoni; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: a512a_a512-038.jpg
Notes:
61687300 This is the beautiful native Fire Poppy (Papaver californicum in the Papaveraceae family). It's the other "California Poppy" that only grows as a fire-follower after recent wildfire burns. I found this one today growing on the site of the "Gap Wildfire" two summers ago. I rarely find it even when I'm looking and it "should" be there! It's a real poppy - it has nodding buds, crinkly petals, and a seed pod shaped like a skinny Papaver somniferum pod. Just beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2010)

The wind finally backed off today, so I hiked down to "The Playground" to scout my hike for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society on Saturday. The wildflowers on the burned ground were crazy! It will be a memorable hike.

More photos tomorrow. I had a board meeting for the natural history society tonight, and I got home late with 180 photos to download and sort through. I'm tired!

File.Name: a513a_a512-101.jpg
Notes:
61732878 Pretty flowers of native Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia) which usually grows near the beach, but here it is on "The Playground" trail down from West Camino Cielo Road on top of the mountains. You can make out the city of Goleta (just north of Santa Barbara) in the background, and the Santa Barbara Channel, and Santa Cruz Island off in the distance. The plants are twining up the burned stems of a chaparral shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2010)

Our usual mountain species is Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) which has a pair of bracts below the calyx of the flower rather than overlapping the calyx like these. But the coastal species is going crazy with flowers right now as a fire-follower on ground burned by the Gap Fire almost two years ago. More photos tomorrow!

I hiked down to "The Playground" yesterday to scout my hike for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society on Saturday. The wildflowers on the burned ground - especially the morning-glories - were going crazy! It will be a memorable hike.
ScientificName: Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata
associatedTaxa: Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia; Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata

File.Name: a513b_a512-065.jpg
Notes: 61731424 These are native Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) flowers in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. This is a classic "fire follower" plant that we rarely see in the mountains except after a wildfire. The seeds can remain in the soil for many years until triggered to sprout by heat and whatever "fire-traces" a wildfire leaves behind in the soil. This plant is growing as a fire-follower on ground burned by the Gap Fire almost two years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2010)

File.Name: a513c_a512-039.jpg
Notes:
61730506 This is the native California Mustard - Thelypodium lasiophyllum. The flowers are tiny, but the plant gets as tall as I am. The mature stems look shaggy, decorated with pendent long seed pods. This plant is growing as a fire-follower on ground burned by the Gap Fire two years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2010)

Look close and you'll see that these small flowers have 4 petals and 6 stamens, arranged 4 long and two short. That's a sure sign that this is a mustard in the Brassicaceae plant family, but it's not one of those showy introduced mustards that have taken over California.

This is the native California Mustard - Thelypodium lasiophyllum. The flowers are tiny, but the plant gets as tall as I am. The mature stems look shaggy, decorated with pendent long seed pods. This plant is growing as a fire-follower on ground burned by the Gap Fire almost two years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2010)

File.Name: a514a_a512-092.jpg
Notes:
61783195 It's like a wave of morning-glories is engulfing the mountain!

These are native Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia) flowers. They usually grow near the beach, but here they are on "The Playground" trail down from West Camino Cielo Road on top of the mountains. This is another wildflower growing as a fire-follower on ground burned by the Gap Fire almost two years ago. I guess the seeds have lain dormant in the soil since the last wildfire came through - maybe the Refugio Fire in 1955? (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2010)

As a naturalist, the other colors in the photo are more interesting - there are blue Phacelia grandiflora in the lower left, and an unknown yellow composite above it!

I hiked down to "The Playground" last week to scout my hike for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society on Saturday, and got this photo. The wildflowers on the burned ground - especially the morning-glories - are going crazy! I think it will be a memorable hike.

File.Name: a514b_a512-042.jpg
Notes: 61782093 Here are new flowers of native Hairy Sun Cups (Camissonia hirtella) in the Onagraceae plant family. New flowers keep appearing as the stem unfolds on the right, and the old flowers on the left turn rusty-colored to be replaced by red seed pods. This is another wildflower growing as a fire-follower on ground burned by the Gap Fire almost two years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2010)

File.Name: a514c_a512-036.jpg
Notes:
61781460 These are the tiny flowers of Common Eucrypta (Eucrypta chrysanthemifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae family. It's growing as a fire-follower on ground burned by the Gap Fire almost two years ago. This native plant has a distinctive strong smell - it stinks! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2010)

*Sigh* - these flowers are hard to photograph. You either get in close enough to show the flower, and lose the plant - or you back off the show the plant, and lose the flower. This photo is a compromise.

File.Name: a515a_a515-001.jpg
Notes:
61831111 I led a hike today for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society to "The Playground". It's a fantastic area of rock outcrops that burned almost two years ago in the "Gap Fire". The fire-follower wildflowers are at their peak right now! (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2010)

Here are most of my group enjoying the wildflowers on our hike today. The weather was perfect, and the flowers were memorable - it was all I could ask for!

File.Name: a515b_a515-014.jpg
Notes:
61830597 I led a hike today for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society to "The Playground". It's a fantastic area of rock outcrops that burned almost two years ago in the "Gap Fire". The fire-follower wildflowers are at their peak right now! (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2010)

Here is Dr. Fred leading the pack through a thicket of interesting fire-follower plants - Chaparral Lotus (Lotus grandiflorus) in the Fabaceae) plant family on the left, and Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia) in the Convulvulaceae plant family on the right.

Our mountains are full of flowers!
Locality: Lotus grandiflorus) in the Fabaceae) plant family on the left

Here are two more of my hike members pushing through the native Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia) flowers that have engulfed the mountain!

Here is my entwife Julie standing by a burned shrub with the new growth of native Chaparral Lotus (Lotus grandiflorus) in the Fabaceae) plant family that will take it's place for a few years.

The fire-follower plants like this lotus are only temporary. The native shrubs will soon return.

File.Name: a515f_a515-028.jpg
Notes:
61828529 I led a hike today for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society to "The Playground". It's a fantastic area of rock outcrops that burned almost two years ago in the "Gap Fire". The fire-follower wildflowers are at their peak right now! (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2010)

File.Name: a516a_a516-074.jpg
Notes:
61879429 Here is an early Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working new composite flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertiflorum in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2010)

Butterflies - and all insects - are late this year. I'm not sure if it's the cool weather and late season rains this year or the drought last year. But they are unusually late.

File.Name: a516b_a516-066.jpg
Notes: 61878141 Pretty little Beetle (order Coleoptera) on a tiny flower of Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides) in the Onagraceae plant family. These flowers are having a good year on the Pass. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2010)

File.Name: a516c_a515-053.jpg
Notes:
61876690 I was sure we were in for a major "ladybug convergence" yesterday afternoon when I got this photo. Hundreds of ladybugs were in the air, and I captured a few on a stem of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. These are Convergent Lady Beetles - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2010)

But when I went back this morning, there were only scattered ladybugs to be found. I guess the party moved on!
ScientificName: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: a517a_a516-072.jpg
Notes:
61924305 Interesting female Snakefly (genus Agulla, family Raphidiidae, order Neuroptera) insect at the throat of a native Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina) flower in the Lamiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2010)

We're having some uncommon cold late-season rain today, and more is expected tonight! We never get rain in May in Santa Barbara with our Mediterranean winter-wet, summer-dry weather pattern, but here it is. It's not good for the butterflies, but it won't hurt the summer flowers...
ScientificName: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae

File.Name: a517b_a516-069.jpg
Notes:
61922120 Here is a native shrub of Ms. Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family with it's first flowers. It also has Spittle Bug slime on it - like someone hocked a big loogie on the plant stem! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2010)

Tiny Spittle Bugs aka Froghoppers (family Aphrophoridae of the order Homoptera) make these frothy masses on the plant stems to protect their larvae.

I love that little sky-blue water drop hanging from the lowest frothy mass!

How many other insects can you see in this photo?
CommonNames: Ms. Trask's Yerba Santa; Spittle Bug; Spittle Bugs; Froghoppers

File.Name: a517c_a515-059.jpg
Notes:
61920721 Here is a pretty Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly working native flowers of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Lamiacae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2010)

Sorry, the photo is a bit under-exposed. I've been distracted preparing the school slide show... I'm about to call it a day.

File.Name: a518a_a515-050.jpg
Notes:
61966877 This tiny wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) is working a native flower of native Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Malvaceae plant family growing along our driveway. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2010)

We had 1/4 inch of unusual May rain last night, and it never cleared up today. Is this what global warming looks like?

I've been working on my school slide show all day. It's looking pretty good!

File.Name: a518b_a516-068.jpg
Notes: 61965984 Here is a little caterpillar (the larvae of a Lepidoptera) on a native flower of Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. I don't know what butterfly it will become! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2010)

File.Name: a518c_a515-062.jpg
Notes: 61965367 Here's a beautiful new Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly resting on an oak branch by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2010)

File.Name: a519a_a519-092.jpg
Notes:
62010922 Here is an elegant new flower of Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus). It's kind of an upside-down Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae plant family. This one has raindrops from our uncommon late May rain shower yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2010)

Almost June, and we still had some real rain! This will be good year for the summer flowers.

File.Name: a519b_a519-090.jpg
Notes:
62010250 Look very close!

This is a Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) on the side of our old bathtub that we turned into a garden pond. It's easy to provide habitat for frogs - any old bathtub and a cheap submersible water pump will do! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2010)

File.Name: a519c_a519-088.jpg
Notes: 62009593 Here are little winged Termites (order Isoptera) that are emerging from a crack in the ground after a bit welcome May rain yesterday. The lizards are standing ready to pick them off - once the photographer gets out of the way! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2010)

File.Name: a520a_a519-085.jpg
Notes:
62053048 Here is another new mushroom in the woods, thanks to the recent rains!

I guess this is a Springtime Amanita (Amanita velosa) mushroom. It doesn't have that "color of a brown hen's egg" that they typically have. My book says that the caps can be much paler, almost white - so I guess it passes. I apologize that that the white balance is off - no time to correct it tonight. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2010)

There's a little Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae sitting on the front of the 'shroom.

It was too windy for new photos today, and I'm too busy and jittery with my school slide show - due in a week! *Yipes*
CommonNames: Springtime Amanita; Pomace Fly; Small Fruit Fly

File.Name: a521a_a512-029.jpg
Notes:
62100455 This is a trio of little native Common Monkey Flowers (Mimulus guttatus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing near the start of the trail to the "Playground" in our mountains.

But maybe it's not so common. These flowers are much smaller than the usual monkey flowers. I believe they are Mimulus guttatus ssp. micranthus. This subspecies isn't recognized in the 1993 Jepson Manual, but it is in the 1959 Munz flora. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2010)

There were gale-force winds blowing over the mountains today - the power flickered a few times. Too windy for photos, and just as well, because I'm busy with my school slide show. I did a quick back-up when the power went off-and-on!

File.Name: a522a_a519-089.jpg
Notes:
62138681 This is a little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) on the side of our old fiberglass bathtub that we turned into a garden pond. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2010)

There were more gale-force winds blowing over the mountains today. It's too windy for photos, and just as well, because I'm busy with my school slide show. Almost done with selecting photos and arranging them - 1800+ photos! On Monday, I will start sequencing the video. Show time is Thursday night.

File.Name: a523a_a515-061.jpg
Notes:
62185172 This is a small Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family. It's only a few inches high. It's very different from the large Bush Lupine in my [Previous] photo, but it has the same characteristic hand-like palmate leaves of all lupines. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2010)

The winds are finally backing off tonight, but it's been windy all day - like the last four days! All that wind moving by, I wonder where does it come from?

The DMS slide show sequencing is done! The next step is to render it all as video. I work with my friend Nick Vincent to do this using his Mac with Final Cut Pro software. Show time is Thursday night!

File.Name: a523b_a515-057.jpg
Notes: 62184014 Here are new flowers of a Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius in the Fabaceae family) growing in the canyon. This is a big lupine - a meter tall - with leaflets in the characteristic hand-like palmate clusters of all lupines. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2010)

File.Name: a524a_a523-008.jpg
Notes:
62230679 Luscious new flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2010)

I got this photo yesterday morning before the winds kicked up too bad. Today was less windy, but now there's a chance of more uncommon late-season rain tomorrow night. What a strange Spring we've been having!

My school slide show work is on track, about 1/3rd rendered to video. My work on this massive project will soon be over!

File.Name: a525a_a522-004.jpg
Notes:
62249143 This looks more like a bit of dirty fluff in the woods than an insect, but look again! You can make out a leg in the lower-center and maybe an eye.

I guess this is a Mealybug (family Pseudococcidae of the order Homoptera). It was by itself. It's perched on a flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2010)

My school slide show is almost done - only one final segment to render as video!

File.Name: a526a_a508-007.jpg
Notes:
62272784 I found this little spider (Arachnida) in the woods. It's web is not organized, so I guess it might be a Cobweb Spider aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae). (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2010)

I got this photo a few days ago before the wind kicked up too bad. The spider is guarding a hooked seed cluster of Pacific Sanicle (Sanicula crassicaulis or kin) in the Apiaceae plant family. I think. We have several native Sanicles in our mountains, and I always get them confused.

The school slide show is done! My video-partner Nick Vincent just has to burn it from his computer to DVD. That's about 1,800 of mostly my photos squeezed into 61:55 minutes. We aim for 60 minutes, and that's close enough. *Whew!* I hope we can get it on to YouTube (in segments) this year. It's a good show that I'd like to share.
Locality: Sanicula crassicaulis or kin) in the Apiaceae plant family. I think. We have several native Sanicles in our mountains
CommonNames: spider; Cobweb Spider; Combfooted Spider; Pacific Sanicle; Sanicles

File.Name: a527a_a527-011.jpg
Notes:
62317020 This little Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) caterpillar is either lost or it's coming down for a drink. It's visiting a leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae) plant family - but these caterpillars feed exclusively on leaves in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. Go figure! (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2010)

We had a bit of unusual late-May rain last night - just a trace, but it was enough to make drops on the lupine leaves. Any rain this late in the season is unusual.

The Dunn Middle School slide show that I've been working on happened tonight, and it was a great success. *Whew* It's a lot of work to make it right. I hope my video partner Nick can get it on to YouTube (in segments) this year. It's a good show with mostly my photos that I'd like to share.
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: a528a_a527-012.jpg
Notes:
62318426 Here is a perfect Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly resting with wings out to catch some warmth on a cloudy morning. It's perched on a native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. This butterfly looks freshly-emerged, but I went back to check after it left, and found no signs of a cocoon. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2010)

I'm done with school for a while! Graduation happened today, and my speech was well-received. Now I get to gear-up for my Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society lecture and slide show on Insect Galls next Thursday. Life is full!

File.Name: a528b_a527-017.jpg
Notes:
62317675 Here are more luscious new flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2010)

You can see a glimpse of the distinctive "connate" upper leafs of this plant - that means the leaves are grown together on the stem like an umbrella.

File.Name: a528c_a527-018.jpg
Notes:
62317467 This Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly caterpillar is feeding on the foliage of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae) family - as it should be. (See my [Previous] photo.) It's left a fresh package of frass behind! (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2010)

These caterpillars are scarce this year. I'm not sure if it's the cool, rainy spring or the drought last year. But all insects are scarce this year.

File.Name: a529a_a529-009.jpg
Notes:
62339876 This handsome California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly is working a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family growing in the canyon today. The flat filaments of the stamens remind me of those wide matches that restaurants used to give out. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2010)

Ah it's summer - not yet by the calendar, but it is by my school calendar! I have just one more school duty - to receive the "Legacy Cup" at the Dunn School graduation tomorrow after 27 years of teaching at Dunn Middle School. My name appears on the cup after Bruce "Doc" Macomber. That's good company, and an honor.

File.Name: a529b_a529-025.jpg
Notes:
62339132 This pretty West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly is working new flowers of composite Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2010)

We have four of these "Lady" Vanessa butterflies in our woods. This is the smallest.
ScientificName: Vanessa annabella; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Vanessa
associatedTaxa: Vanessa annabella; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Vanessa

File.Name: a529c_a529-016.jpg
Notes:
62338763 There were hundreds of Convergent Lady Beetles - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - in the air today. Maybe they are converging for a mating rave? (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2010)

I set my camera (on a tripod of course) to be as un-sensitive as possible - ISO 50, f/32, with a shutter speed of 1/4 second. Every wing beat shows as a separate blip on each ladybug's trace. I figure these ladybugs beat their wings at about 23/0.25 = 92 beats/second!
Locality: on a tripod of course) to be as un-sensitive as possible - ISO 50

File.Name: a530a_a529-010.jpg
Notes:
62364600 For the scant week or three that it flowers, we have no finer plant in our mountains than this native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2010)

It used to be called Styrax officinalis var. redivivus - but it's too strange that a species native to southern Europe and the middle east is also a native shrub of Southern California! The name has been put right, but it's still an interesting shrub!

This is an example of a "Mediterranean-American disjunction", and there are more examples - like maybe native Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii in the Lamiaceae) which is related to the Mediterranean summer and winter savory plants, but grows in our canyon.

There's a web page about this by Arthur C. Gibson at:
   http://www.botgard.ucla.edu/html/membgnewsletter/Volume5number2/Styraxofficinalis.html

Gibson concludes that: "The only reasonable conclusion is that this genus was once widely distributed across the Northern Hemisphere and became restricted to certain smaller zone."

This plant also has a curious local distribution. It's very common on San Marcos Pass in both the woodland and chaparral, but where else do you find it in Santa Barbara County?
Locality: Satureja douglasii in the Lamiaceae) which is related to the Mediterranean summer and winter savory plants

File.Name: a530b_a530-036.jpg
Notes:
62363282 Here is a well-camouflaged little Katydid (family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera) nymph on a leaf of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. "Welcome to my world!" (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2010)

I wonder - if a katydid nymph loses the tip of antennae - like this one - does it get it back in it's next molt? What about whole legs?

File.Name: a530c_a530-034.jpg
Notes:
62362858 There are still mushrooms in the woods - and it's almost June! We have a typical Mediterranean winter-wet, summer-dry weather pattern, so rain this late in the season (like we had on Wednesday) is uncommon.

I guess these are the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox) mushrooms in the Bolbitaceae family because of the fissured caps, but I wouldn't bet the farm on it. They might also be the Big Laughing Mushroom (Gymnopilus spectabilis or kin). They are growing on wood chips left over from tree work last autumn, which also suggests Agrocybe. Maybe edible, but not recommended. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2010)

File.Name: a531a_a531-056.jpg
Notes:
62390568 These flowers always remind me of Holy Rollers having a meeting in the woods with arms raised for glory!

Here are native flowers of Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) in the Papaveraceae family - terrible common name! The foliage looks like poppy leaves, but the flowers are very different. I see faces... (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2010)

The plants are fire-followers growing in a place that burned two years ago in the Gap Fire.

File.Name: a531b_a531-041.jpg
Notes: 62390267 This is a tattered Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly resting on a branch in the woods above a wet area at the start of the trail to "The Playground". These butterflies are endemic to our part of the California coast. It looks like this one has been around for a while! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2010)

File.Name: a531c_a531-047.jpg
Notes:
62389763 Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera in the order Hymenoptera) delving deep into a native flower of Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2010)

The plant is a fire-follower, but it actually follows any disturbance in the woods, including bulldozing. This one is growing on ground burned by the Gap Fire almost two years ago.

File.Name: a601a_a530-031.jpg
Notes:
62412947 Here are more little winged Termites (order Isoptera) emerging from a crack in the ground after a bit of welcome May rain a few days ago. The lizards are standing ready to pick them off! (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2010)

They sure look like winged ants, but I'm pretty sure they are termites because of their wide abdomens.

No time for photos today - it was a crummy day anyway. File.Name: a601b_a531-043.jpg
Notes: 62412514 Here are interesting tiny native flowers of Mimulus floribundus in the Scrophulariaceae) plant family. They are growing in a spring-fed wet place in the chaparral on ground burned by the Gap Wildfire two years ago, though I don't count it as a fire-follower. The leaves feel distinctly slimy when you grasp them. It's uncommon in our mountains, like the habitat. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2010)

File.Name: a601c_a531-053.jpg
Notes: 62412070 Here are more of the "Holy Roller" flowers - native fire-follower Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) in the Papaveraceae plant family. I think alansky22 nailed the description in his comment on my [Previous] photo: "They look like alien druids conducting an act of worship!" But here you can see they are just flowers - albeit interesting ones! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2010)

File.Name: a602a_a602-087.jpg
Notes:
62434789 Look close!

Here is a wedded pair of uncommon "Timema" insects! They are members of the Walkingstick clan, in the family Timematidae of the suborder Timematodea of the order Phasmida.

I guess that's the skinny male on top and the voluptuous female (large with eggs) below. They've had their nuptials in a Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. I wish I could move that leaf in the lower-left over a bit! (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2010)

The last time I noticed these uncommon insects was on 5 June 2005:

File.Name: a602b_a602-095.jpg
Notes:
62433211 This is a little diurnal (daytime) Moth in the order Lepidoptera. It's visiting new flowers of composite Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum in the Asteraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2010)

Look close at its head and follow the proboscis - perfect cast! (I'm already thinking about fly fishing on the North Umpqua River in Oregon this summer!)

File.Name: a602c_a602-088.jpg
Notes:
62432692 I'm doing my public lecture on "Insect Galls of San Marcos Pass" tomorrow night, so I guess I should post at least one photo of an insect gall today!

I believe this is the gall of a Tapered Stem Gall Wasp (Andricus spectabilis) in the in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's host plant is a Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Fagaceae chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. These oaks don't usually form many galls, at least in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2010)

Look close - this gall has engulfed a terminal meristem, and it's sprouting a few individual catkin flowers!

Soon the rush of events will be over - with nothing to worry about other than taking photos, visiting my grandkids (they're coming soon!), and getting ready for the Live Oak Music Festival!

File.Name: a604a_a604-118.jpg
Notes:
62474522 Everyday miracles...

"And the day came when the risk it took to remain tight inside the bud was more painful than the risk it took to blossom." - Anais Nin

Here is a caterpillar just beginning its transformation into a chrysalis. You can see its web-spun anchor at the top.

This is the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. They feed on "scrophs" - plants in the Scrophulariaceae) plant family - but they pick any old twig to build their cocoons on. I hope to see you soon as a butterfly! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2010)

I found this caterpillar in the shade this morning. I waited 1/2 hour until the sun hit it, but I like this "not-quite-sunny" photo the best. I waited another 1/2 hour for it to shed its skin, and finally gave up. I don't think it liked being watched.

File.Name: a604b_a604-115.jpg
Notes:
62473591 Impressive insect!

After checking at BugGuide, I guess this is (now) Apiomerus californicus - one of the "Bee Assassin" true bugs in the Reduviidae family of the insect order Hemiptera. I found this fearsome predator on a leaf of just-starting-to-flower Coffeeberry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae plant family today. You can see it's mighty proboscis folded up under its thorax. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2010)

This pollen-dusted fly is standing "butts-up" in a flower cluster of composite Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum in the Asteraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2010)

Yellow is the hardest color to expose for. To capture any detail of the fly, I had to over-expose the background flower. I apologize.

Notes:
62496869 These pretty beetles are "doing it" on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2010)

I guess they are in the family of Long-horned Beetles (Cerambycidae) in the order Coleoptera. They didn't like me watching and soon broke up. I can't blame them!

Despite my photo-stream, insects are still scarce in the woods. I'm not sure if it's because of the cool spring with late rains or the drought last year. I hope it's the former reason and all will soon be well!

62496371 Shouldn't these Convergent Lady Beetles - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - be off eating aphids somewhere?

62496084 Yesterday's caterpillar is today's chrysalis - and tomorrow's butterfly!

This is the same Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) that I showed yesterday in caterpillar form:
   (http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/62474522)

Now it has become a chrysalis. Look close and you can see its future butterfly wings and antennae. It seems a bit smaller. I wonder what happened to its skin? I looked on the ground underneath it, and didn't find it. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2010)

File.Name: a606a_a606-044.jpg
Notes:
62520925 They don't call them "Bee Assassins" for nothing!

This is an impressive true bug in the Reduviidae family of the insect order Hemiptera - probably Apiomerus californicus. This one has a Bumblebee (family Bombus in the order Hymenoptera) kill in its grasp. If you look very close, you can see the stroke of its mighty proboscis stuck in the bee sucking its juices. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 June 2010)

This happened in the shade under a flower head of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. I had to over-expose by 2.5 stops to shed some light on the action.

File.Name: a606b_a606-031.jpg
Notes:
62520185 This nymph of a Katydid (family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera) is molting. Pulling its legs out from the old larval shell is hard enough, but it really takes some work to get its antennae free. Yes - the larval shell is perfect, right down to the antennae! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2010)

It's doing this on a plant of native Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing over the wall above our garden and parking area. I wondered if it would fall to the ground when done, but somehow it kept its place.
Locality: Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing over the wall above our garden and parking area. I wondered if it would fall to the ground when done

File.Name: a606c_a606-049.jpg
Notes:
62519712 Summer blue flowers of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing on the dry ground along the road at Arroyo Burro on East Camino Cielo road. They seem a bit early this year - or maybe I'm just early. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 June 2010)

This plant is called "penstemon" for a reason - there is something strange about its fifth stamen. You can see the infertile fifth stamen sticking out of the flower like a tongue as a "staminode". The pistle is visible at the top of the flower.

File.Name: a607a_a607-061.jpg
Notes:
62564250 This perfect little "true bug" in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Hemiptera is resting on a grass head on the edge of the garden. Despite its appearance, it moves v_e_r_y__s_l_o_w_l_y - which I used to capture this photo at 1/30 sec. You can see its impressive proboscis held under its head. I dig those crazy antennae! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2010)

We went to visit my Dad in L.A. yesterday, but I got a few photos before we left. We got home to late to post.

File.Name: a607b_a607-066.jpg
Notes: 62563472 This little Cobweb Spider aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae) has captured a Snipe Fly (order Diptera, family Rhagionidae - probably genus Rhagio). Unfortunately it's not one of the biting snipe flies (genus Symphoromyia) that are so nasty in the woods right now! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2010)
CommonNames: Cobweb Spider; Combfooted Spider; Snipe Fly

File.Name: a607c_a607-069.jpg
Notes: 62562949 It looks like a little flying "T-pin" - they're that small, like flying needles! I guess this is a little Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae or kin) in the order Hymenoptera caught in the air. They seem to cruise constantly and rarely land. This will do for an "I see you" photo until I can get a clear shot. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2010)

File.Name: a608a_a608-077.jpg
Notes:
62565474 This beautiful Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly is ovipositing (laying eggs) on the native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2010)

Don't miss my photos "yesterday" - actually posted today just before these. There are some good ones!

File.Name: a608b_a608-083.jpg
Notes: 62565061 This perfect little Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae) in the insect order Neuroptera) seems to be on the prowl for prey - but I saw it visiting a flower of this native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family a few minutes after I got this photo. I guess even the predators need a sugar hit to sustain them! (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2010)

File.Name: a608c_a608-081.jpg
Notes:
62564570 This pollen-dusted Convergent Lady Beetle - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - is working the new flowers of the native shrub Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2010)

It's a dirty job, but someone's got to do it!
Ah - summer days!

Notes: 62594872 Here is a handsome Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly resting on a leaf in the woods. Well he should be tired - I chased it for some distance for this photo! They are having a good year, maybe more common (so far) than the usually abundant Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfles. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2010)

File.Name: a609b_a608-072.jpg
Notes: 62594753 Here is another Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly resting (for a moment) on a grass blade in the woods. They are one of our earliest spring butterflies, and we don't usually see them this late. This must be their second or third brood this year. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2010)

File.Name: a609c_a609-105.jpg
Notes:
62594584 When a butterfly sits this still on flowers, something is wrong!

It seems to be an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly perched on new summer flowers of the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrubs in the Polygonaceae plant family growing by our mailbox.

But I'm sure this butterfly has been killed by a little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera). I never saw the predator, but I've seen others hiding in the flowers waiting for prey. I came back about 10 minutes later, and the cast off body had fallen to the ground below. Nature is harsh sometimes. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2010)

Notes:
62610662 For the few weeks that it flowers, we have no finer plant in our mountains than this native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2010)

For more discussion, see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/62364600

File.Name: a610b_a606-023.jpg
Notes: 62610063 Here are more native flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Maybe you can see that the uppermost leaves are "connate" - grown together to encircle the stem like an umbrella. There is a little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working the flower on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2010)

File.Name: a610c_a606-057.jpg
Notes:
62609757 This is a beautiful new flower head of native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2010)

These flowers seem naked without a swallowtail butterfly working them, but butterflies are scarce this year. There is a little tan-colored fly on the flower head.

There are few plants more beautiful than the native thistles! And few uglier than the non-native weedy thistles. If you are doing weed control in our mountains, be sure you can tell the difference. If unsure, leave it be!

File.Name: a611a_a611-113.jpg
Notes:
62642433 This is an action photo, so look close!

Here are native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) checking their flock of Scale Insects (Coccoidea) in the order Homoptera on the Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in front of our house. I'm not sure what the ants get from the scale insects - if it's honeydew, I can't see it. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2010)

Now look closer. The scale insect in front of the top ant is just a husk. It has exit holes where internal parasites got away. And on the right side of the photo - behind the top ant - you can see a little wasp just waiting for its chance to move in! It must be hard being the shepherd of prey as vulnerable as scale insects!

My kids and grandkids are arriving tomorrow morning, flying down from Portland for the Live Oak Music Festival next weekend! We've been busy trying to child-proof our house from a pair of 17 month old little boy toddlers. Yipes, we'll need constant vigilance! I'll do what I can to get new photos.

For more info about Live Oak see here:
   http://www.liveoakfest.org/

It seems like a plain brown butterfly, but look closer. It has flecks of purple - scattered single scales - on it's hind wings to intensify its color like an impressionist painting!

This one is working the new flowers of a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family growing by our mailbox this morning. Usually I just pass these little bugs by, but it looks pretty up this close like this. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2010)

We suddenly have a house full of babies again! My twin 17 month old grandkids (and their parents) are visiting from Portland, Oregon. They immediately set to work pulling all the books from the bottom two shelves of the bookcase. Great fun!

62649218 Like Hamlet contemplating Yorick's Skull, this Katydid (family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera) seems to be contemplating his own "mortal shell" - in this case, the larval shell it just emerged from. The yuccas are just starting to flower. I'll try to get some photos soon without stabbing myself!

File.Name: a613a_a613-017.jpg
Notes:
62673215 I'm pretty sure this is the caterpillar of an American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly. I haven't seen it before. It's working the "everlasting flowers" that grow as a weed in the garden - maybe the Cotton-batting Plant (Gnaphalium stramineum) in the Asteraceae plant family? (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2010)

File.Name: a613b_a613-012.jpg
Notes: 62672237 I believe this is a little Thread-legged Bug - subfamily Emesinae in the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera. It's perched on a leaf in the garden.
