File.Name: a614a_a614-034.jpg
Notes:
62694452 Only large-bodied bees like this Carpenter Bee (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae of the order Hymenoptera can unlock the new flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I can only describe the bee's position on the flower as a "copulatory pose"! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2010)

The flowers have a hinged lower lip which initially stands up and blocks the entrance to the flower tube. Honey bees can't unfold the petal, so they wait until the lock is broken by these larger bees.

I'm sure this is the male Carpenter bee, with the white patch on its forehead. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/61373573

The female bees are busy slowly destroying the fascia boards of our house with their nests. I let them drill their holes! The females come home every night, but I don't know where the males go.

I had some time to kill this morning before we took the kids and grandkids to the beach for fun and a BBQ, so I planted myself by the White Sage on the native side of our garden to see what I could get. Sure I got grandkid photos, but that's family stuff.

File.Name: a614b_a614-037.jpg
Notes: 62693246 Here is a tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae in the order Diptera) ovipositing (laying eggs) on the tip of a flowering branch of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the wild side of our garden. Look how its abdomen is bent with a crease! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2010)

File.Name: a614c_a613-006.jpg
Notes:
62692837 Here is another photo of a native Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) protecting its flock of Scale Insects (Coccoidea) in the order Homoptera on the Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in front of our house. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2010)

I'm not sure what the ants get from the scale insects - if it's honeydew, I can't see it. But they are real guardians against the parasitic wasps that are trying to get at the scale insects. Several times I've see the ants snap at the wasps to chase them off!
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Coccoidea; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Coccoidea; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: a615a_a615-062.jpg
Notes:
62723170 This is a close shot of a golden pug-nosed Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working an English Lavender (Lavendula angustifolia in the Lamiaceae plant family) flower in the garden. It almost bumped the front of my lens! I didn't even try to figure out which of the many kinds of skipper this is - I just "skip her" to make life easy! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2010)

Just this one photo today. We loaded kids and grandkids into cars this morning and drove down to Santa Monica to visit my Dad who is stuck in the hospital there. Great-Grandpa Bob turned 90 last week, so of course we're concerned. It was a remarkable visit with four generations of us Kummels all together at one time.

File.Name: a616a_a615-067.jpg
Notes:
62742380 Here is a tiny Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) nymph perched on a flower of English Lavender (Lavendula angustifolia in the Lamiaceae plant family) flower in the garden yesterday morning. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2010)

You can get a sense of scale by looking at my photo of a little Skipper butterfly on the same flowers:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/62740111

No time for photos today. We're getting ready for the Live Oak Music Festival this weekend! The line-up to get in and claim a camping space happens tomorrow at 3:00 pm. We get our car in line and come home - it's only a few miles away. But there's the pre-party music at our house... I may or may not get a chance to post photos tomorrow, and definitely not for the weekend!

File.Name: a616b_a613-027.jpg
Notes:
62740712 This is another perfect Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly perched on its host plant, the native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's been sampling the flowers, and still has its proboscis hanging out. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2010)

It's been a strange year for butterflies (and other insects), with a lot of some species, but other common and uncommon species are missing from the woods. I'm still not sure if it's the summer drought last year or the unusually cool spring with late rains this year. Or maybe nature is just coming apart?

At least I'm here to document what happens on the Pass!

File.Name: a616c_a615-064.jpg
Notes:
62740111 Here's another photo of the golden Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working an English Lavender (Lavendula angustifolia in the Lamiaceae plant family) flower in the garden yesterday - just to prove I didn't imagine it! (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2010)

Thank you Nano - coming from you, that comment means something!

File.Name: a623a_Bob and Norma.jpg
Notes:
62892169 Robert Kummel (6 June 1921 - 22 June 2010)
Norma Kummel (13 May 1921 - 6 January 2006)
Rest in peace

My father passed away yesterday morning. He finally got his wish to "join Norma" as he always used to say.

Dad died peacefully at home, as it should be. It was unfortunate that he picked the weekend of the Live Oak Music Festival to die - but it was also fortunate because my kids and grandkids were visiting from Portland, Oregon for the festival. We all visited Bob in his hospital room room last week before he took a turn for the worst. There were four generations of us Kummels in the room - Bob; me and Julie; my kids Ewan and Roz and Ry; and my 16 month old grandkids - Bob's great-grandkids - Otto and Kelvin. It was an occasion to remember!

This photo must date back to 1968 or 1969. It shows Mom and Dad in their garden at home.

Sorry, no Live Oak photos this year. Julie and I got the word on Saturday morning and had to leave early. At least I managed to record the Friday night campground music jam. It was fabulous. That's the real reason I love the Live Oak Festival! (I'll try *real hard* to get my recordings on the web.)
Locality:
13 May 1921 - 6 January 2006)
Rest in peace

My father passed away yesterday morning. He finally got his wish to "join Norma" as he always used to say.

Dad died peacefully at home

File.Name: a624a_a624-052.jpg
Notes:
62919592 Here is a little Convergent Lady Beetle - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - prowling through a mass of galls on a native Squaw Bush (Rhus trilobata) shrub in the Anacardiaceae plant family. Maybe the galls are formed by Jumping Plant Lice (Psyllidae) insects? (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

This is the kind of photo I love the most. It shows real ecology and lots of critters living together. I can rank my photos by the number of Latin names I need to include to tell the story!

It's been a rough week, but it was a pleasure to get out with my camera today. I remember the words of Robert Frost:
   "In three words I can sum up everything I've learned about life: It goes on.
CommonNames: Convergent Lady Beetle; Squaw Bush; Jumping Plant Lice
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae; Psyllidae; In three words I can sum up everything I've learned about life: It goes on.
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae; Psyllidae; In three words I can sum up everything I've learned about life: It goes on.

File.Name: a624b_a624-071.jpg
Notes:
62918400 Here is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) captured in the air working a new flower of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

There's some other insect in the background. This shrub is full of life!

File.Name: a624c_a624-078.jpg
Notes:
62918135 I've never seen such a display of native Sticky Snapdragon (Antirrhinum multiflorum) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family! Those are yellow flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family filling in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

This place hasn't burned, but it was "masticated" - mechanically cleared for a fire break - about three years ago. I got this photo on the side trail to the left from the Knapp's Castle parking area that goes to the upper ridge of Paradise Creek. Lynn, take note!
ScientificName: Antirrhinum multiflorum; Scrophulariaceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Antirrhinum multiflorum; Scrophulariaceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: a625a_a624-059.jpg
Notes:
62928201 Here is a beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working new summer flowers of Toyon aka California Holly shrub (as in Hollywood) (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

Usually these butterflies are abundant in June, but this year they have been scarce. I hope they are just late.

File.Name: a625b_a624-067.jpg
Notes: 62928060 Here is another little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) captured in the air working a glandular flower of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

File.Name: a625c_a624-065.jpg
Notes: 62927923 This is a large Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, family Tettigoniidae, in the order Orthoptera). Despite the size and the long ovipositor, I don't think it's quite mature - note the small wing buds. That's a curious pile of "frass" (insect scat) on the leaf. It seems to come here often. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

File.Name: a626a_a625-096.jpg
Notes:
62964816 This is another "True Bug" (order Hemiptera). It's a remarkable ant-mimic - but note the long proboscis folded under it's thorax! This bug is still a nymph. It's perched on a flower head of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2010)

There are several families of Hemiptera that have ant-mimicking members - especially as nymphs. This bug may well be in the family Miridae, but there are also ant-mimics in the family Alydidae and probably more. Maybe all my photos today show insects in the hemipteran family Miridae?

I got this photo yesterday morning as I waited for Julie in the garden. We drove down to LA to my Dad's house to start sorting, and stayed the night. My Mom and Dad were collectors like me, and this is a big job that will take many trips. It's sad, but there are also surprises - I found an old album of my surfing photos from 1962 when I was still in high school!
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Salvia "Waverly"; Hemiptera; Miridae; Alydidae; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Salvia "Waverly"; Hemiptera; Miridae; Alydidae; Miridae

File.Name: a626b_a624-075.jpg
Notes: 62964168 This may be another mirid "true bug", but this one is resting on a flower cluster of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. It's definitely in the order Hemiptera, maybe in the family Miridae? It's perfectly camouflaged against the flowers - a perfect match. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

File.Name: a626c_a624-041.jpg
Notes: 62963938 Here is a pretty "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera perched on a new flower head of a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family. Maybe it's in the family Miridae? (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

File.Name: a627a_a624-042.jpg
Notes:
62980035 The colors of summer...

Here are the red oh-so-red flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family shot against the blue oh-so-blue summer sky. I did no color correction on this photo. The colors are not quite true - too much yellow that I don't see. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

This uncommon native flower only grows in a few places in our mountains - along the Knapp's Castle trail (where I got this photo) and near Arroyo Burro on East Camino Cielo road.

The colors of summer? Blue skies and red wildfire - yipes!

File.Name: a627b_a627-006.jpg
Notes:
62979496 Here are new flowers of the uncommon native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae family. It's a real orchid growing in our summer-dry woods! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2010)

You can see that the pedicel - the stem of the flower - is rotated 180 degrees in the process known as resupination. These flowers also have a long nectar spur. They are beautiful and elegant up close like this, but from a distance they look more like asparagus stalks than anything exotic!

I mostly find them in sunny places on the north-facing side of our mountains. I guess they want shade as seedlings and sun to flower. They need a few years to flower, and only last 1 or 2 years.

I have a photo of the early spring leaves here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/59762038

File.Name: a627c_a624-068.jpg
Notes:
62978835 Here is another ant-like "True Bug" in the order Hemiptera - maybe it is a mature form of the nymph in my [Previous] photo? I believe that this is Closterocoris amoenus in the family Miridae. It's working flowers of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

See the BugGuide photos at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/153885
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: a628a_a628-014.jpg
Notes:
63008526 Clash of the (tiny) Titans!

This little spider is bringing down a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae) larva in the insect order Neuroptera - what jaws! The spider has a pretty random web, so I guess it is a Cobweb Spider aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae) - though I can't make out the hind legs to be sure. They are wrestling on a leaf of native Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing on the wild side of our garden. The spider won. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2010)

I was busy with errands today, and it was breezy. I got this photo in late afternoon. I used my on-camera flash to bring some details out of the shadows.
CommonNames: Green Lacewing; Cobweb Spider; Combfooted Spider; Wild Sweet Pea
ScientificName: Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Theridiidae; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Theridiidae; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: a628b_a623-008.jpg
Notes:
63008077 This is a spiky little Plume Moth (family Pterophoridae in the order Lepidoptera) perched on a native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

The outstanding feature of these moths is their "cruciform" (cross-like) wings. The wings are not really visible in this edge-wise photo, but you get a good view of the spines on its legs and its curved abdomen.
Locality: cross-like) wings.  The wings are not really visible in this edge-wise photo

File.Name: a628c_a623-020.jpg
Notes:
63007034 This is a little gnat-like Fly in the order Diptera working a summer flower of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2010)

Ah, but which fly? I suspect it is an atypical Bee Fly - family Bombyliidae in the subfamily Toxophorinae, maybe the genus Geron. But I won't rule out the Dance Flies (family Empidiae). The Empidiae are mostly predators which live near water, so I suspect Bombyliidae.

I have a real problem photographing a dark subject on a yellow background. To get any detail on the fly (for example), I have to blow out the flower colors. Any tips?
CommonNames: Fly; Golden Yarrow; Bee Fly; Dance Flies
ScientificName: Diptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Bombyliidae; Toxophorinae; Geron; Empidiae; Empidiae; Bombyliidae

File.Name: a629a_a629-016.jpg
Notes:
63026505 This is native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Lamiaceae) plant family growing on a sandy high place in the Santa Ynez River. The plants have an intense peppermint smell and make good tea. It's not common, but this the REAL local yerba buena for those who know it! red

It was a strange day. The marine layer was thick enough to cover us at home, so I went for a drive. The County was working on the road, so I couldn't go up the mountains - so I went down to the river instead. It was too windy for insects, but I got these flowers between the gusts.
Locality: Monardella lanceolata) in the Lamiaceae) plant family growing on a sandy high place in the Santa Ynez River. The plants have an intense peppermint smell and make good tea. It's not common

File.Name: a629b_a629-028.jpg
Notes:
63025844 Here is the sweet-smelling Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I know that "yerba buena" means any local plant that smells like peppermint, but in California this is THE yerba buena, the original namesake of the city of San Francisco. It has a spicy taste and makes great peppermint tea when dried. It's not common in our area. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2010)

The plant seems related to the Mediterranean summer and winter savory plants in the Satureja genus, but it's been recently renamed as Clinopodium douglasii, which makes the relationship obscure. Those darned botanists!
ScientificName: Satureja douglasii; Lamiaceae; Satureja; Clinopodium douglasii
associatedTaxa: Satureja douglasii; Lamiaceae; Satureja; Clinopodium douglasii

File.Name: a629c_a629-037.jpg
Notes:
63024945 Here is our other native orchid - the Stream Orchid (Epipactis gigantea) in the Orchidaceae plant family. The flower is looking a bit "long in the tooth" but it's still recognizable as an orchid. This was growing along the Santa Ynez River at the delightful spot where Lewis Creek joins the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 June 2010)

I have a recent photo of our other native orchid at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/62979496

(Actually according to the Santa Barbara Flora, we have other orchids in the woods. I haven't found them yet!)
Locality: Actually according to the Santa Barbara Flora

File.Name: a630a_a630-049.jpg
Notes:
63046391 It's a choo-choo train of Carpenter Bees!

This all began when the first male Carpenter Bee (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae of the order Hymenoptera jumped on the female. Then another male came in to join the party to form a three-some! From right to left, the bees were doing what they could to shake off those behind. (Look at the antennae for sharpness!) That bee in the back looks like he means business - but what?? (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2010)

I got the photo this morning on the flowers of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. The female bees have black eyes and hairs; the males have gray-spotted eyes and a white mane.

The female bees are busy slowly destroying the fascia boards of our house with their nests. I let them drill their holes! The females come home every night, but I don't know where the males go.
Locality:
Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. The female bees have black eyes and hairs; the males have gray-spotted eyes and a white mane.

The female bees are busy slowly destroying the fascia boards of our house with their nests. I let them drill their holes! The females come home every night

File.Name: a630b_a630-058.jpg
Notes:
63045629 This is an impressive Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) resting on a summer-dry plant in the woods. It's a large fly - at least an inch (2.5 cm) long. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2010)

I don't often notice these flies. They don't visit flowers, so I only spot them by chance in the woods.

Robber Flies are built like dragonflies. They have bristles on their forelegs to catch prey in the air, and big eyes to find them. They even have the back of their head scooped out like dragonflies so they can swivel their head to spot their prey.

File.Name: a630c_a630-068.jpg
Notes:
63044943 Here is a beautiful native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flower in the Lilium plant family growing in the canyon. These flowers are always a surprise in the mountains, like a celebrity at a campground music jam! The light on the flower is a bit weak, but I really like the dappled background. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2010)

This year with few insects has been good for the lilies. For the past few years an unknown insect grub has been destroying the stem bud and keeping the lilies from flowering. This year we have no grubs, and two plants in the canyon that are just starting to flower - at least 12 feet tall (3 meters) with 20 or more buds!

I hope the swallowtail butterflies find them soon - what few butterflies there are this year. The swallowtails seem perfectly adapted to pollinating the flowers. See this photo (and [Previous] to that):
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/14398898

File.Name: a701a_a630-047.jpg
Notes:
63069968 Here is another photo of the Carpenter Bees (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae of the order Hymenoptera that I featured yesterday. They are working flowers of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2010)

This is just before the two-some became a three-some! See yesterday's photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/63046391

The female bee - up front with the dark hair and eyes - is engaging in "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering under the sepals rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. In fact there's a good chance she is a secondary nectar robber who is using a ready-made hole in the flower already made by a primary nectar robber! Most insects - including honey bees - know this trick.

There's some more info about nectar theft at:
   http://www.jstor.org/pss/1936841

File.Name: a701b_a701-077.jpg
Notes:
63068764 "We're just friends!"

These Darkling Beetles (family Tenebrionidae, order Coleoptera - probably genus Eleodes) look like they're mating, but there's no *ahem* transfer of genetic material taking place. Maybe they are just snuggling before or after "doing it". I dig the pattern of their "feet" in the upper left! (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2010)

These beetles are snuggling on an old sun-cracked mushroom of (I think) Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox) mushrooms in the Bolbitaceae family.

I got this photo in some shade today. I got another photo in the sunlight, but it's way too contrasty to see anything. It's hard to photograph these large black beetles!
Locality:
Agrocybe praecox) mushrooms in the Bolbitaceae family.

I got this photo in some shade today. I got another photo in the sunlight

associatedTaxa: Tenebrionidae; Coleoptera; Eleodes; Agrocybe praecox; Bolbitaceae

File.Name: a701c_a701-085.jpg
Notes:
63067911 I found this beautiful little Bee Fly - family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera - in the garden today. It looks dusted with pollen, but I think those are real hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2010)

After browsing at BugGuide.net, I believe this is Thevenetimyia luctifera or close kin. See the photos at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/160602

(Hint: searching for "bee fly" at BugGuide eats a lot of computer memory!)

File.Name: a702a_a702-114.jpg
Notes:
63091812 Here is a perfect little Bee Fly - family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera - working summer flowers of Turkish Rigging (Chorizantha staticoides) in the Polygonaceae plant family. The flower has tough stems and only grows a few inches tall. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2010)

The bee flies never really land on the flowers - they just "tiptoe through the tulips" while hovering all the time, leaving the smallest footprint you can imagine!

I went for a drive today up East Camino Cielo into the higher mountains. I found flowers and new insects!

File.Name: a702b_a702-135.jpg
Notes:
63091411 We have three very similar Hairstreak butterflies in our mountains - the Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium), the Gold Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum), and the Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra). I can't tell them apart, and I've probably got them wrong before in my fotolog.

I'm not sure, but I'll guess this is the Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) butterfly. It has pointy wings and a fairly long tail. I didn't get a good look at the upper wings.

The butterfly is working the new summer flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family that are starting to flower on the summit between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro in our mountains. It's not flowering yet in our canyon. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2010)
CommonNames: Hairstreak; Hedgerow Hairstreak; Gold Hunter's Hairstreak; Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak; Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Satyrium saepium; Satyrium auretorum; Satyrium tetra; Satyrium tetra; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Satyrium saepium; Satyrium auretorum; Satyrium tetra; Satyrium tetra; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: a702c_a702-125.jpg
Notes:
63090446 Remarkable native flowers of Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. They are all the more remarkable because this is our summer-dry season when the ground is parched. I did no color correction on this photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2010)

These flowers are growing near Arroyo Burro Pass along East Camino Cielo.

File.Name: a703a_a702-123.jpg
Notes:
63111855 These are ripe berries of native Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The ripe fruit are edible and tasty if you can get past the spines. The trick is to carefully split the berry between your front teeth and then squeeze out the flesh inside. I got this photo yesterday near Arroyo Burro pass in our mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2010)

Today was a crummy day for photos - I didn't even try. The coastal fog was almost to the top of the mountains, so it was cool and windy ("fog winds") all day long.

File.Name: a703b_a702-095.jpg
Notes:
63111543 These are the rasta-style catkins of a native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see an acorn in the lower-right. Tanbark oaks differ from true oaks (Quercus) by having acorns with spreading scales and catkins that stand up like rasta dreds! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2010)

I got this photo near the summit between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro in our mountains. We have the trees at home too, but they only make catkins too high up in the tree to photograph.

File.Name: a703c_a702-105.jpg
Notes:
63111145 This butterfly has me stumped - and I'm not often stumped by local butterflies! It's working summer flowers of Turkish Rugging (Chorizantha staticoides) in the Polygonaceae plant family. From the pattern of dots on the wings, I guess that it's a threadbare Copper (Lycaena) butterfly, but it really doesn't look like anything in my books. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2010)

I won't try to guess a name - after almost 40 years in these mountains, I'm glad there are still a few mysteries!

File.Name: a704a_a704-005.jpg
Notes:
63134773 This remarkable Wasp (Hymenoptera) was working the summer flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family today. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2010)

I believe this is a Scolliid Wasp in the family Scoliidae - maybe genus Crioscolia. It's a small family of large wasps. The wasps hunt scarabs or (more likely) weevils and bury their prey. They lay an egg that hatches and slowly destroys the beetle.

My first impression is that it's a ferocious wasp with a buzzy flight and in-your-face attitude, but my second impression is that it's just very wary. It was hard to get this photo. I pre-focused on the flower and got lucky.

Today was another gloomy day at home, so I drove up East Camino Cielo road to the top of the mountains - the fog didn't clear until I got to about 3800 ft. on the summit between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro in our mountains. That's a thick marine layer!

We're off to my Dad's house again in the morning. I hope I have to post before we leave.
Locality:

My first impression is that it's a ferocious wasp with a buzzy flight and in-your-face attitude

File.Name: a704b_a704-004.jpg
Notes:

File.Name: a704c_a704-008.jpg
Notes:
63132424 Pretty little "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera perched dramatically on a flower head of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It has four antennae segments so it might be family Miridae. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2010)

This is the same yellow bug I found on buckwheat a week ago. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/62963938

File.Name: a705a_a630-056.jpg
Notes:
63263661 Here is another photo of the remarkable ant-mimic in our canyon. Look close! It's really a "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera. You can tell it's not a real ant by the wings and the sucking proboscis barely visible under its head. This insect is working new flowers of Toyon - Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family, growing in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2010)

This is surely the same critter I posted on July 26:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/62964816
But it's not the same critter I posted on July 27:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/62978835

Therefore this is not Closterocoris amoenus in the family Miridae - but it still looks like a mirid.

The mimicry is remarkable - it even has a faux "nodal bump" made by the upturned "scutellum" - that triangular piece you see between the upper wings of all true bugs.

Ants are so common - I'm not surprised there are so many insect and spider ant-mimics. There's a word for it: "Myrmecomorphy". See the Wikipedia entry:
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Myrmecomorphy
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Myrmecomorphy
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae; Myrmecomorphy

File.Name: a706a_a702-086.jpg
Notes:
63264913 Here is a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly working new flowers of Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family, growing in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2010)

It's a "Blue" butterfly, but this female looks pretty grey both above and below. The real question is what is a "Spring" butterfly doing in the woods in mid-summer? Well, it's been a strange year with few insects on the wing. We've had a very cool spring, so I guess the butterflies come when they can.

Well blow me down if we didn't get a splash of rain this evening. It smells wonderful! We have a typical Mediterranean winter-wet, summer-dry weather pattern, so we almost never get summer rain. This must be a bit of tropical moisture making its way into our part of California.

File.Name: a706b_a702-090.jpg
Notes:
63264656 This is a small predatory fly with a tiny prey - a single green aphid. I'm pretty sure this is a little Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera). They have stiff hairs on their front legs so they can catch their prey in the air like dragonflies. It's resting on a leaf of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2010)

I dig those long white eyebrows! With age, I've got long eyebrows too (mostly on my left side) so I can sympathize!

It's hard enough to photograph a scene so small, but the real trick is to spot it!
Locality:
mostly on my left side) so I can sympathize!

63264322 This is a tiny Tingidae "true bug" of the order Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae) plant family. It's left a shiny black "package" on the leaf. They have a remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body that reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor! (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2010)

I'll try to get a better photo of their top-side tomorrow.

Update:
Nano is being modest. He posted his own [i]Tingidae[/i] photo today at:

File.Name: a707b_a707-016.jpg
Notes:
63285120 This ant-like "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera has crawled into the open throat of an orange Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus aka Diplacus longiflorus) flower in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - maybe to escape the treat of rain? I guess the insect is in the family Miridae with those four-part antennae. This is like a natural light tent. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2010)

That heart-shaped structure hanging down from the top of the flower like your uvula is the stigma of the flower. It's fun to play with. If you poke it, you can watch it fold up!

We didn't get any more rain today (so far) - though its been cloudy and windy all day, and we had some thunder in the morning. The weather still feels tropical.
Locality: so far) - though its been cloudy and windy all day

File.Name: a707c_a703-001.jpg
Notes:
63284325 Here is a perfect little Pomace Fly in the family Drosophilidae, order Diptera. It has its proboscis extended on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae) plant family - maybe sucking up the powdery mildew spores? (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2010)

Its proboscis really is built like a vacuum cleaner! The fly can push it out of its face or retract it at will. It's thin, like a balloon or a condom. See my photo at:

The wasp has a long slender abdomen. This one is working a flower head of Cilantro (Coriandrum sativum in the Apiaceae plant family) in the garden that got away from us. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2010)

63307276 Here is a mating pair of charcoal-black Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterflies. They're perched on a stalk of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family, which happens to be their larval food plant. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2010)

There is a discreet stem blocking details that we don't need to see here!

*Sigh* - We're driving back down to LA again early tomorrow morning to deal with my Dad's stuff. I'll be back in a day or two.

File.Name: a708c_a707-030.jpg
Notes:
63306636 This is a remarkable summer wildflower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. It's just starting to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2010)

Everything comes in 3's with this flower - 3 sepals, 3 petals, 3 early stamens and 3 late ones, 3 carpels, a 3-parted stigma - even three bees visiting the flower! (One in the lower-right, a mating pair in the upper-right.)

It's remarkable looking - and it's also remarkable because it's a lily that picks the middle of our bone-dry summer drought to flower!
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily

63330350 It looks like this fly has green mirror shades for eyes - and carrots for a nose!

Those are it's real sense organs. I guess this large honeybee-size fly with the remarkable eyes is a Deer Fly (family Tabanidae in the order Diptera), but I'm really not sure. They've never shown any interest in biting me like deer flies do, though maybe this one is male? Whatever it is, it's beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2010)

It's working new flowers of Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family, growing in the canyon.

I have another recent photo of this "green-eyed monster" showing the entire fly at:

Whew - we got back early from LA and didn't have to spend the night. Too many trips...

File.Name: a712a_a712-050.jpg
Notes:
63374026 I don't know what this impressive Beetle (order Coleoptera) is - but I know where and when to find it. In fact, I'll christen it with a name - it's a "Mid-summer Mugwort Beetle".

It's a good-sized beetle, about an inch (25 mm) long. Last summer I found it on June 24. Two years ago I found it on July 18. I always find it on the Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) plants in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the bridge over our creek. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2010)

I don't know what kind of beetle it is - there are too darn many beetles! I won't even guess.

I went out for photos today, but it was too darned hot - even for the insects! We hit 98 degrees Fahrenheit (37 degrees Celsius) today on the porch in the shade. "It's too darn hot!

File.Name: a712b_a712-043.jpg
Notes:
63373381 I believe this goofy critter is a "Toothpick Grasshopper" - in the Mermiria group of the Slant-faced Grasshoppers (subfamily Gomphocerinae); of the Short-horned Grasshoppers (family Acrididae); of the grasshopper sub-order (Caelifera); of the insect order Orthoptera)! *Whew* That's what happens when you get your I.D.'s from BugGuide! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2010)

The little grasshopper looks hopelessly cross-eyed - even upside-down! - but I believe those dark "pupils" are just an optical effect that all insects with complicated-enough eyes show.

These little grasshoppers really do have strongly slanted faces. I'll try to get a photo that shows it.

File.Name: a712c_a711-013.jpg
Notes:
63372757 This is the caterpillar of an American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly. It's working the "everlasting flowers" that grow in the canyon - probably the Fragrant Everlasting (Gnaphalium canescens ssp. beneolens) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers have a sweet maple smell. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2010)

The caterpillars build soft shelters (visible below) from the flowers, and it's unusual to find one completely outside it's shelter. Once you notice the larval shelters, you will probably see them everywhere!

I have many photos of the adult butterflies. Note that the butterflies only have two eyespots on the underside of their hindwing. Other ladies have four eyespots.

File.Name: a713a_a708-068.jpg
Notes:
63392166 If you were to drive over San Marcos Pass on Highway 154, the last thing you would think of are native orchids growing in the summer-dry woods - but here they are!

This is the uncommon native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family. It's a real orchid growing in our summer woods! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2010)

You can see that the pedicel - the stem of the flower - is rotated 180 degrees in the process known as resupination. These flowers also have a long nectar spur. They are beautiful and elegant up close like this, but from a distance they look more like asparagus stalks than anything exotic!

I mostly find them in sunny places on the north-facing side of our mountains. I guess they want shade as seedlings and sun to flower. They need a few years to flower, and only last for 1 or 2 years. They are one of those plants that could disappear without effecting anything - though our woods would be the poorer without them.

It's still too hot for insects. We hit 96 degrees Fahrenheit (36 degrees Celsius) today on the porch in the shade. "It's too darn hot!

File.Name: a713c_a711-002.jpg
Notes:
63391452 Here are new galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. They are growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. This native shrub is especially prone to forming galls. I've been watching for them, and these are the first I've found this year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2010)

These are the unisexual summer galls that will only bear females. The ladies will emerge in late winter to spawn a new bunch of galls that will give rise to both males and females that will mate to produce more of these summer galls.

It's still a stumper to me why the galls are so darned pretty. Doesn't that just draw attention to the potential prey inside?
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: a714a_a714-084.jpg
Notes:
63412456 This is Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working native flowers of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Lamiaceae) plant family growing on a sandy high place along the Santa Ynez River. This is a diurnal (daytime) moth that works flowers and hovers like a hummingbird! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 July 2010)

It was windy today. It took many shots to get this almost sharp photo!

The plants have an intense peppermint smell and make good tea. They're not common, but this the REAL local yerba buena for those who know it!

It was another very hot day, so I went down to the river to see what I could find - and to get wet! It's just a few miles from home as the turkey buzzard flies.

These butterflies have much more color than the Pale Swallowtail in my [Previous] photo, but both are beautiful.

This plant is uncommon, but only because it's habitat is so uncommon - there aren't many flowing streams in midsummer in our summer-dry mountains. I got this photo on the travertine steps where Lewis Creek enters the Santa Ynez River. The plants are growing in the water.

It's another hot day. I was in the water too!

It was 10 degrees cooler today. Whew!

File.Name: a718c_a718-066.jpg
Notes:
63501126 Ah - red thistles with butterflies, as it should be!

Here is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly snuggling into a beautiful native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This butterfly is really "getting into" the flower! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 July 2010)

These native thistles without butterflies is like the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden without butterflies - it shouldn't be! But it's been that kind of summer here in California. Maybe the butterflies are finally coming out with the recent warm weather?

On the other hand, it was foggy and cool and breezy today. Quite a change from the almost 100 degree heat we were having only two days ago!

Today was the first morning all week that was clear! Well, not when I got up, but the fog cleared out by 9:00 am. I hope a new weather pattern is moving in.

Notes:
63220334 Here is another photo of the remarkable male Mealy Bug (genus Pseudococcus in the order Homoptera) that I featured in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2010)

The male doesn't look anything like the female - this is an extreme example of sexual dimorphism. The males don't feed. They exist to mate and live only a few days. This was a lucky capture. It's been 4 years since I noticed another one!

This critter is perched on a native grass - probably a floret of Western Rye (Elymus gaucus) in the Poacea plant family. But I reckon it was just a handy rest stop.

Kmdevos - those "2 white stripes at his back" are really just long filaments covered with a waxy exudate. I made a grab for one a few years ago, and the white stuff came off in my fingers leaving just the filament. I guess the whole insect is covered with the same waxy exudate.

File.Name: a723d_a723-199.jpg
Notes:
63546671 An enormous native Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa in the Platanaceae plant family) tree came down at my Dad's house last week. Actually the trunk split and half the tree came down, exposing this large hive of Honey Bees (Apis mellifera in the order Hymenoptera). The hive is at least two feet across. The tree split right at the bee hive, but I figure the cavity came before the bees. (Santa Monica Mountains, 23 July 2010)

The bees are distressed - they can't overwinter exposed like this. (I didn't get stung but my son Ryan did, and I did last week!)

I'm no expert on honey bees - I don't even like them much. They are not native insects, and they compete too well with the wild bees. But I think that flat sheet of honeycomb below is a nursery for new bees. I didn't photograph it on July 13 right after the tree fell, and surely I would have? The bees might either go deeper into the tree or make a new swarm. Time will tell.

See my [Next] few photos.

File.Name: a817a_a801-123.jpg
Notes:
64037915 Going full circle...

I believe this is a mating pair of Pacific Spiketail (Cordulegaster dorsalis) dragonflies in the insect order Odonata. That is the male above and the female below. These are large dragonflies - about 3 inches long with even greater wingspreads. I captured this romantic pair in a dry place in the woods, far from fresh water. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 August 2010)

Dragonflies have an odd way of "doing it". Males have a genital opening at the end of their abdomen like most insects, but before mating they must transfer their sperm to a special structure on their second abdominal segment, near their wings. When they mate in "full circle" like this, the male holds the female behind the head with special "claspers" at the end of its tail, and the female brings her abdomen up to touch the second abdominal segment of the male. Thus the circle is made.

I'm back from Oregon! I'm still sorting out my photos from the trip.

It's much easier to capture the pair! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 August 2010)

There were few butterflies in the Oregon woods this year - even in the high mountain meadows near Twin Lakes and the Umpqua Hot Springs. I saw very few Fritillaries and Parnassians, and couldn't capture their light. I guess Oregon is sharing our cool weather, because there are few butterflies on San Marcos Pass at home as well.

In a day or two I'll show photos of the mushrooms I found on the Oregon coast where they haven't seen the sun for weeks. According to the local Port Orford paper, bears are becoming a problem because there are no ripe blackberries yet in the woods. Go figure.

File.Name: a818c_a729-035.jpg
Notes:
64054506 Here are a frisky pair of (I think) Northern Checkerspot (Chosyne palla) butterflies doing a mating dance on a conifer branch in Oregon. The female (above) is holding up her abdomen as a tease, and the male is all aflutter below. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 July 2010)

I hope they will make many little caterpillars. Butterflies are scarce in the Oregon woods this summer, and they can use some help for next year!

File.Name: a818d_a729-029.jpg
Notes:
64054169 This is another butterfly I could find at home, but it wouldn't be so perfect!

It's a Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of (I think) False Solomon Seal (Smilacina racemosa) in the Lily (Lilium) plant family. I found it in a mountain meadow. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 July 2010)
CommonNames: Lorquin's Admiral; False Solomon Seal; Lily

File.Name: a818e_a803-138.jpg
Notes:
64053558 This is a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working a flower head of Teasel (Dipsacus sativus) in the Dipsacacea plant family. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 August 2010)

Hah - I could have stayed home and found this butterfly, though I would have to drive to Lompoc to find the non-native flower. Butterflies were scarce in Oregon this summer. I guess Oregon is sharing our cool weather pattern, though it's plenty hot at home as I write this.

File.Name: a818f_a730-096.jpg
Notes:
64053192 Here is a little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a flower of Perennial Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 July 2010)

Pretty as the flowers are, they are an introduced weed in Oregon. They mostly stay by the roads, and they are pretty. We cut a bouquet of the flowers to cover my parents' ashes at the memorial we held.

File.Name: a819a_a729-050.jpg
Notes:
64075546 Life and death in the Oregon woods...

This is a large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with its Bumblebee (family Bombus in the order Hymenoptera) prey. They are perched on a flower head of Wild Onion (genus Allium in the Liliaceae plant family, but I don't know what kind. Watch out little bees, there is danger in the flowers! (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 July 2010)

Today was my first day with a new school commitment - CPR and First Aid training for the 20th time or so! School doesn't start until after next week. We're off to L.A. again tomorrow to take care of my Dad's estate. I'll be back on Saturday.

File.Name: a819b_a730-079.jpg
Notes:
64075052 I have found many interesting examples of insects mimicking other insects, but I think this is the most convincing mimic I've found.

This isn't an ant. It's a "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera! You can see a bit of its long proboscis folded under its head. I guess the insect is in the family Miridae with those four-part antennae, but I can't find anything similar at BugGuide. They might be common, but they look and move so much like real ants that they're hard to notice. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 July 2010)

I only noticed this one because we have a similar ant-mimicking bug at home. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/63263661

Look close! It's interesting that our ant-mimic at home has one faux "nodal bump" while this ant-mimic from Oregon has two bumps. I wouldn't be surprised if the local ants are different.

File.Name: a819c_a803-139.jpg
Notes: 64074492 This is a remarkable wasp-mimic Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae in the insect order Diptera). I captured it at a wet spring seep in the Oregon woods. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 August 2010)

File.Name: a819d_a728-017.jpg
Notes: 64074153 This is a mating pair of Long-horned Beetles (Cerambycidae) in the order Coleoptera. They are "doing it" on a floral bed of introduced Queen Anne`s Lace (Daucus carota) in the Apiaceae plant family. This "wild carrot" is an ubiquitous weed along the roads of Oregon. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 July 2010)

File.Name: a819e_a803-141.jpg
Notes: 64073745 Here is another Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) collecting pollen from stamens on a flower of (I think) Western St. John's-wort (Hypericium formosum) with elliptical leaves - though it may also be the introduced Klamath Weed (H. perforatum) that has become a common weed throughout the northwest. The plants are in their own family - the Hypericaceae plant family. We don't have them at home. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 August 2010)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Western St. John's-wort; Klamath Weed
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Hypericium formosum; H. perforatum; Hypericaceae
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Hypericium formosum; H. perforatum; Hypericaceae

File.Name: a819f_a806-033.jpg
Notes: 64073086 We didn't spend all of our Oregon time in the woods. This is a Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) resting on a Common Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale) in my kids' backyard in Portland. There aren't a lot of interesting insects in the city, but I found enough to keep me busy and take a break from the grandkids! (Portland, Oregon, 6 August 2010)

File.Name: a820a_a820-049.jpg
Notes:
64114135 Here's a whole lot of worker Honey Bees (Apis mellifera in the order Hymenoptera) - and there's a whole lot more in this hive! See my [Previous] two photos for the story. (Santa Monica Mountains, 20 August 2010)

The bees are sleepy now in the evening. (I used flash for this photo.) Note how they are all arranged heads-up.

File.Name: a820b_a820-039.jpg
Notes:
64113655 Here's a bunch of worker Honey Bees (Apis mellifera in the order Hymenoptera) on their new honeycomb at my Dad's house. Notice that all the hive cells are occupied with new bees. I guess the hive is building up its population to swarm to a better place to survive winter. See my [Previous] photo. (Santa Monica Mountains, 20 August 2010)

Of course I used my telephoto lens for this shot. "Once bitten, twice shy!

File.Name: a820c_a820-048.jpg
Notes:
64113430 We went down to my Dad's house yesterday, so here's an update on the bees. If you can't remember, see my photos from June 23 starting with this one and clicking the [Next] button:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/63546671

The Honey Bees (Apis mellifera in the order Hymenoptera) have multiplied! Their old hive was torn apart when an enormous native Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa in the Platanaceae plant family) tree came down a month ago. They had only begun the new flat sheet of honeycomb when I last checked, but now it has filled the cavity. (Santa Monica Mountains, 20 August 2010)

Note that there's a gleam in most of the new cells of the hive. I suspect that they will swarm before the winter rains. The hive is fully exposed now - a great natural observation hive, but it will be fully exposed to the weather as well.

I expect they will swarm. I hope I'm there for the occasion.
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: a821a_a807-046.jpg
Notes:
64128845 Wil Ridge (guitar) and my son Ewan Kummel (mandolin) jamming music at Colleen and Hannah's anniversary party at the Panorama Lodge at Hood River, Oregon. Note the ubiquitous Zoom H2 digital recorder in the lower right corner. (Mount Hood, Oregon, 7 August 2010)

Colleen, I want a copy of the recording!

File.Name: a821b_a805-016.jpg
Notes: 64128245 Grandma Julie and the grandkids playing with the water table in the backyard. Julie thinks that's Otto on the left and Kelvin on the right, but it sure beats me! Even when they are together I have a hard time identifying these identical twins. (Portland, Oregon, 5 August 2010)

File.Name: a821c_a731-102.jpg
Notes:
64128059 My son Ewan and grandson Otto hiking in the Oregon woods. At least I think that's Otto and not his twin brother Kelvin. It's getting hard to tell them apart, especially when they aren't both in the photo! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2010)

Look close - Otto has a walking stick! I think he takes after his grandpa.

File.Name: a822a_a814-069.jpg
Notes:
64130624 "Seek the light..."

Here is Julie hiking through a natural tunnel of vegetation to a hidden beach in Northern California. The plants grow so lush here, that they have to literally carve the trails through the woods! (Northern California, 14 August 2010)

I have a day or two more photos from our Oregon trip to show, but I'm taking new photos every day, and I really want to show them!

File.Name: a822b_a814-077.jpg
Notes: 64130424 This is a fractal photo - the rock in the foreground matches the island in the background. I think I got this photo near Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park - somewhere between Crescent City and Eureka. (Northern California, 14 August 2010)

File.Name: a822c_a807-041.jpg
Notes:
64129254 Under the volcano...

This is the amazing view from the porch of Panorama Lodge overlooking the Hood River Valley, with Mount Hood dominating the background. It's a fabulous place, highly recommended for a wedding or just hanging out. (Mount Hood, Oregon, 7 August 2010)

The website for Panorama Lodge is:
   http://www.panoramalodge.com/

File.Name: a823a_a730-074.jpg
Notes:
64149126 Some Oregon orchids - 1 of 6

This is a very beautiful native Orchid in the Orchidaceae plant family - I wish I knew what it is! Maybe it is a form of Piperia elegans? It looks a bit like the Platanthera unalascensis, but the nectar spurs are way too long. I'm glad there are still some mysteries (for me at least) in the woods! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 July 2010)

(New thought - maybe this the same Piperia elongata we have at home???)

(Update - I don't think it's P.elongata, but Piperia transversa sounds just right!)

It seems backwards to travel north for orchids rather than south towards the tropics, but I found many orchids on our recent Oregon trip!
ScientificName: Orchidaceae; Piperia elegans; Platanthera unalascensis; Piperia elongata; P.elongata; Piperia transversa
associatedTaxa: Orchidaceae; Piperia elegans; Platanthera unalascensis; Piperia elongata; P.elongata; Piperia transversa

File.Name: a823b_a729-044.jpg
Notes:
64148037 Some Oregon orchids - 2 of 6

This is a beautiful White Bog Orchid (Platanthera dilatata var. leucostachys) in the Orchidaceae plant family. It's growing in a soggy meadow in the Oregon woods. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 July 2010)

File.Name: a823c_a801-109.jpg
Notes:
64147483 Some Oregon orchids - 3 of 6

The Rattlesnake Orchid aka Rattlesnake-Plantain (Goodyera oblongifolia) in the Orchidaceae plant family is just starting to flower, rather than being almost done as it is on our usual late-summer Oregon trip. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 August 2010)

My book says that it got it's name because someone thought the markings on the leaves resemble snakeskin markings, but I can't see it. It's by far the most common orchid near our cabin.

File.Name: a823d_a812-022.jpg
Notes:
64147024 Some Oregon orchids - 4 of 6

I believe this is the Coast Piperia aka Elegant Rein Orchid (Piperia elegans in the Orchidaceae plant family. This one is just starting to flower at Cape Blanco State Park in the wind-blown scrub along the lighthouse trail. (Cape Blanco, Oregon, 14 August 2010)

We enjoyed this state park so much that we stayed an extra day on our way home. It was foggy and windy the whole time we were there, but still very nice. I even found some interesting mushrooms!

File.Name: a823e_a729-056.jpg
Notes:
64146422 Some Oregon orchids - 5 of 6

Not all orchids are beautiful, but they are all interesting. I believe this is the Slender Bog Orchid (Platanthera stricta) in the Orchidaceae plant family. It's growing in a soggy bog. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 July 2010)

File.Name: a823f_a729-021.jpg
Notes:
64145754 Some Oregon orchids - 6 of 6

I wish this photo was sharper, but at least it's recognizable. I believe these are the Western Coralroot Orchid (Corallorhiza mertensiana) flowers in the Orchidaceae plant family. The leaf-less plant was growing in a wet place in the woods. (Twin Lake trail, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 July 2010)

This orchid is "mycotrophic" - it gets help from fungi (who probably get help from trees!). It needs help because it has no chlorophyll of it's own, and therefore lacks green coloration. The woods are connected by fungi! See Paul Stamets' talk on 6 ways mushrooms can save the world:
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XI5frPV58tY

I was quoting my book "The Wild Orchids of California" (Cornell University Press, 1995) by Ronald Coleman just now - but aren't all orchids mycotrophic because the orchid seeds are dust-like and carry no food supply for the emerging plant?
ScientificName: Corallorhiza mertensiana; Orchidaceae; The Wild Orchids of California
associatedTaxa: Corallorhiza mertensiana; Orchidaceae; The Wild Orchids of California

File.Name: a824a_a729-069.jpg
Notes:
64170381 Here is a single perfect flower of native Columbia Lily (Lilium columbianum) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's catching the sunlight just right, though I had to climb down a steep hill to get it. This flower looks too beautiful to be a native, but here it is. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 July 2010)

I did no color correction to this photo - it didn't need it, and everything I tried made it worse. Good enough!

I reckon I have one more day of Oregon photos to show some mushrooms from the Oregon coast, then back to home on San Marcos Pass in the Santa Barbara mountains. It's been very hot, but I have a few new photos to share!

File.Name: a824b_a729-032.jpg
Notes:
64170042 Perfect flower of native Red Columbine (Aquilegia formosa) in the Ranunculaceae plant family growing in a dry meadow in the Oregon woods. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 July 2010)

It sure doesn't look much like a simple Buttercup (Ranunculus sp.), but it does have lots of stamens and pistils and carpels like the namesake of the plant family.

I did no color correction to this photo - it didn't need it, and everything I tried made it worse. Good enough!

File.Name: a824c_a729-064.jpg
Notes: 64169745 Amazing colors of (I guess) Orange Agoseris (Agoseris aurantiaca) in the Asteraceae plant family. I found this beautiful flower in a dry mountain meadow. I'm sorry there's not a butterfly working the flower, but butterflies were scarce this trip. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 July 2010)

File.Name: a824d_a801-127.jpg
Notes: 64169465 This is another one of those little plants that grow under the conifers in the Oregon woods. I believe this is the Rough Harebell (Campanula scabrella) in the Campanulaceae plant family. Scouler's Harebell (C. scouleri) is very similar, but it has white flowers. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 August 2010)

File.Name: a824e_a812-017.jpg
Notes: 64169183 This is the true native Douglas iris (Iris douglasiana) in the Iridaceae plant family. It's growing in the fog at Cape Blanco State Park in the wind-blown scrub along the lighthouse trail. (Cape Blanco, Oregon, 12 August 2010)

File.Name: a824f_a730-075.jpg
Notes:
64168841 You'll have to look close to notice the native Orchid in the Orchidaceae plant family in this photo - just like you have to look close to notice them in the woods! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 July 2010)

This is the same orchid as my [Previous] photo. I'm pretty sure it's Piperia transversa. My orchid book has this description: "Plants slender, delicate, the scape mostly 1-3 mm diameter; spur longer than lip, 7-13 mm, straight, stout for the size of the flower, mostly oriented horizontally; dorsal sepal usually projecting forward between the narrow, widely spaced petals; petals white with green midvein; strong, clove-like scent produced at night."

Everything fits - though I didn't visit the plant at night to enjoy the fragrance. It never occurred to me that native orchids have a scent! According to my book, it's pollinated by night-flying moths.

Like our native Piperia elongata orchids at home, the basal leaves are totally shriveled up by the time it flowers.

File.Name: a825a_a812-007.jpg
Notes:
64189900 These are handsome Amanita pantherina mushrooms growing in the fog drip on the southern Oregon coast. They are quite poisonous - don't eat them! (Cape Blanco, Oregon, 12 August 2010)

These mushrooms show some influence from the Jonquil Amanita ([i]Amanita gemmata[/i]) mushrooms in my [Previous] photo. Note the yellow colors, particularly on the ring.

Mushrooms in August? Well, in this part of Oregon near Port Orford, it felt like December. We had two days of fog and wind, just right for mushrooms, but we were ready to leave for some sun.

That's it for our Oregon trip this year. Insects, wildflowers, scenery, grandkids, even mushrooms! It was a great trip. Now it's back to the local insects!
Locality: [i]Amanita gemmata[/i]) mushrooms in my [Previous] photo.  Note the yellow colors

File.Name: a825b_a812-031.jpg
Notes:
64189651 These are beautiful Jonquil Amanitas ([i]Amanita gemmata[/i]) mushrooms growing in the fog-shrouded woods on the Oregon coast. I believe these yellow mushrooms are also crossing with the Amanita pantherina in the woods creating confusing hybrids. They are growing in a forest of mostly Sitka Spruce (Picea sitchensis) trees. (Cape Blanco, Oregon, 12 August 2010)

Pretty as they are, these mushrooms are quite poisonous. They probably contain the same "interesting" toxins as A. muscaria and A. pantherina.
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Picea sitchensis; A. muscaria; A. pantherina
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Picea sitchensis; A. muscaria; A. pantherina

File.Name: a825c_a812-003.jpg
Notes: 64189396 This is a beautiful "Grisette" (Amanita vaginata?) mushroom in the Oregon woods. At least I'm pretty sure it's in that group of mushrooms. It's edible, but I'm nervous about eating any Amanita mushrooms so I didn't collect it for the table. It was growing under Sitka Spruce (Picea sitchensis) trees on the southern Oregon Coast. (Cape Blanco, Oregon, 12 August 2010)

File.Name: a825d_a812-030.jpg
Notes: 64189132 This is a strange mushroom I found in the Oregon woods. It must be some kind of "Tooth fungus" in the family Hydnaceae. These "mushrooms" don't have gills, but they have spore-bearing soft spines that hang like little icicles from the caps. I'm not sure what species this is. It does seem to have a stem. (Cape Blanco, Oregon, 12 August 2010)

File.Name: a825e_a812-013.jpg
Notes:
64188813 This is a strange Lobster Mushroom (Hypomyces lactifluorum) in the Oregon woods. The "mushroom" is really a reddish parasitic crust over another host mushroom, probably a Russula in this case. It's a fungus growing on a fungus! (Cape Blanco, Oregon, 12 August 2010)

My mushroom book says that they are "rated highly and sold in markets in Mexico". If you have first-hand experience eating this fungus, please let me know! I think it would depend which mushroom it was growing on.

We have another species in the the genus in our woods at home - the Bolete Eater (Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus that destroys good boletes and turns them to golden mush. I have at photo from 12 February 2008 at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/30979190

That does not look edible!

----
My son Ewan sent an update from Portland:

"I saw your fotolog post about lobster mushrooms and I wanted to mention that they are pretty common at farmers markets in portland. Pretty damn tasty too.. The name is supposed to reference the flavor not just the color. Not exactly lobster, but definitely a seafood taste. They don't look very appetizing until you cut them open and the inner flesh is beautiful milky white. In fact, Lobsters are a pretty "mainstream" wild mushroom up here, appearing even in supermarkets occasionally

File.Name: a825f_a813-044.jpg
Notes:
64188218 This a a speckled Banana Slug on some kind of Russula mushroom in the Oregon woods. The slug looks like an over-ripe banana! I've been looking on the web, and this may actually be the same species of slug - Ariolimax columbianus - that we have at home, but they sure look different. Our slugs are bright orange with no spots, but these slugs are dark and sometimes spotted. They are quite large - 6 inches or more long. (Cape Blanco, Oregon, 13 August 2010)

All of today's photos are from Cape Blanco State Park in southern Oregon along the coast, not very far from Port Orford. There were mushrooms and banana slugs in August! Also crappy weather - we had two days of fog and wind, just right for mushrooms, but we were ready to leave for some sun. We never did see the lighthouse on the cape.

File.Name: a826a_a822-026.jpg
Notes:
64207270 Here is my favorite summer moth working my favorite summer wildflower!

This is a diurnal (daytime) Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working native flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family - caught in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2010)

This was a lucky shot. The moths are hard to photograph because they move so fast between flowers. I had my camera pre-focused on another flower cluster further away, when it suddenly appeared at the closer flowers. I swung my camera around and managed to get the photo.

The Monardella flowers are all done in the higher mountains, but they're still blooming in our canyon down by the creek. The spring flowers start low and work up into the mountains. But the summer flowers start high and work down!

I have just a few days before school starts next Monday. It's good to post photos from the canyon again!
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Hemaris diffinis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Monardella
associatedTaxa: Hemaris diffinis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Monardella

File.Name: a826b_a826-120.jpg
Notes: 64206871 This is a perfect little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly "puddling" getting moisture or salts from the soil - on the gravel edge of our garden where the sprinkler went over. This butterfly is tiny - about the size of my thumbnail. Note the iridescent sparkles on the hind-wing eye spots. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2010)

File.Name: a826c_a826-109.jpg
Notes:
64206589 Here is a handsome Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) - working a composite flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 August 2010)

This humble leafless shrub is the giver-of-life for many insects that depend on it for flowers in late August and September. That's perfect timing in our summer-dry climate, when most flowers are finished. It's just beginning to flower this year.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: a827a_a827-135.jpg
Notes:
64225054 The stuff of nightmares...

This large Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae is finishing off her captive Wandering Glider (Pantala flavescens dragonfly. See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2010)

I guess she captured the dragonfly early this morning. I noticed her about 8:00 am and she was still working on it. She's one of our house spiders who rebuilds her web in the same place on the porch every night. I've never seen her out in the daytime before, but I guess she needed extra time to finish off such a large prey.

After she crawled back to her hideout, I took a ruler and measured her abdomen - about 18 mm across. She looks bigger this photo!

I also inspected the dragonfly after she left it hanging. It was just a husk and the thorax was totally destroyed. Sure I got a photo, but this is a family site and the image is too darned forensic!

File.Name: a827b_a827-130.jpg
Notes: 64224624 Here's a closer photo of the large Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae with her captive Wandering Glider (Pantala flavescens) dragonfly. It took some real work to tie that dragonfly up like this! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2010)

File.Name: a827c_a827-131.jpg
Notes:
64224371 "Tangled up and blue..."

I walked out on the porch early this morning and noticed something strange in the usual spider web on the corner. The large Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae - it's one of our house spiders - has captured a dragonfly! I've never seen the spider out in daylight before, but I guess it needed extra time to finish off such a large prey. Look how the spider has hog-tied the dragonfly! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2010)

I guess the unfortunate dragonfly is a Wandering Glider (Pantala flavescens - suborder Anisoptera, order Odonata. These are summer dragonflies that wander far from water. It was too late to rescue it.

File.Name: a828a_a828-013.jpg
Notes:
64244543 This is a little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a late-season native flower of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the composite Asteraceae plant family. Sheer beauty. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2010)

It was 20 degrees color today than yesterday - I'm glad.
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: a828b_a828-008.jpg
Notes:
64244345 This is an unlikely combination!

Here is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly perched in the same frame as a summer flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus), now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. The are both surrounded by a soon-to-flower native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2010)

These creatures don't have anything in common expect proximity. It was a cold morning for a change, and the butterfly was trying to warm up enough to fly away. The flower just happened to be there.

This pair seems like a musical discord to me - beautiful, but not together. The geometries are totally different!
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Weed's Mariposa Lily; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: a828c_a828-014.jpg
Notes:
64243918 Despite your likely first impression, this is one of the good flies!

It's a bristly Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae in the order Diptera) working some kind of late-season composite Aster (Asteraceae) in the garden today. The adults visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2010)

First impressions aren't always right. And of course spraying poison in your garden kills these helpful insects and makes your insect problems worse!

File.Name: a829a_a817-021.jpg
Notes:
64262888 Here is a cute little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) in the garden near our old fiberglass bathtub that we turned into a garden pond. These frogs are color-shifters. This frog is bright green, but they are more often brown or tan. They always have a dark stripe through their eyes. See my photos from 22 May 2010 and 19 March 2009:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/62138681
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/48351653

This frog seems pretty happy in our garden. There's no Chytrid fungus here! (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2010)

I'm too preoccupied for new photos today, but I have lots in the recent archive. I get jittery packing before trips, and I get jittery the day before school starts - tomorrow, on Monday! It will be a busy week, even though I'm "semi-retired". I can't miss the first day of school, I may have to substitute on Tuesday, and there's a school camping trip to Monterey from Wednesday to Friday. I'll post new photos when I can.

This one is working a flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2010)

Pretty as they are, the Tithonia flowers are almost too orange to photograph!

I believe it is an atypical Bee Fly - family Bombyliidae in the subfamily Toxophorinae, maybe the genus Geron. But I won't rule out the Dance Flies (family Empidiae). The Empidiae are mostly predators which live near water, so I'm pretty sure it's Bombyliidae. Compare the photos at BugGuide:

File.Name: a830a_a817-012.jpg
Notes:
64284362 This is the third - and final - stage caterpillar of a Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly. It doesn't look much like either of the two earlier stages in my [Previous] photos, but they are all the same kind of caterpillar. Of course I can't really call it an "adult" caterpillar because it still has two changes to go - a pupa, and finally a beautiful adult. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2010)

This caterpillar is munching a leaf of it's favorite food plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. I really wonder what the native caterpillars ate before fennel was introduced. Sure there are native parsley relatives in the Apiaceae family, but I can't think of any natives that flower in the summer when the butterflies are on the wing.

Fennel is a bad invasive weed in our mountains, but we always leave a few just for the caterpillars. We try to remember to prune the seeds before they can mature. (The caterpillars do a pretty good job of pruning too!)

I am substitute teaching in my old role as Dunn Middle School science teacher tomorrow. And maybe next week as well. It's great fun, but I won't have much time for new photos for awhile.
Locality:
The caterpillars do a pretty good job of pruning too!)

64284184 Here is the second stage caterpillar of a Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly. It's just stepped out of it's first stage skin. The black and white "bird dropping" colors are gone, and it has noticeable spikes on it's back. It's working its usual host plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. One more stage to go! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2010)

See my [Previous] and [Next] photos.

File.Name: a830c_a824-089.jpg

64284079 This is the first stage caterpillar of a Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly. I call it the "bird-dropping" stage, because the caterpillars really do look like little bird droppings from a distance. It's working its usual host plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2010)

See my [Next] two photos.

File.Name: a831a_a824-073.jpg
Notes:
64302796 This is a remarkable Wasp (Hymenoptera) working an orange oh-so-orange flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2010)

Maybe this is a Scolliid Wasp in the family Scoliidae - maybe genus Crioscolia? That's a small family of large wasps. The wasps hunt scarabs or (more likely) weevils and bury their prey. They lay an egg that hatches and slowly destroys the beetle.

This wasp has a buzzy flight and an in-your-face attitude. It's very camera-shy. It's hard to get any photo.

I'm off to Monterey tomorrow on a school camping trip. We return late on Friday.

Notes:
64302126 This is a brutal photo, but what can I say?

Here is an Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) that has captured (I think) a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a flower cluster of horticultural Goldenrod (Solidago, Asteraceae) in the garden. Look how the ambush bug is holding the fly by the proboscis with its crab-like front leg as it inserts its own proboscis into the fly's throat. Brutal! (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2010)

64301755 Woop-de-doo cowboy!

This is a little Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly throwing out his lasso-like proboscis over a late-season native flower cluster of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2010)

File.Name: a903c_a902-208.jpg

File.Name: a904a_a902-237.jpg
Notes:
64376553 Dunn Middle School students looking at jellyfish at the fabulous Monterey Bay Aquarium last week. I can't remember for sure, but these might be Black Sea Nettles Chrysaora achlyos. They live up to their name! (Monterey, 2 September 2010)

We had a good school trip with perfect weather in Monterey, but we got home in a heat wave. It was 100+ degrees Fahrenheit (38+ degrees Celsius) today. Too hot for insects.

File.Name: a904b_a902-265.jpg
Notes:
64375983 Here are several Leafy Sea Dragons (Phycodurus eques) seahorses at the Monterey Bay Aquarium last week. They are native to Australia. I reckon these were my favorite critters of the trip. (Monterey, 2 September 2010)

It's hard to get a photo as they don't allow tripods (or monopods) at the aquarium. I did my best by leaning against a wall to get the photo.

Notes:
