File.Name: a906a_a817-006.jpg
Notes:
64411486 This is a beautiful female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly perched on a flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2010)

The females are pretty enough, but the iridescent males are the reason why this is the official California State Butterfly. I've seen males on the wing, but no photos so far.

File.Name: a906b_a822-017.jpg
Notes:
64411189 Here is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a late-season native flower cluster of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Check out her long proboscis exploring the flowers! I got this photo by the bridge in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2010)

Damn - a truck too big for the road tried to cross the bridge on Sunday after I took this photo and ripped off the steel railing on the side of the bridge. The drivers dumped the old railing on top of these unusual native summer flowers. Everyone who works in the woods should know some botany!

File.Name: a906c_a905-037.jpg
Notes: 64410812 This humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly is working a non-native flowering succulent in the garden. It's our least patterned butterfly - really they are a "weed" species that was introduced from Europe in the 1860s. They visit many flowers, but they lay their eggs on the non-native Mustards (Brassicaceae) that devil us in California. This butterfly has a strong yellowish cast on the underside of it's hindwings. Maybe it is really the native Pieris napi? (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2010)

File.Name: a907a_a904-020.jpg
Notes:
64433185 This is a structurally perfect Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae in the order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2010)

The adults visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial.

File.Name: a907b_a824-087.jpg
Notes: 64433109 I call this a "Bridge Fly". It's a large Bee Fly - family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera - that I always seem to find perched on the steel guard rails of the bridge across our creek. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2010)

File.Name: a907c_a825-094.jpg
Notes:
64433014 This little Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) was perched on Julie's gray car. When I looked closer, I found several of them. Que pasa? (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2010)

Notice how the fly's head is scooped out between the eyes. This is diagnostic for the family Asilidae.

File.Name: a908a_a905-072.jpg
Notes:
64469024 This is the gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the Cynipidae family of the Hymenoptera order. It's growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family). It's about the size of a grape. I don't think I've ever seen the wasp. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2010)

It's remarkable to find such a thing on a scrub oak - remember that oaks make acorns! These galls are the tumor-like growths caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) and other organisms which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. They are fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Disholcaspis plumbella) in the Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae

File.Name: a909a_a905-082.jpg
Notes:
64469502 These are the brilliant red galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. These galls produce sweet honeydew which attracts ants and little flies. One ant is visible in my photo on the left. The galls look like little mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2010)

These galls are tumor-like growths caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) and other organisms which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. They are fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.

I did a public lecture on local insect galls for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society last spring. I know something about these galls, but it is remarkable how little is known about them - including how they form.

It's a stumper to me why these galls are so darned pretty. Doesn't that just draw attention to the potential prey inside? Maybe the ants protect them - but this doesn't apply with the galls in my [Previous] two photos that don't make honeydew.

64485287 Here is a handsome Scolliid Wasp in the family Scoliidae in the order Hymenoptera - maybe genus Trielis or Campsomeris? It's hunched over a flower cluster of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by what's left of the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 August 2010)

According to my book, these wasps parasitize scarab beetle larvae. That's all I know about them.

File.Name: a910b_a823-052.jpg
Notes:
64484616 Just because a plant is rare doesn't mean it's beautiful.

Here are late-season flowers of the uncommon (in our mountains) Rough-leaved Aster - Aster radulinus in the Asteraceae plant family. This native plant is growing - and flowering! - on bone dry soil in a place that was cleared for a firebreak a few years ago. The leaves are crunchy-dry, but it still has managed a few flowers in our summer-dry climate. It's a fire-follower plant, but sometimes clearing the chaparral down to bare ground is good enough to induce them to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2010)

File.Name: a910c_a904-029.jpg
Notes:
64484199 Speaking of galls...

This is one of our most interesting insect galls, though it doesn't have any visible structure like the galls I showed yesterday. I believe this is the gall (or at least a sign of the gall) of a Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis) - or kin - in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The gall wasp larvae is making changes in the oak's acorn to produce honey under pressure that will ultimately force the immature acorn from its cup. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2010)

An ant (middle-left) is interested. No surprise - it's real honey, I've tasted it. It's remarkable that a gall wasp can coax sweet honey from the bitter oaks! The acorns are stunted and will fall prematurely with a dark brown color. I guess the galls fall with the acorns - or are they part of the cup?
Locality: middle-left) is interested.  No surprise - it's real honey

File.Name: a911a_a911-029.jpg
Notes:
64505294 I believe this is a little Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae in the order Diptera), though it sure isn't typical of the family. Most of our tachinid flies are bristily, but this beauty is quite smooth. Studying the pictures at bugguide.net, I'm pretty sure this critter is in the genus Gymnosoma. It's resting on a leaf of flowering composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It looks like it howling at the sky! (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2010)

The adults visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial.

Today's photos are all interesting flies on native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. This shrub is the staff of life for late-season insects in our summer-dry woods.

File.Name: a911b_a911-039.jpg
Notes:
64504889 I believe this is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae of the order Diptera. It's working new flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a slim mosquito-like fly with a mighty proboscis, but it's definitely no mosquito. It looks a lot like the humpbacked little Bee Flies (order Bombyliidae) that I've been showing, but there are differences. The up/down split eyes seem distinctive. I got this photo close to our creek. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2010)

Does the photo look normal? I actually flipped it upside down to look more natural. The real fly is hanging down from the flowers.

File.Name: a911c_a911-022.jpg
Notes:
64503126 This is a little Bee Fly - family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera - on a new flower cluster of native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. But look below the fly's abdomen! There's the even tinier Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) that I was trying to focus on when the bee fly suddenly appeared in my viewfinder! (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2010)

The ambush bug didn't even twitch when the bee fly landed. I guess it knows it's limits, though these little bugs can bring down surprisingly large prey.

File.Name: a912a_a912-095.jpg
Notes:
64521664 Feathers are flying - or scales - as this Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly falls into the hands of a Vespid Wasp (family Vespidae) of the order Hymenoptera by the river today. The wasp is chewing the wings off the butterfly before he carries it away.

The action is taking place on a flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by what's left of the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2010)

I was already focused on the Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) that initiated this series. I took about 50 photos! See my [Previous] photos.

I'm semi-retired, but I'll be working yet again next week as the substitute science teacher at Dunn Middle School. Donna, get well! I need my mornings for photos!

64521235 Suddenly this Wasp (Hymenoptera) came by and wrenched the little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly from the Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) predator! I didn't know they did this! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2010)

Nano, I'll bet there's a word for one predator stealing another predator's catch...

I'm not sure what the wasp is. It has desert colors. I'm pretty sure it is in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. But the BugGuide Vespidae pages go on forever - 265 pages!

File.Name: a912c_a912-083.jpg
Notes:
64520182 I noticed this unfortunate Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly perched on a flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by what's left of the Santa Ynez River. It was in a strange pose - still, with it's head buried in the flower head. I've learned to investigate!

Sure enough, an Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) has captured the butterfly! You can see a bit of the predator's abdomen on the left opposite the butterfly. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2010)

Then things got interesting...
(See my [Next] photos!)

File.Name: a913a_a912-080.jpg
Notes:
64541657 Love play among the butterflies!

This pair of Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies was playing on the late-summer flowers of a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2010)

I reckon that's the male pursuer in the back and his lady up front - but maybe I'm just dreaming of being a butterfly. The butterfly in the front does seem to have a much larger abdomen - full of eggs. They fluttered for a while, and then disappeared - hopefully to do you-know-what and make more butterflies for next year!

File.Name: a913b_a912-062.jpg
Notes:
64541286 This is the most gnarly fly in our mountains! It's an enormous Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. Well, it SEEMS enormous because it's just a fly - but it's about 3/4 inch (18 mm) long. I captured this one this one working the flowers of a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2010)

I'm confused by my photo. It sure looks like the fly has an extra set of antennae on what must be its proboscis below its head. I need more photos!

It seems like the "doctrine of signatures" - these bristly flies are apparently predators of the bristly local "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae!
ScientificName: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Arctiidae

File.Name: a913c_a911-010.jpg
Notes:
64540752 This is the perfect predator that started my photo-story yesterday. It's an Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) - probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). It's perched on a new flower head of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family just waiting for prey with it's crab-like front legs. It's hard to tell where the flowers end and the legs begin. That's the point! (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2010)

This humble shrub keeps many local insects going at the dry end of our typical summer drought. It's an important plant.

File.Name: a914a_a912-060.jpg
Notes: 64557734 I believe this is a little Pomace Fly in the family Drosophilidae, order Diptera, perched on the ripening seeds of a Sorghum plant in the Poaceae plant family that came up unexpectedly in our garden. I'm sure the plant came from the birdseed we put out in the feeder. It was early morning, and the fly was just waking up. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2010)

File.Name: a914b_a912-079.jpg
Notes:
64557302 Here is more love play among the butterflies!

This pair of Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies was feeling romantic on the late-summer flowers of a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2010)

I believe that's the male in the air flying round 'n round his lady, who is on the plant at the right pretending to be interested in food. But we know what they both really want!

File.Name: a915a_a911-014.jpg
Notes:
64579671 This is a perfect Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working the late-season native flowers of composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2010)

If it wasn't for the late-season Coyote Bush and the Scale Broom, I'd have to hang up my photos for a month or two about now! But there's always something starting to flower in our mountains...

This is the photo I was all set to post last night, but the internet (and our telephone service) went down just as I was about to post it. I blame Verizon, but they did manage it bring it back after a phone call today. What did the person on the other end of the phone actually do??

I have a "Back to School" night tomorrow evening, even though I'm just the substitute science teacher at my school. *Sigh* Busy, busy busy!
Locality: and our telephone service) went down just as I was about to post it.  I blame Verizon

File.Name: a915b_a911-058.jpg
Notes:
64579398 This beautiful yellow Colias Sulfur butterfly is similar to the female California Dogface (Colias eurydice), but it is subtly different. It doesn't have pointed wing tips, and you can see some dark trim on the outside of its wings.

This is a Harford's Sulphur (Colias harfordii aka C. alexandra harfordii) butterfly working the flowers of a non-native succulent Sedum in our garden - maybe Sedum spectabile. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2010)
CommonNames: Colias Sulfur; California Dogface; Harford's Sulphur

File.Name: a916a_a911-054.jpg
Notes: 64598271 This shockingly beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly was "puddling" - getting moisture or salts from the soil - on the ground in our garden. It must be newly emerged to have such brilliant colors, but it is already missing a bit of its wing in the upper left. It's a wild world for butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2010)

File.Name: a916b_a912-101.jpg
Notes: 64598080 My [Previous] photo showed a common Grass-Skipper butterfly. Here is an uncommon Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working the new flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2010)
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper butterfly.  Here is an uncommon Northern White-Skipper; Scale Broom

File.Name: a917a_a911-012.jpg
Notes:
64616046 This looks like a Blue Bottle Fly aka Blow Fly (Calliphora vicina or kin, family Calliphoridae, order Diptera) - but it's working a native flower head of composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family, and the fly sure acted like a wild fly. I'm not sure what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2010)

Don't miss Nano's "insanely great" photo of a blow fly's eyes. It must be a different species with very large ommatidia! (Look it up.)

File.Name: a917b_a911-017.jpg
Notes: 64615558 Here is a pretty little Bee Fly - family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera - working the late-season flowers of composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2010)

File.Name: a917c_a917-132.jpg
Notes: 64615434 We found this large Underwing Moth (genus Catocala, family Noctuidae, order Lepidoptera) on the inside of the science room door at school this morning. I wanted to move it to the oak tree in the yard for a better photo, but one of the kids accidentally brushed against the door and it took off. Its wings were brilliant orange underneath as it flew away. The upper wings look like the inside covers of old books. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 September 2010)

File.Name: a918a_a918-010.jpg
Notes:
64631401 Sometimes being sharp isn't everything!

This is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera in the order Hymenoptera) coming in for a landing on new flowers of composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. The bee has full pollen sacks and a face dusted with pollen! (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2010)

Today was the first day since summer when I felt a lack of insects in the woods... Soon they will all disappear for another year. It's close to the equinox, and I also notice the lack of sunlight as the sun gets lower in the sky. Autumn is here!
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: a918b_a918-006.jpg
Notes:
64631149 This is a pretty Wasp (Hymenoptera) working a flower cluster of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family in the canyon. I don't know what kind of wasp it is, and I have no time to look it up tonight. It seems to have a doubly-constricted abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2010)

We always leave a few fennel plants for the swallowtail butterflies, but we try to remember to cut the flowers before they make seeds. It's a bad invasive weed in our mountains.

File.Name: a918c_a918-033.jpg
Notes:
64630709 Green on Green...

This is a goofy-looking Katydid in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera perched on a grape leaf in the garden today. It's still one molt shy of being an adult with real wings. Maybe it's an Angular Winged Katydid (Microcentrum rhombifolium)? Its antennae keep going for another photo or more! (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2010)

File.Name: a919a_a919-050.jpg
Notes:
64648245 AprËs sex...

The female Tarantula Hawk (maybe Pepsis mildei?, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) got right back to work feeding on the new flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub. She's got fertile eggs to make! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2010)

See my [Previous] four photos for the rest of the story. It was a windy day. I did the best I could for photos.

The wasps are almost 2 inches (48 cm) long. They really look like hummingbirds when they fly by!

This is our largest wasp - one of the biggest in the world. I haven't seen any tarantula spiders out yet this year, but they usually come out of their burrows in the fall to find mates. The wasps battle and try to sting a tarantula to paralyze it. Then they drag the poor spider into their nest and lay an egg on it. Think of the movie "Alien"...
Locality:

File.Name: a919b_a919-046.jpg
Notes:
64647920 AprËs sex...

The male Tarantula Hawk (maybe Pepsis mildei?, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) is grooming his impressive dred-like antennae. Look how he is running his antennae past his front legs. On the right you can see that he has a special hook on his foreleg for the job. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2010)

File.Name: a919c_a919-041.jpg
Notes:
64647597 "Shall We Dance?"

Male (left) and female (right) Tarantula Hawks (maybe Pepsis mildei?, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) sharing "table talk" over new flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river today. The answer was YES! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2010)

Note the gender differences between the wasps. The male on the left is much larger and has more robust antennae.

The foreplay went on for awhile, but the actual act of transferring genetic material was over in a few seconds. Is that all there is? (Or did they fail because my camera was there?)

It was foggy on the mountain and windy at the river today. I did the best I could.
ScientificName: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: a919d_a919-040.jpg
Notes:
64647238 I had another "in the viewfinder" experience today!

The dragonfly-size female Tarantula Hawk (maybe Pepsis mildei?, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) on the right was working new flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. Suddenly the male Tarantula Hawk on the left jumped into the frame! It was a windy day, I did the best I could. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2010)

This is our largest wasp - one of the biggest in the world. I haven't seen any tarantula spiders out yet this year, but they usually come out of their burrows in the fall to find mates. The wasps battle and try to sting a tarantula to paralyze it. Then they drag the poor spider into their nest and lay an egg on it. Think of the movie "Alien"...
ScientificName: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: a920a_a919-074.jpg
Notes:
64666413 This might be the most impressive fly in our mountains!

It's an enormous Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. Well, it SEEMS enormous because it's just a fly - but it's about 3/4 inch (18 mm) long. I found this one working the flowers of a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. Not bad for a windy day. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2010)

It's interesting that I can't see the impressive "maxillary palps" that are visible in my previous photo of this critter:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/64541286

I'm working myself into a blue funk... Tomorrow is Elliott Smith Day, the anniversary of his self-inflicted death. I've been listening to his sad songs to pick just one to play at school tomorrow in his memory for the kids who have never heard of him. Some high school student celebrate Elliott Smith Day by ditching school that day.

If you don't know Elliott Smith, listen to the "Live at Studion on 1998-06-02" session at the Live Music Archive:
   http://www.archive.org/details/esmith1998-06-02.flac

File.Name: a920b_a918-012.jpg
Notes: 64665725 This is a remarkable honeybee-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working new flowers of native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family in our canyon. The thin yellow bands on its abdomen are striking. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2010)

File.Name: a920c_a919-081.jpg
Notes:
64665388 Despite the petioled abdomen, this is a fly, not a wasp.

This a remarkable wasp-mimic Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) in the insect order Diptera working flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. Look at the face to convince yourself that it's really a fly! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2010)

It was windy when I took this photo at the river yesterday. I did the best I could. It's hard to get sharp focus when everything is moving!

File.Name: a921a_a919-036.jpg
Notes:
64683160 Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower cluster of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2010)

Now look very close at the spider's face - it's very deliberately holding up the pistil of an individual mint flower with one of its leg-like [i]pedipalps[/i] as a bait to attract prey!

Also notice the spider's screen-left front leg - it looks smaller and paler than the other front leg. I guess crab these spiders DO regenerate lost legs when they molt! I've wondered about that for some time.

File.Name: a921b_a918-024.jpg
Notes:
64682714 My favorite predator - this is an Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) - probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). It's perched on a new flower head of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family just waiting for prey with its head resting on the flower head. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2010)

These critters are color shifters. It's interesting that this one shifted yellow while the other ambush bugs on the same flowers shifted white. See my recent photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/64540752

I wonder which ambush bug gets the most "hits"?
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: a921c_a911-056.jpg
Notes:
64682231 Here is a young Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) on the prowl in the tangle of ground cover plants in the garden. It's only an inch or so long. I reckon it hatched from its egg this summer. Good hunting little lizard! (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2010)

Today's theme is predators. If you don't think of lizards as predators (and even if you do!), see my 2005 photo and the one [Previous] to it - taken about the same time of year:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/11084256

There's gold in the archives! And yes, I do photograph the same critters over and over, but every photo is different!

File.Name: a922a_a919-070.jpg
Notes:
64703699 Here is a beautiful Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. Not bad for a windy day! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2010)

Wednesday is my busy night - I do school photos for the weekly DMS newsnote. I apologize if my comments don't make sense. I've processed a lot of photos tonight!

The good news is that Donna is finally back as the DMS science teacher with a clean bill of health! I've been subbing for Donna all month at school. I'll help her for the rest of this week, but next week my mornings will finally be free for photos!

File.Name: a922b_a919-073.jpg
Notes:
64703290 I guess this is a Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata or kin). It's perched on the stem of a different late-season Baccharis in the woods. This is Ms. Plummer's Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2010)

It's a Long-Horned Grasshopper in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera, but this critter has surprisingly short horns (antennae). It must have lost them in some confrontation with another critter!

I don't know what that white thing is. Maybe a bird turd?

Our late season katydids don't seem to sing. They just make "clicking" noises in the trees.
CommonNames: Fork-tailed Bush Katydid; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Long-Horned Grasshopper
ScientificName: Scudderia furcata; Baccharis; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera
associatedTaxa: Scudderia furcata; Baccharis; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera

File.Name: a922c_a911-051.jpg
Notes: 64702896 This strange net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera is perched on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia in the Fagaceae plant family) leaf in the woods. I don't know anything about them, but I always find them at this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2010)

File.Name: a924a-dsc_0054.jpg
Notes:
64738596 This is the remarkable red rock at the popular Red Rock swimming hole in Paradise along the Santa Ynez River. My Hike Club kids are at the base of the rock for scale. I usually avoid it because it's also a legendary party spot, but I took my after-school Hike Club kids there yesterday for a swim, and we had it almost to ourselves. The reddish rock is a low-grade form of cinnabar - mercury ore. There are old mines nearby. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 September 2010)

My computer crashed last night after a power glitch just as I was about to post the photos. My trusty old Win98 computer finally won't boot up. That computer has almost 12 years of my life on it! Fortunately, I have backups and a new HP computer sitting on the desk in the living room, but the software feels strange and uncomfortable. You know - like when you're wife finally tosses out your favorite shoes that fit so well!

I've been so busy at school that I got lax about backups. I seem to have lost most of my September photos, though I have plans for hopefully recovering them.

Be sure YOU back up your photos!

File.Name: a924b-dsc_0015.jpg
Notes:
64738030 Here are my Thursday afternoon Hike Club kids in the lower left hiking to Red Rock. It's a perfect swimming hole located under Camuesa Peak on the edge of the Santa Barbara backcountry. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 September 2010)

It was a perfect autumn day - the first day of autumn on the spring equinox. Perfect... until my computor crashed that night. See my next photo for the story.

File.Name: a925a-a925_052.jpg
Notes:
64753989 Here is a beautiful Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus) in the garden working flowers of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2010)

We only live a few miles (as the condor soars) from the Eucalyptus trees where they overwinter by the tens of thousands, but they are uncommon butterflies here at home.

It's been a bad week for electronics! First my computer died, and today my camera started acting up - giving me photos that were way too dark and way too bright. I finally noticed that like "Goldilocks and the Three Bears", my camera was taking one photo too bright, and one photo too dark, and one photo just right - in that order!

I disabled all the "exposure bracket" functions I could find, and now it seems ok. I think it's time to reinstall the camera software!
Locality: as the condor soars) from the Eucalyptus trees where they overwinter by the tens of thousands

File.Name: a925b-a925_055.jpg
Notes:
64753617 This is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly perched on a flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2010)

Note that she is only resting on a platform of four legs. As a Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae family, she is perched on just four legs, with her two front legs held up in front in T-rex fashion. Many of our most distinctive butterflies share this trait of perching on four legs with two in the air.

File.Name: a925c-a925_010.jpg
Notes:
64753339 This handsome California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly is "puddling" - getting moisture or salts from the garden soil. Sometimes you catch more butterflies by watering bare ground than with flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2010)

I rarely find these butterflies visiting flowers. What do they eat?

File.Name: a926a-a926_027.jpg
Notes:
64771329 Maybe this is the Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus)? It's definately an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2010)

I believe it's perched on new flower clusters of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is usually a coastal flower, but here it is growing along the upper Santa Ynez River.

The Assassin Bug was feeling *blah* on a very hot day - 100 degrees at home, and 109 (43 degrees Celsius!) at the river! It was really too hot for insects today.

I get a lazy morning tomorrow - Donna is finally back as the DMS science teacher! I might even get a few photos in the morning...
Locality: Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is usually a coastal flower
CommonNames: Four-spurred Assassin Bug; Assassin Bug; Coastal Goldenbush
ScientificName: Zelus tetracanthus; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Zelus tetracanthus; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae

File.Name: a926b-a925_040.jpg
Notes:
64770344 This is the damnedest camouflage I've ever seen - kind of like a terrestrial caddis fly!

I'm pretty sure there's a moth caterpillar hiding under those stuck-on buckwheat flowers. It moves like a "looper", so maybe it's a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae or kin in the order Lepidoptera). It's perched on a late-season California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family. I had to pick through my photos to find one where you can actually see the caterpillar! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2010)

File.Name: a926c-a925_020.jpg
Notes: 64769865 Here is a perfect female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly perched on a composite flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. The pointy forewings are distinctive with this species. The males have amazing colors on their upper wings, but that's another story. (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2010)

File.Name: a927a-a927_020.jpg
Notes:
64791901 Like animals collect at a water hole in drought-stricken Africa, many wasps are cooperating at Julie's water hole in the garden!

It's just an old bathtub with a submersible water pump to move the water around, but there is more insect activity here today than anywhere else in the woods!

I believe these are Yellow-legged Paper Wasps (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin on the left, and Yellow Jacket-type wasps on the right. Both wasps are in the Vespidae family of the order Hymenoptera. They are sharing the water hole with few conflicts. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2010)

Good Lord, it was hot today! We "only" got to 102 degrees at home, but it reached 116 degrees Fahrenheit = 47 degrees Celsius at Dunn School in Los Olivos where I go to work! It must be a record. And then the power went out just as I went home...

File.Name: a927b-a927_008.jpg
Notes: 64790667 Here is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched in the throat of a succulent flower in the garden. I guess the flower is in the family Crassulaceae, but I don't know what it is. Please let me know! (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2010)

File.Name: a927c-a926_009.jpg
Notes:
64789798 Here is another photo of the Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus or kin). It's definitely an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2010)

The assassin bug has captured a tiny fly or bee. I expect bigger prey with that mighty proboscis!

File.Name: a928a-a928_002.jpg
Notes:
64808477 Here is the uncommon female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working a brilliant composite flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. She has more extensive black markings than the male in my [Previous] photo.

There may be a stumper here. In many critters the male has the brighter colors, but in others it's the female. Is there a pattern that makes sense?

My trusty 12 year old Win98 computer went down last week. The hard drives seem ok - using a command line prompt from the boot menu, I can see all my files. I expect to get everything back *sometime* soon.

Meanwhile, I'm using my new HP super computer, with new software and a new uncalibrated monitor. Does everything look "normal" for [i]Treebeard's Photos[/i]?

File.Name: a928b-a926_017.jpg
Notes: 64807889 I believe this is an uncommon male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working composite flowers of a Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. They look very plain. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2010)

File.Name: a928c-a926_019.jpg
Notes: 64807639 This Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly is working the flowers of the composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrubs on the dry wash by the river. Usually these moth-like butterflies stay in the grasses. Despite it's weak flight, it is a real butterfly, with knobs on its antennae and soccer-ball eyes. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2010)

File.Name: a929a-a925_003.jpg
Notes:
64827843 I was prefocused on the little Crab Spider (Thomisidae), when this enormous Honey Bee (Apis mellifera in the order Hymenoptera) flew into my photo. Of course it's only enormous by comparison! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2010)

Wednesday is my busy night preparing school photos for the week. One photo is good enough!

File.Name: a930a-a930_008.jpg
Notes:
64845346 Here is another photo of the remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - maybe Spilomyia interrupta or kin. It's perched on a leaf of Spearmint (Menthe spicata) in the garden. See my [Previous] photo which shows the body of the fly better, though this photo shows the fly-like face better. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2010)

This fly is strongly territorial. It was hard to photograph, but it kept coming back to the same perch and I finally got the photo.

It's a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - maybe Spilomyia interrupta or kin. It's perched on a leaf of Spearmint (Menthe spicata) in the garden. File.Name: aa01a-a926_032.jpg
Notes:
64861016 "Are you looking at me?"

This little Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) is looking right at me. I believe it's perched on new flower heads of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2010)

File.Name: aa01b-aa01_015.jpg
Notes:
64860854 This tattered Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly is pausing for a moment on a composite flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. I'll bet she has some stories to tell from her travels! (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2010)

My butterfly book says: "A cross-dresser of sorts, this species is a lady, not an admiral.

File.Name: aa01c-a926_015.jpg
Notes:
64860494 Many of these large Argiope spiders seem to have faces on their abdomens. This one has several!

I believe this is a Silver Argiope (Argiope argenata) spider or kin. It's hanging out on the very dry wash along the Santa Ynez River. It's the first one I've seen this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2010)

File.Name: aa02a-aa02_005.jpg
Notes:
64876199 This beautiful Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) bird landed in a broken bowl with water in it this morning, and took a bath. I was just a few feet away trying to photograph flies on the mint. I stopped breathing and got the photo - I didn't dare adjust my tripod! I got this photo with my 105 mm macro lens. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2010)

That's a fallen Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae) plant family berry - not a bird flop!

This little bird is a sign of a regime change in our mountains from summer into winter. It's a winter bird in our mountains. The seasons, they are a' changing.
Locality:
Rhamnus tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae) plant family berry - not a bird flop!

This little bird is a sign of a regime change in our mountains from summer into winter. It's a winter bird in our mountains. The seasons

File.Name: aa02b-aa02_030.jpg
Notes:
64875864 The holy grail of local butterfly photographers must surely be to capture the "dog face" on the upper wing of a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly. This is close! The flower is a composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2010)

It's tough because the butterflies always close their wings when they land on a flower and they seem to spring jet-like without warning into the air - they can even fly backwards! Even my butterfly books fudge this with a museum specimen. I had to put my camera on "automatic burst mode" to get this photo, and delete many photos to get this one.

It's a beautiful butterfly, with a brilliant iridescent violet sheen that is only revealed in the air when the butterfly takes wing. This is the official State Butterfly of California, but I reckon only lepidopterists and a few butterfly photographers know why!

File.Name: aa02c-aa02_009.jpg
Notes:
64875508 This is a beautiful little immature Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera on a leaf of a native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the woods. I seems very late in the year to be finding them, but it's been a strange year for insects. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2010)

It's not surprising to find new predator insects in the woods. Almost every insect that's now on the wing will disappear in the next month or so as winter approaches. It's time for the predators!

File.Name: aa03a-aa02_045.jpg
Notes:
64892883 This is a little Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus sp. in order Orthoptera, family Gryllidae) perched on the flower scar of an Opuntia cactus in the garden. This one looks green and not white, but others are brown. Good enough! This is a female. The males are more flattened with broad wings. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2010)

The crickets have really been going at it the last few nights when it's been warm. These are "thermometer crickets" because the modulated frequency is dependent on the temperature. The folklore is to count the chirps in 15 seconds and add 40 for degrees Fahrenheit. It's been warm and they've been chirping fast!

Today (Sunday), it feels like the weather is finally turning. It was cool and breezy. A trough is moving down from the north and there's a slight chance of real rain in the next few days... I'm ready.

File.Name: aa03b-aa03_021.jpg
Notes:
64892175 I found this California Spreadwing (Archilestes californica or kin) dragonfly in the family Lestidae of the order Odonata on the dry flats by what's left of the Santa Ynez River today. True dragonflies hold their wings horizontal at rest and true damselflies hold their wings vertical. These critters are in between. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 October 2010)

I think they're uncommon, but they are almost invisible against the Scale Broom plant stems. Maybe I only notice them in the fall when insects - and photos - are getting scarce?

File.Name: aa03c-aa03_005.jpg
Notes:
64891892 "It feels soooo good!"

Here's another photo of the Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) which came back to the bird bath with 5 or 6 friends this morning. I have sharper photos, but this one feels just right - the bird is totally digging it! I got this photo with my 105 mm macro lens, hunkering close by. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2010)

Mixed in the flock was a striking Black-throated Gray Warbler (Dendoica nigrescens). I didn't get a photo, but I saw it in the trees this afternoon, and I'll try again tomorrow morning.
Locality: Dendoica nigrescens). I didn't get a photo

File.Name: aa04a-aa04_024.jpg
Notes:
64910040 It rained for my birthday today! We got less than a tenth of an inch of drizzle, but it's our first real rain since last spring.

Here are raindrops on a stem of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family growing on the edge of our garden. Look at the reflections in the drops! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2010)

File.Name: aa04b-aa04_018.jpg
Notes:
64909935 It rained for my birthday today! We got less than a tenth of an inch of drizzle, but it's our first real rain since last spring.

Here are raindrops on a succulent flower in the Crassulaceae plant family - I don't know what species it is. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2010)

File.Name: aa04c-aa04_015.jpg
Notes:
64909621 It rained for my birthday today! We got less than a tenth of an inch of drizzle, but it's our first real rain since last spring. It's a real storm from the north, followed by cold winds. What a change from the record 116 degrees heat last Monday!

I found this Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing next to a seedling Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods this morning. It looks like an alien being strolling across the landscape!

It's probably been here since last year. The "rays" of the fruiting body fold back to lift up the spore sack inside when it's damp. When it's dry, they fold up to enclose the spore sack, and it almost disappears. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2010)

File.Name: aa05a-aa02_034.jpg
Notes:
64928929 Here are the first new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. These are the first flowers of the new rain year to appear! They've already (as of Oct. 5) dropped those old leaves. Life goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2010)

We have a summer-dry and winter-wet Mediterranean climate here in central California. I'm still finding late-season flowers at this dry end of the rain year, but these pretty flowers mark the beginning of a new year. I hope we get real rain this winter, though the forecast is not good.

This is interesting. In the past few years, I've noticed these first new flowers on:
3 October 2009 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/55235295
27 September 2008 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/41622484
6 October 2007 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/25713417
7 October 2006 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/16229427

9 October 2004 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/8569575
8 November 2003 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/2122554

How do they know when to flower?

File.Name: aa05b-aa03_037.jpg
Notes: 64927534 This is a flower of the remarkable late-season native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium) in the Onagraceae plant family. You can see the interesting pollen grains and the mop-like pistle of these favorite hummingbird flowers. Pretty. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2010)

File.Name: aa05c-a925_018.jpg
Notes: 4927099 These new berries of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family look delicious, but they have a nasty bitter taste. Many web sites repeat the claim that they are "edible but bitter" - isn't that like "hot but cold" or maybe "free with tax"? (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2010)

File.Name: aa06a-a925_033.jpg
Notes:
64947903 This tiny native Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) is stroking an even tinier Aphid (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera). The setting is a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2010)

This is a deep crop. I need a set of extension tubes like Nano uses for his fabulous closeups.

We had real rain today, about 1-1/4 inch or 3 cm. Not much, but it's a good early start for the new rain year! I took this photo before the rain.
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: aa06b-aa04_033.jpg
Notes:
64947691 Fallen apple with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2010)

We had real rain today, about 1-1/4 inch or 3 cm. Not much, but it's a good early start for the new rain year. I got this photo two days ago after a night of drizzle. I figure it will stand in for a rain photo today.

File.Name: aa06c-aa04_004.jpg
Notes:
64947584 I believe this is a cactus-like Milkweed in the Asclepiadaceae plant family growing in the garden - with raindrops! We had real rain today, about 1-1/4 inch or 3 cm. Not much, but it's a good early start for the new rain year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2010)

I got this photo two days ago after a night of drizzle. I figure it will stand in for a rain photo today.

File.Name: aa07a-aa07_014.jpg
Notes:
64964769 I believe this is a goofy-looking Slant-faced Grasshopper - subfamily Gomphocerinae); of the Short-horned Grasshoppers (family Acrididae); of the grasshopper sub-order (Caelifera); of the insect order Orthoptera)! *Whew* It's munching a succulent leaf in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2010)

I was looking for insects this morning, but this little grasshopper was the most interesting insect I found. I guess it will take a few days for the insects to recover from our first winter rain a few days ago.

File.Name: aa07b-aa07_027.jpg
Notes:
64964522 I took my after-school Hike Club kids to Surf Beach out past the town of Lompoc today. It's the estuary of the Santa Ynez River. We found this pretty wild bird egg on the sand by the estuary. What a work of art! (Surf Beach, 7 October 2010)

Look closer - the beach is covered with some kind of sand worms!

File.Name: aa07c-aa07_005.jpg
Notes: 64964379 There are no new mushrooms in the woods yet after our early rain, but there is a new fungus growing on some old mushrooms of (I think) Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox) in the Bolbitaceae family. The mushrooms have been here since spring. It's interesting how the mold grows in raised clusters - like a culture on an agar plate. I can almost make out letters and words... (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2010)

File.Name: aa08a-aa08_009.jpg
Notes:
64979711 I believe this is a Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. The wasp is trying to warm up on a cold morning, perched on a cactus in the garden. What a perfect pose! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2010)

Another trip to L.A. tomorrow, but it will be interesting to see what the bees are up to!

File.Name: aa08b-aa08_007.jpg
Notes: 64979505 I've been noticing these little four-winged Termites (order Isoptera) in the air after our first rain a few days ago. This one landed upside-down in a puddle on a succulent leaf (of Sedum) in the garden. It's floundering, but I don't think it's in trouble. It's trying to break off its ungainly wings - and one wing is already disconnected. I don't know what it will do next. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2010)

File.Name: aa08c-aa08_006.jpg
Notes:
64979118 This is a little female Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus in the order Orthoptera, family Gryllidae) hiding "Ostrich-style" on a cluster of pretty deep-purple flowers in the garden this morning. I don't know what kind of flower it is, but "purple haze" would be a good name for it! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2010)

It was interesting last night. I could hear the crickets across the canyon on the south-facing slopes, and I hear the crickets above us to the south - but there were no crickets here on the north-facing slope. These are "thermometer crickets" because the modulated frequency of their song is dependent on the temperature. I guess it was too cold here on the north-facing slope.

File.Name: aa09a-aa09_007.jpg
Notes:
64996540 I went to L.A. today to take care of business - and to check on the Honey Bees (Apis mellifera in the order Hymenoptera) at my Dad's house.

Their great hive was exposed when an enormous native Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa in the Platanaceae plant family) tree came down in July, but the bees tried to rebuild. Now the hive is gone. Much of it is in a pile on the ground. This is what's left in the tree. (Santa Monica Mountains, 9 October 2010)

See my photo for 20 August 2010, and [Next] from that:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/64113430
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: aa09b-aa09_002.jpg
Notes: 64996420 Here's a closer photo of what's left of the great hive of Honey Bees (Apis mellifera in the order Hymenoptera) at my Dad's house. There's still some sweet honey in the broken honeycomb - sure, I tasted it. (Santa Monica Mountains, 9 October 2010)

File.Name: aa09c-aa09_001.jpg
Notes:
64996328 I went to L.A. today to take care of business - and to check on the Honey Bees (Apis mellifera in the order Hymenoptera) at my Dad's house.

Their great hive was torn apart when an enormous native Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa in the Platanaceae plant family) tree came down in July, but they tried to rebuild. Now the hive is gone. Much of it is in a pile on the ground. Maybe raccoons or even a bear got to it? (I like to think it was a bear - my Dad would like that!)

See my photo for 20 August 2010, and [Next] from that:
