File.Name: aa10a-aa10_024.jpg
Notes:
65011695 "Slowly he turned ... step by step ... inch by inch ... "
(Apologies to Abbott and Costello!)

This is looking almost head-on at a little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera on a leaf in the woods. Actually, it's backing up, trying to get away from me. Maybe you can see its impressive proboscis folded under its chin. It's the nightmare of many small critters! (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)
ScientificName: Slowly he turned ... step by step ... inch by inch ... "; Reduviidae; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Slowly he turned ... step by step ... inch by inch ... "; Reduviidae; Hemiptera

File.Name: aa10b-aa10_016.jpg
Notes:
65011077 This large Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) with a bottle cap-sized abdomen is perched in the woods - with perfect backlight. I guess it is genus Araneus. It's web is quite large - four or five feet across. Don't walk into it at night! (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

This spider posed a focusing problem - do I focus on the back, or the legs, or the head? I went with the legs. Good enough!

File.Name: aa10c-aa10_002.jpg
Notes:
65010574 Here is a family nest of Yellow-legged Paper Wasps (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. Those green-eyed wasps on top of the nest look like they mean business! (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

Most of the cells are empty - or rather filled with tiny eggs or larvae that you can't see. Yet a few weeks ago, the cells were filled with new wasp pupae. I guess the wasps reuse the cells.

I'm interested in the eyes - some are green and some are black. I don't know if the difference is sexual, or a matter of caste, or age, or something else. I took this photo with flash - the wasps invariably nest where the sun doesn't shine - but it's not a "red-eye" kind of thing. I can clearly see the difference in normal light. There's so much to learn - even on the back porch!

File.Name: aa11a-aa10_050.jpg
Notes:
65031623 Life in the hive (3 of 3)

After posting my first photo last night: http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65010574,
I went out to check on the nest of Yellow-legged Paper Wasps (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera on the porch. I saw something remarkable! It was getting dark. I used flash and did the best I could for focus as the light was fading.

The wasps have now managed to squeeze out the "something" from it's white wrapper. The wasp on the right got its mandibles into it and flew away with its prize. I'm sure that it's an insect pupa, but it's definitely not a wasp! I guess this counts as housecleaning.

There are many different kinds of "nest parasites". I guess these wasps can recognize at least one of them. See my [Previous] few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

File.Name: aa11b-aa10_047.jpg
Notes:
65031302 Life in the hive (2 of 3)

After posting my first photo last night: http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65010574,
I went out to check on the nest of Yellow-legged Paper Wasps (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera on the porch. I saw something remarkable! It was getting dark. I used flash and did the best I could for focus as the light was fading.

This wasp has almost managed to squeeze out the "something" from it's white wrapper. It's an insect pupa, but it's not a wasp! See my [Next] photo for the rest of the story. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

File.Name: aa11c-aa10_043.jpg
Notes:
65030992 Life in the hive (1 of 3)

After posting my first photo last night:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65010574,
I went out to check on the nest of Yellow-legged Paper Wasps (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera on the porch. I saw something remarkable! It was getting dark. I used flash and did the best I could for focus as the light was fading.

The wasp on the left brought out "something" in a white wrapper. The wasps proceeded to peel the wrapper and expose an insect pupa inside. It's not a wasp! See my [Next] photos for the rest of the story. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

File.Name: aa12a-aa10_026.jpg
Notes: 65048766 This little Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera is resting on a leaf of a Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) plant in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the bridge over our creek. It's a slow-moving, but it's a mighty predator with that sword-like proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

File.Name: aa12b-aa10_020.jpg
Notes:
65048412 Here is another strange net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera perched on a leaf in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

Prowling at BugGuide.net, I think it might be family Issidae and genus Neaethus. See: http://bugguide.net/node/view/46331

File.Name: aa12c-aa03_035.jpg
Notes:
65048044 Here is a female Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) on a Chrysanthemum flower in the garden. The crickets have been singing up a storm the last few nights. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

Look close at the cricket - it's missing it's right rear leg! This seems to be a common affliction with Orthoptera. Do predators get them?

File.Name: aa13a-aa13_001.jpg
Notes:
65067060 This is a tattered Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly perched on a flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. Judging by their wounds, some of these butterflies have stories to tell! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2010)

Today's photos are all large butterflies on the fabulous Mexican Sunflower in the garden. This year, it is our best butterfly plant.

File.Name: aa13b-aa02_019.jpg
Notes: 65066998 Here is the male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on a composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden. He is more yellow in color than the female. You can see a hint of the wonderful "dog face" on his upper wings, but he mostly keeps it hidden. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

File.Name: aa13c-aa10_040.jpg
Notes: 65066941 This is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on a composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) flower in the garden. She is distictly greenish. Look at the cyan colors on her hindwings - like old peeling paint on a Mediterranean building! (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

File.Name: aa14a_aa02_039.jpg
Notes:
65088991 This is bad grass!

This is a new invasive grass that is becoming common in coastal areas - and Julie found a colony of it in our garden. It is the Panic Veldtgrass (Ehrharta erecta) which is native to South Africa. Maybe she brought it home as seeds stuck on her muddy boots after doing restoration work on the coast? (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2010)

I hope it doesn't get established. Our other fairly recent grass invader is Smilograss (Piptatherum miliaceum) which has done become a horrible invader of our mountains in the past 20 years.
ScientificName: Panic Veldtgrass; Ehrharta erecta; Piptatherum miliaceum
associatedTaxa: Panic Veldtgrass; Ehrharta erecta; Piptatherum miliaceum

File.Name: aa15a_aa02_050.jpg
Notes:
65100852 These are the galls of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. They are growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family along our driveway. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

Remember - if it looks like a fruit, but it's growing on an Oak, and it's not an acorn... then it must be a gall!

When insects get scarce in the mountains, there are always new galls to photograph! They nicely fill the gap between summer insects and winter mushrooms.
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: aa15b_aa01_008.jpg
Notes:
65100569 Here are a pair of remarkable galls of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. This is about 20 feet from our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

Remember - if it looks like a fruit, but it's growing on an Oak, and it's not an acorn... then it must be a gall!
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: aa15c_aa10_010.jpg
Notes:
65100434 This is the gall of a Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) growing on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae family near the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2010)

Remember - if it looks like a fruit, but it's growing on an Oak, and it's not an acorn... then it must be a gall!
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: aa16a_aa16_041.jpg
Notes:
65115227 This is an amazing honeybee-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working the late-season flowers of native Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) flower in the Asteraceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 October 2010)

Don't believe me? Look close - it has two wings, fly-like antennae, and you can see its fly-like proboscis down between it's front wings!

This fly really does look like a honeybee, and the fly in my [Previous] photo is too dark. I guess they are mimicking different bees? The story gets complicated because honeybees are not native to California, so they can't be a factor in how the honeybee-mimicking flies appear. Which native bee is it made to mimic?

There was drizzle and gloom all day at our house on the pass (2,400 feet), so I drove up to the summit of La Cumbre Peak (4,000 feet) at the top of the Santa Ynez Mountains, and found some sun - and a few insects!

File.Name: aa16b_aa16_039.jpg
Notes:
65114751 This is a remarkable bee-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working the late-season flowers of native Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) flower in the Asteraceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 October 2010)

It's the same size as a Honey Bee, but it has different colors. Maybe this fly is based on the old native bees before the Honey Bees were introduced?

File.Name: aa16c_aa16_027.jpg
Notes:
65114446 This is one of those WTF photos!

I drove up to the top of La Cumbre Peak today (4,000 feet) at the top of the Santa Ynez Mountains. I went high to escape the drizzle and gloom below at our house (2,400 feet).

I found this nest - I guess it's an insect nest - on a leaf of a different native Scrub Oak (Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens) in the Fagaceae plant family. It doesn't appear to be a pupal case - you can see a well-used opening at the bottom. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 October 2010)

It looks like a terrestrial Caddisfly made it. It appears to be put together with black asphalt. How did any insect larva lug all those rocks (really sand grains) up to the leaf? WTF?

File.Name: aa17a_aa17_036.jpg
Notes:
65131920 This is a Tiger Moth (family Arctiidae, order Lepitoptera) clutching her new eggs in the drizzle today. I didn't realize she was ovipositing when I got the photo, and I moved her over a bit to get a better background before I took the photo. It's touching how strong the motherly instinct is!

I believe this is a Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta). I don't understand moth anatomy well enough to explain her mouthparts.

This is one of the moths that come from the "Woolly Bear" caterpillars that are often parasitized by the Tachinid Flies in my [Previous] photo!

We had steady drizzle all day at home. It sounds like the real rain is finally starting!
CommonNames: Tiger Moth; Painted Tiger Moth; Tachinid Flies

File.Name: aa17b_aa17_010.jpg
Notes:
65131371 Look close! A little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) has hold of a bristly Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae in the order Diptera) by the proboscis. It's about to go in with its own icepick-like proboscis to finish the job. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2010)

The action is unfolding on the still-flowering (barely) composite flowers of a Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river today.

I went to the river today to escape the steady drizzle at home. I hope there's real rain coming!
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Tachinid Fly; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: aa17c_aa17_004.jpg
Notes:
65131003 The first mushroom of the new rain year!

I know exactly what this is - it's an LBM ("little browm mushroom") growing in the garden, with raindrops! Maybe it's a white-spored Mycena in the Tricholomataceae mushroom family? (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2010)

It's been drizzly all day, and at times it feels like real rain. Tomorrow is forecast to be our real rain day!

File.Name: aa18a_aa18_008.jpg
Notes:
65148272 It seems unlikely after the meager inch and a quarter of rain we've had so far this rain year, but I found this beautiful large Hygrophorus mushroom in the woods today. It's almost a foot across! It keys to Hygrophorus sordidus in my mushroom book, which says "edible but not choice". The new mushroom year has begun! (Just in time - insects are getting scarce.) (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2010)

So far, our rain event has been a bust. We've had two days of drizzle, with a bit of real rain last night, but it hasn't added up to much. There's still hope.

File.Name: aa18b_aa18_003.jpg
Notes:
65147609 Here are new grasses coming up in the woods after our recent rains. There is also a seedling native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and a Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2010)

Look at the "raindrops" on the grass leaves. These are probably not drops from our recent rain, but the result of the process of guttation - look it up!

These first seedlings may all be non-native grasses. I think the natives need a couple of inches of rain to sprout. But this is a place where native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass grows, so I'll leave them just to be safe.

Pitquim, it is possible to learn grasses, but no one can identify a grass just by it's seed leaf!
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Wild Blackberry; California Brome
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Rubus ursinus; guttation; Bromus carinatus
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Rubus ursinus; guttation; Bromus carinatus

File.Name: aa18c_aa17_001.jpg
Notes: 65146606 Here is another photo of the beautiful Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta, family Arctiidae, order Lepitoptera) that I showed in my [Previous] photo. She has oviposited a clutch of her precious eggs. It's interesting that the eggs look haphazard here, but I went back this morning and found the eggs neatly arranged in rows. (She was gone.) Does she rearrange the eggs after laying them? (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2010)

File.Name: aa19a_aa19_007.jpg
Notes:
65165068 This is a spectacular "Jerusalem Cricket" aka "Potato Bug" that I uncovered when I lifted up one of the boards in my shot of the Collybia mushroom two photos [Previous]. I believe this is the species Stenopelmatus fuscus in the family Gryllacrididae of the order Orthoptera. It must be wishing I would go away! (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2010)

This specimen is rather small - only an inch or so long, though they can get two inches long. Kids love to torment them, but you should leave them be as the Earth's creatures.

They have many names in the southwest, including the Spanish "niÒo de la tierra" and Navajo "wo se cíini" - the "skull insect". Let me know if you know it by a different name!

See Wikipedia for more info and some speculation about the name "Jerusalem Cricket". They have a sound clip of a noise that I've heard before and didn't know what it was.
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jerusalem_Cricket

We've had less than an inch of rain in the last four days, but it's been gloomy and drizzly and good for the mushrooms. It must be tough for the butterflies!

File.Name: aa19b_aa19_008.jpg
Notes: 65163873 New acorns of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family - with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2010)

File.Name: aa19c_aa19_014.jpg
Notes:
65163555 I believe these are new fruiting bodies of Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms coming up (with other annual plants) between a couple of old boards by our mailbox. There are Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2010)

My book says they are edible, but only the caps are tender enough to eat - and they're pretty thin. I consider it starvation-food. I've never tried them.

Mushroom season seems to be here, but the weather could change to hot and dry in a day. Fall is a changeable time here in central California.

File.Name: aa20a_aa17_029.jpg
Notes:
65184754 This is a perfect Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on the ground by the river last weekend. This was before the rain set in. It was drizzly at home, so I went to the river for a few photos. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2010)

The butterfly is so perfect that I reckon it must be freshly emerged from it's chrysalis. I have photos of other adult butterflies going at it a month ago, and no doubt they were busy before that.
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/64541657

File.Name: aa20b_aa16_028.jpg
Notes:
65184473 Here is a perfect Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly perched on a late-season flower of composite native Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. No surprise - it's one of very few flowers still in bloom in the mountains! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 October 2010)

She must have ambitions of being a museum specimen - look how perfectly she is posing with wings spread! And how hairy she is!

File.Name: aa20c_aa18_021.jpg
Notes:
65184242 These are the eggs of the Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta, family Arctiidae, order Lepitoptera) that I showed a few photos back:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65146606
and [Previous] to that.

They are carrying a precious genetic cargo - the instructions to make new Painted Tiger Moths! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2010)

I moved them back a bit from the edge of the retaining wall over our driveway. Now I can easily follow the progress. But when will they hatch - soon, or not until Spring?

File.Name: aa21a_aa21_004.jpg
Notes:
65200503 There are troops of these tiny Mycena mushrooms in the Tricholomataceae family coming up in the garden today after a week of rain and gloom. I stuck my pen into the photo to give a sense of scale. The ball of the pen is advertised as 0.7 mm, which makes the "large" caps 5 or 6 mm across. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2010)

We have much smaller 'shrooms in the woods - see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/58893706

File.Name: aa21b_aa18_020.jpg
Notes:
65200059 Here is a larger Leafhopper (family Cicadellidae in the order Homoptera) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family, trying to ignore the rain. I guess it's 8 or 10 mm long. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2010)

If you compare my [Previous] photo, you'll see that there are similarities and differences. They both have spines on their rear legs, but the shape of their heads is totally different.

You can see the "stellate hairs" (look it up!) underneath the oak leaf - like tiny bomb bursts!

File.Name: aa21c_aa19_001.jpg
Notes:
65199793 I think this is a tiny Leafhopper (family Cicadellidae in the order Homoptera). It's perched on a fruit of Sorghum grass in the garden - think birdseed - which gives a sense of scale. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2010)

Insects are getting scarce in the woods, and I wonder if they'll be back. It's been cold and drippy all week. But it could warm up again in a day. That's what autumn is here like in California - the temperature swings!

File.Name: aa22a_aa18_014.jpg
Notes:
65215814 This pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) is working a late-season native flower of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the composite Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. Yes Nano, you can see the spurious vein (veins?) in this photo too. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2010)

Today's photos are the last of my insect photos from the past week. It's been cold and drizzly every day, and insects are scarce. It's interesting weather for October!

Another cold storm is expected this weekend, then it should warm up for a few days. I'm curious if the insects will come back - or is their season done?
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; California Aster; Sunflower
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: aa22b_aa18_004.jpg
Notes: 65215580 This fat little Fly in the order Diptera is perched on a seed head of Sorghum grass in the garden - think birdseed for scale. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but it's blowing a bubble! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2010)

File.Name: aa22c_aa17_027.jpg
Notes:
65215350 I believe this is a Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera. Flipping through photos at BugGuide.net, it looks a lot like Epicauta pennsylvanica - though we're not in Pennsylvania!

I guess it's working new flower clusters of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family. We have lots of these late-season yellow composites, so it's hard to be sure. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2010)
ScientificName: Meloidae; Coleoptera; Epicauta pennsylvanica; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Meloidae; Coleoptera; Epicauta pennsylvanica; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae

File.Name: aa23a_aa23_031.jpg
Notes:
65230749 What the rain brings...

The rain brought us a rave of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera converging in the woods today down by the creek! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

It's been drizzly or rainy all week, but it cleared out today around 3:00 pm - just as the low sun is leaving us. There's still a 40% chance of rain tomorrow. I actually saw a California Sister butterfly in the treetops today!

File.Name: aa23b_aa23_020.jpg
Notes:
65230173 A Banana Slug's world...

I found this giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) crawling through the soggy woods today. They're 6-8 inches long! This is the largest terrestrial mollusk in our mountains. (There are much larger mollusks in the ocean.) I dig the reflections on its back! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

Are those eye-spots looking at me?

These critters seem to move slow - until you try to photograph them in the low light they prefer, and then they move way too fast for the slow shutter speeds you need! I bumped my ISO up to 800 for this photo.

I don't think I've ever seen Banana Slugs in October before, but this week of constant drizzle and rain has been just right for them. How do they survive our hot, dry summer?

File.Name: aa23c_aa23_037.jpg
Notes: 65229777 These are tiny Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushrooms sprouting from fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the soggy woods today. If you look very close, you can see an even tinier Leafhopper (family Cicadellidae insect in the order Homoptera) perched atop the largest mushroom on the right! I didn't notice it when I took the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)
CommonNames: Horsehair Fungi; Coast Live Oak; Leafhopper
ScientificName: Marasmius androsaceus; Quercus agrifolia; Cicadellidae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Marasmius androsaceus; Quercus agrifolia; Cicadellidae; Homoptera

File.Name: aa24a_aa24_014.jpg
Notes:
65246535 This large Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) with a bottle cap-sized abdomen has retreated to its shelter from the rain. The spider looks something like the Cat-Faced Spider - Araneus gemmoides - but the markings aren't quite right. I reckon that's the right genus though. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2010)

Today was a tough day for photos - windy, but I managed to capture this grand spider! It appears to be sucking a black "thing". I've seen this before. I don't know what it is.

Our storm today "came in like a lion" - we'll see if it goes out like a lamb! There's a bit of rain falling right now, but that might be all we get.

File.Name: aa24b_aa23_049.jpg
Notes:
65246004 This is another photo of an almost entire giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) crawling through the soggy woods. They're 6-8 inches long! This is the largest terrestrial mollusk in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

They only have the breathing hole - called a pneumostome - on their right side. These slugs have two pairs of 'feelers' or tentacles on their head. The upper pair has eye spots, while the lower pair provides a tactile sense and smell.

Go UCSC! (Banana slugs are the University of California, Santa Cruz mascots!)
Locality: Banana slugs are the University of California

File.Name: aa24c_aa23_034.jpg
Notes:
65245428 Here is another photo of the rave of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera converging in the woods yesterday down by the creek! This time they are converging on a branch of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

The ladybugs appear every year, but there's a question about when they appear. I keep all my fotolog comments in one big text file that I can easily search. Here are my records, as recorded by past photo comments: (You can check the archives by the dates.)


23 October 2010
05 June 2010
17 October 2009
20 May 2009
05 October 2008
18 May 2008
03 February 2007
16 September 2006
15 May 2006
31 December 2003

They seem to appear twice every year in our canyon, in the fall and again in the spring. The rave can last for a day or a week or for months. I don't know what it depends on.

They seem to appear twice every year in our canyon

File.Name: aa25a_aa23_024.jpg
Notes:
65263666 Here are raindrops on flower heads of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family - with a few spider webs. You can see the world in those drops! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

Coyote Bush is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. I think it is also an example of "protandry" - the male flowers produce their pollen before the female flowers are receptive to it. (I'm not sure this word is properly applied to dioecious plants, but it fits.) The fact is clear - the male Coyote Bush started flowering a month ago, and the female plants have mostly just begun. I'm not sure I understand the benefit of this.

Our rain and drizzle is done, replaced by cold wind. I'll take the rain any day! The woods are now in a state of peril. Seedlings have sprouted, mushrooms are fruiting, banana slugs are cruising - but will it stay wet or dry out? There's a chance of rain next weekend, so I hope the wet weather pattern continues.
Locality: I'm not sure this word is properly applied to dioecious plants

File.Name: aa25b_aa23_003.jpg
Notes:
65262742 Remember the beautiful Painted Tiger Moth (Arachnis picta, family Arctiidae, order Lepitoptera) that I showed a week ago clutching her cargo of precious eggs?
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65131920

Here are some of the same moth eggs inside a raindrop after our recent rain and drizzle. I'm sure it didn't hurt them. I'm keeping my eye on those eggs! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

File.Name: aa25c_aa23_019.jpg
Notes: 65262528 I guess these are Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) webs in the grass covered with "drizzle drops". Real rain falls too hard, and knocks the drops off. But the constant drizzle that we've been having is just right. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

File.Name: aa26a_aa23_057.jpg
Notes:
65279379 Welcome to the microverse...

These are the tiniest mushrooms I've seen in the woods - or are they slime molds (Myxomycetes)? Either way, they're fruiting from a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the woods.

I put my pen in the photo for scale. It has a 0.7 mm ball, so these little mushrooms are less than 0.7 mm across! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

I got a couple of ink spots on the caps trying to position my pen, but that "mushroom" in the upper left is doing something different to cause the dark eruption around it.

It's been dry and windy, and this "microverse" is quickly disappearing. The woods are already crunchy and the drying winds are howling tonight. There's a chance of rain next weekend to keep it alive.

File.Name: aa26b_aa23_041.jpg
Notes:
65279140 Welcome to the microverse...

These are tiny Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushrooms sprouting from a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the woods.

I put my pen in the photo for scale. It has a 0.7 mm ball, so these little mushrooms are about 3.5 mm across, about 1/8 inch - but the button caps are much smaller! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

File.Name: aa26c_aa23_016.jpg
Notes:
65278872 Welcome to the microverse...

Maybe these tiny mushrooms are Crepidotus herbarum or kin? But who really cares! They are growing on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods.

I put my pen in the photo for scale. (I left an ink spot trying to position it.) It has a 0.7 mm ball, so these little mushrooms are about 3.5 mm across, about 1/8 inch. "Cat's Ear Fungus" would be a good name for them! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

File.Name: aa27a_aa26_008.jpg
Notes: 65295346 I found this Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) in a prickly place yesterday! For some reason, it climbed up this columnar cactus in the garden. D'oh! It seemed to be stuck, but I came back in 20 minutes and it was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2010)
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; cactus

File.Name: aa27b_aa25_002.jpg
Notes:
65295209 And now for something completely different...

This is a little female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perched on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) right off our upstairs deck. She's scratching herself - look close below her eye and you can see her foot. Ah, it feels so good! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2010)

It was interesting - through the cold weather and constant drizzle last week, the hummers were about the only birds I saw. Yet as the smallest birds, they should have the hardest time keeping warm. Go figure!

File.Name: aa27c_aa24_012.jpg
Notes:
65295015 Welcome to the microverse...

These are tiny Puffballs (Lycoperdon) in the woods last weekend. They look like something found under the sea!

I put my pen in the photo for scale. It has a 0.7 mm ball, so these little mushrooms are about 5-7 mm across. I don't think that edibility matters! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

File.Name: aa28a_aa23-013.jpg
Notes:
65324765 Here is a troop of "Little Brown Mushrooms" - LBMs - fruiting on old wood chips along our road. They have rusty-brown spores and adnate gills, which puts them into the Galerina group. I think these might be Tubaria furfuracea or kin. My mushroom book describes them as the "quintessential LBM". The little mushrooms look robust here, but they dry out to almost nothing.

No LBM with rusty spores should be eaten. They are hard to tell apart, and at least one of them - Galerina autumnalis - contains deadly amanita-toxins!

I'm posting this on Friday, October 29. I couldn't post last night because I had a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society lecture to introduce and I got home late - and Julie got back from 2 weeks in Portland!
Locality:

File.Name: aa29a_aa23-055.jpg
Notes:
65325733 I found this odd little immature Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) resting on a turquoise garden hose in the garden last week in the rain. It looks strange but beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

Did you notice that it's missing its left leg?

It's still clear as I write, but we're supposed to get another rain storm later tonight and tomorrow. I have a duty to work at our Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society plant sale tomorrow. I'll bring a raincoat!

File.Name: aa29b_aa29-008.jpg
Notes: 65325370 This is not-the-best photo of a beautiful Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly. I was looking for birds to photograph this morning, and I noticed the butterfly perched high up in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree trying to get warm in the sun. This is a "tele-macro" shot with my long Tamron 200-500 mm lens, on our shaky upstairs deck. It's a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2010)

File.Name: aa29c_aa24-015.jpg
Notes: 65324940 This humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae ) was the only butterfly I saw out in the rain and drizzle last week. Here it is perched on a leaf of Canna Lily (family Cannaceae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2010)

File.Name: aa30a_aa30-030.jpg
Notes:
65340071 This photo shows a tiny Leafhopper (family Cicadellidae insect in the order Homoptera) perched under an umbrella of water - a raindrop held by surface tension under the seed leaves of a Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) plant on the wild edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2010)

We're having an interesting rain year. I read this morning that we've had the most rain days in October on record, but it's also a La NiÒa year, so it might be a dry spring. We'll see what comes. Every year is different!
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Homoptera; Galium aparine; La NiÒa
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Homoptera; Galium aparine; La NiÒa

File.Name: aa30b_aa30-014.jpg
Notes:
65339718 Yum - here's bunch of delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms for dinner tonight! I found them in the woods today. They sell in local markets for $25 a pound. This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2010)

I have found Chanterelles before in every month between November and March - but this is the first time I've ever found them in October. My earliest prior record is November 25, 2004.

We're having an interesting rain year. I read this morning that we've had the most rain days in October on record, but not the most rain. (I think that was last year - 10.3 inches on October 14!)

File.Name: aa30c_aa30-028.jpg
Notes: 65339004 This is looking eye-to-eye at a "Yellowjacket" type wasp today, probably in the genus Vespula of the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. It seems to have unusually robust antennae. It was cold, and the wasp was grounded on this mint leaf near our water garden. They don't bother us. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2010)

File.Name: aa31a_aa24-008.jpg
Notes:
65352946 Here's a really scary photo for Halloween. Don't keep reading if you are squeamish!

These are the prey at the mouth of the large Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) with a bottle cap-sized abdomen in my [Previous] photo. The spider looks something like the Cat-Faced Spider - Araneus gemmoides. I don't think the prey have been "cocooned" (as they say in the movie Aliens). They are being eaten one-by-one, starting at the bottom. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2010)

The prey are three little Leafhopper (family Cicadellidae) insects in the order Homoptera. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65199793

The upper two hoppers look so sad, like they know their fate. The bottom hopper seems to be missing its face. Did the spider suck it right off?
Locality:
family Cicadellidae) insects in the order Homoptera. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65199793

The upper two hoppers look so sad

associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus gemmoides; Aliens; Cicadellidae; Homoptera

File.Name: aa31b_aa24-007.jpg
Notes:
65352627 Here's a scary spider for Halloween!

This large Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) with a bottle cap-sized abdomen is hanging from it's web in the woods. The spider looks something like the Cat-Faced Spider - Araneus gemmoides - but the markings aren't quite right. I reckon that's the right genus though. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2010)

But what does it have in its mouth? See my [Next] photo for a tight crop!

I took this photo with my on-camera flash. I really didn't have a choice because the woods were dark and breezy.

File.Name: aa31c_aa23-001.jpg
Notes:
65352338 Here's a spider web with raindrops for Halloween. It's the web of the same kind of large Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) with a bottle cap-sized abdomen that I posted last week, at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65246535

This web is under the eaves of our house, and I can see the spider perched on the side of it. The spider looks something like the Cat-Faced Spider - Araneus gemmoides. Fortunately the web is up high in the eaves so I can't walk into it! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2010)

File.Name: ab01a_aa31-039.jpg
Notes:
65370128 This is another ecology photo - you can tell by the number of Latin names it takes to describe it!

This is a Yellowjacket-type wasp, probably in the genus Vespula of the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's perched on a honeydew-producing gall of the Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis melliflica) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (Maybe - see my [Previous] photo.) The galls are growing on a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. These galls produce honeydew which attracts many insects. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2010)

The weather is turning warm again - maybe up to 90 degrees by Wednesday. The crickets are going at it again tonight. Fall is an interesting time in Central California!
CommonNames: Yellowjacket; Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Vespula; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis melliflica; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
