File.Name: ab01b_aa31-012.jpg
Notes:
65369715 This interesting Fly in the order Diptera is perched on the galls of a Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis melliflica) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - but cp. the similar galls of the Flat-topped Honeydew Gall wasp (Disholcaspis eldoradensis) and the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa). It's one of those critters! The galls are growing on a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2010)

I think the fly might be a Scavenger Fly aka Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae.

These galls produce honeydew which attracts many insects. See my [Next] photo.
CommonNames: Fly; Twig Gall Wasp; Flat-topped Honeydew Gall wasp; Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Scavenger Fly; Flesh Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Disholcaspis melliflica; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis eldoradensis; Disholcaspis prehensa; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Sarcophagidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Disholcaspis melliflica; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis eldoradensis; Disholcaspis prehensa; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Sarcophagidae

File.Name: ab01c_aa31-045.jpg
Notes: 65369161 Here is the perfect web of a small Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) - maybe a "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa), though it doesn't have any "garbage" to show yet. It's only a few inches across - smaller than a CD, which it resembles because of the interference colors that are reflected. This one seems to be made between natural goalposts! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2010)

File.Name: ab02a_ab02-012.jpg
Notes: 65386116 This photo shows the strange geometry of the leaves and new staminate flower buds of Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum) growing on Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa in the Platanaceae plant family). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 November 2010)

File.Name: ab02b_aa31-024.jpg
Notes:
65385912 Who says we don't have "fall color" in California!

These are the red autumn leaves and seeds of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) plant family. It's beautiful, but don't touch it! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2010)

File.Name: ab02c_aa31-028.jpg
Notes:
65385646 Here's another "ecology-type" photo...

These tiny Flies in the order Diptera are gathering on a new bud of a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I wonder what they find so interesting - is the bud producing honeydew? (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2010)

File.Name: ab03a_ab02-003.jpg
Notes:
65402515 Insects are getting scarce in our woods, but I found this interesting Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working the late-season flowers of native composite Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the dry flood plain by the river yesterday. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 November 2010)

This is the last of the late-season yellow composite flowers on the dry river bed.

File.Name: ab03b_ab02-001.jpg
Notes: 65402388 I believe this is an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe it's a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus or kin) because of the humps on the back of its thorax. It's perched on a late-flowering composite plant of Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the dry flood plain by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 November 2010)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Four-spurred Assassin Bug; Palmer's Goldenbush
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Zelus tetracanthus; Ericameria palmeri; pachylepis
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Zelus tetracanthus; Ericameria palmeri; pachylepis

File.Name: ab03c_aa17-026.jpg
Notes:
65402244 This photo shows something remarkable - the "gall" where Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum in the Viscacea plant family) meets its host plant, the Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa in the Platanaceae plant family). Mistletoe is a common plant parasite on our local trees. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2010)

I've been sitting on this photo for a while, but this seems like the right time to show it after yesterday's photo.
ScientificName: Phoradendron macrophyllum; Viscacea; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Phoradendron macrophyllum; Viscacea; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: ab04a_ab04-007.jpg
Notes:
65416932 I found this cute little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) today on a succulent plant about 15 feet from our old fiberglass bathtub that we turned into a garden pond. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2010)

Look close - you can see the sun rise as the gleam in its eye!

(The sun rises at about 10:30 AM at this time of year on the shady side of our east-west mountains!)

File.Name: ab04b_ab03-003.jpg
Notes:
65416647 The mushrooms are trying to come out, but the weather is not cooperating. It got to 96 degrees yesterday! The oak leaves in the woods are crunchy, but the ground is still moist from recent rains.

This is a Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) mushroom in the woods under the oaks that I found yesterday. It's edible, but not choice. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2010)

File.Name: ab04c_aa31-042.jpg
Notes:
65416388 This is bad grass - Smilograss (Piptatherum miliaceum)!

This non-native invasive grass has become a horrible invader of our mountains in the past 20 years. Here it has filled the side trail from Knapp's Castle. It comes up in the middle of native plant clumps and drives them out. This plant invasion has happened within my time - watch out! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2010)

I think of this as a "Forest Service Weed" - where ever they work it seems to come up. Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) is another.

File.Name: ab05a_aa30-024.jpg
Notes:
65432880 The forests of Lilliput...

This is a peek at the tiny forest of mosses, liverworts, and lichens growing on a rock just beyond our garden in the woods. The largest lichens in the photo are about 1 cm high. You can imagine yourself small and get lost in these tiny forests! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2010)

File.Name: ab06a_ab06-067.jpg
Notes:
65442766 I found this delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom in the woods today. It's good timing because we're going out to dinner at a friend's who does free-diving in the Santa Barbara channel. Chanterelles will go great with lobster and abalone! Maybe I'll get a photo of the spread. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2010)

Rain, then sun - now more rain! That's what autumn is like in the Santa Barbara mountains. It was hot all week, but the weather has turned gloomy again today with a chance of rain tomorrow. I hope it's enough to keep the mushrooms coming!

File.Name: ab06b_ab04-005.jpg
Notes: 65442547 It warmed up this week, and I found this California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - getting moisture or salts - from a sandstone outcrop. She's a bit tattered, but in good shape for this late in the season! (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2010)

File.Name: ab06c_ab04-003.jpg
Notes: 65442468 This is just pretty! It shows flowers of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. austrolitoralis) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family growing on the wild edge of our garden. It's remarkable that these plants are still flowering after our long dry summer. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2010)

File.Name: ab07a_ab06-074.jpg
Notes:
65460016 This is our dinner-to-be last night. Starting with the limes on top and going in a counter-clockwise spiral, it includes: limes, bread, wine, abalone, Chanterelle canapÈs, lemon slices, lobster canapÈs, more wine, and lobster. This doesn't show the fine salad and the rice with local porcini (Boletus edulis) mushrooms!

We had a fabulous mostly-wild dinner last night with Matt Lum and family. Matt is a local freediver who took the lobster and abalone himself. It's a rich man's dinner that cost almost nothing. Thanks Matt! (Goleta, 6 November 2010)

File.Name: ab07b_ab06-087.jpg
Notes:
65459540 After Chanterelle canapÈs and abalone steaks, we had California spiny lobster (Panulirus interruptus) for the final course of our meal last night. I see why the local lobster divers call them "Bugs"! "May I have another piece of thorax please?"

We had a fabulous mostly-wild dinner last night with Matt Lum and family. Thanks Matt! (Goleta, 6 November 2010)

Matt took this lobster. He has some renown as a local freediver - diving in the ocean without scuba. See:
  http://sites.google.com/site/diversgame/home
(and search the page for "Matt Lum").

File.Name: ab07c_ab06-077.jpg
Notes:
65458949 Sizzling Abalone (Haliotis) steaks...

I have to admit, I hardly recognized the meat - Matt must have really pounded them, because they were so easy to eat. They are breaded in Panko breadcrumbs. I haven't eaten abalone for 30+ years!

We had a fabulous mostly-wild dinner last night with Matt Lum and family. Thanks Matt! (Goleta, 6 November 2010)

Matt took this abalone. He has some renown as a local freediver - diving in the ocean without scuba. See:
   http://www.celebrationsca.com/InfoGiantAbaloneSpillsOutOfItsHugeShell.htm

File.Name: ab07d_ab06-079.jpg
Notes:
65458459 Chanterelles transformed!

Our local Cantharellus cibarius mushrooms have been transformed into tasty canapÈs - marinated in balsamic vinegar and herbs. The secret is to dry sautÈ the mushrooms until they expel all their water, then transfer them to the marinade so they soak it all back up. Delicious!

We had a fabulous mostly-wild dinner last night with Matt Lum and family. Thanks Matt! (Goleta, 6 November 2010)

File.Name: ab08a_ab07-031.jpg
Notes:
65476836 Look very close to see the remarkable fine pattern on this mushroom cap - like little leaves!

I believe this is a Gemmed Puffball (Lycoperdon perlatum) mushroom growing under a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the woods. It has a well-developed sterile base - like a light bulb. It's about the size of a golf ball. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2010)
ScientificName: Lycoperdon perlatum) mushroom growing under a California Bay (Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Lycoperdon perlatum) mushroom growing under a California Bay (Umbellularia californica

File.Name: ab08b_ab07-006.jpg
Notes:
65476738 I'm still not sure whether these are the Big Laughing Mushroom (Gymnopilus spectabilis or kin) or the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox) mushrooms. Given the season, my money would be on the Gymnopilus species. But they are growing on wood chips left over from tree work a year ago, which suggests Agrocybe. Maybe they are edible, maybe not - I don't know what it is!

The mushroom cap is cracked because of the hot, dry weather last week. It's growing by a seedling Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2010)

We had a bit of rain last night - less than 1/4 inch, but it's enough to keep the mushrooms going.
CommonNames: Big Laughing Mushroom; Spring Agrocybe; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Gymnopilus spectabilis; Agrocybe praecox; Gymnopilus; Agrocybe; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Gymnopilus spectabilis; Agrocybe praecox; Gymnopilus; Agrocybe; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: ab08c_ab07-029.jpg
Notes:
65476503 I found this King Bolete (Boletus edulis) mushroom - aka Porcini, Steinpilz, Le Cep etc. - in the woods yesterday. It's a bit old, as the pores (not gills) are yellowish rather than white. It's growing in a clump of native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2010)

Of course Boletus edulis is edible - and delicious!

This is the other wild mushroom we had for our fabulous dinner - mixed with rice - on Saturday night. See my [Previous] photos. I found this mushroom on Sunday. Too late for the dinner - I guess we'll have to eat it ourselves!
CommonNames: King Bolete; Porcini; Steinpilz; Le Cep; Giant Rye Grass; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Boletus edulis; Leymus condensatus; Quercus agrifolia; Boletus edulis
associatedTaxa: Boletus edulis; Leymus condensatus; Quercus agrifolia; Boletus edulis

File.Name: ab09a_ab06-057.jpg
Notes:
65490538 This is the beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom growing in our woods. The caps are smooth and polished, and these are split from the dry weather last week. It has a ring on the stalk and a "death cup" volva at the base, but you can't see either in the photo. I'll post another photo tomorrow that shows both. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2010)

This is the wild mushroom you must learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous!

This photo looks sunny, but it's an illusion. My exposure was 2 seconds at f/22 at ISO 200. After chasing insects all summer, it's a treat to photograph subjects that stand still!

File.Name: ab09b_ab07-022.jpg
Notes:
65490288 When good mushrooms go bad...

This "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus has taken over an edible King Bolete (Boletus edulis) mushroom, like mold on a piece of bread. I broke the edge of the cap so that the bolete-type "tubes" are visible.

The infection starts as a moldy-looking growth, then turns bright yellow and powdery like this. It smells like a diaper pail. Needless to say, it's not edible even though the host mushroom is. It's associated with decay. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2010)

File.Name: ab09c_ab07-010.jpg
Notes: 65489988 This is a different puffball mushroom in the woods - several of them. It may be [i]Lycoperdon marginatum[/i] because of the way the outer layer of spines is starting to peel away in sheets - but there are many different kinds of puffballs. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2010)

File.Name: ab10a_ab07-007.jpg
Notes:
65506097 This cheerful little Lepiota mushroom in the woods along our driveway is growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. The Lepiota mushroom caps usually have a dark center that breaks into scales as it expands. With some practice, they are easy to recognize. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2010)

I have no idea what species this is - a good reason to avoid eating it. Some relatives are poisonous!

File.Name: ab10b_ab07-008.jpg
Notes:
65506001 I believe this handsome mushroom is part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group. The gills are white, but they develop sordid reddish-brown spots as they mature. It's quite large. It has a strong "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2010)

According to my books, these mushrooms are probably poisonous - not "drop down dead" poisonous, but "wish you were dead" stomach upset. I'm not tempted to nibble.

File.Name: ab10c_ab06-064.jpg
Notes:
65505946 This is looking under the skirts of the beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom in my [Previous] photo. You can see the distinctive "death cup" at the base of the stem, but most of the "ring" ended up on the margin of the cap. The real "ring" is close to the gills. The other circular features on the stem are just scars where the mushroom has split because of the dry weather. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2010)

This is the wild mushroom you must learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous! Learn the poisonous mushrooms first, then try everything else! But try them cautiously - just a nibble at first, then a bit more if it tastes ok.

You know the old saying, "There are old mushroom collectors and bold mushroom collectors - but no old, bold mushroom collectors!

File.Name: ab11a_ab11-059.jpg
Notes:
65524487 Here are my my after-school Hike Club kids playing on the fantastic sandstone rocks at Lizard's Mouth yesterday. It was late in the day, and the rocks are picking up some color from the low sun. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2010)

It's been a busy couple of days with Natural History society meeting, a lecture last night, and a David Grisman concert tonight. Hot Dawg!

File.Name: ab11b_ab11-043.jpg
Notes: 65524410 Shadows of me and my after-school Hike Club kids on the sandstone rocks at Lizard's Mouth up in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2010)

File.Name: ab11c_ab11-045.jpg
Notes:
65524335 People keep adding things to this memorial for Nicholas Samuel Markowitz who was murdered and buried near here in the mountains on August 8, 2000. It's a sad story, told in the movie "Alpha Dog". I don't know who carved the memorial, but they did a good job. I reckon it's good to keep the story of Nicholas' life and death known. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2010)

I got this photo near "Lizard's Mouth" - a popular site for rock climbing and hanging out away from authority in the mountains near home. I brought my after-school hike club kids here yesterday.

File.Name: ab12a_ab12-002.jpg
Notes:
65531328 I found this California Spreadwing (Archilestes californica or kin) dragonfly in the family Lestidae of the order Odonata this morning. This is a "tele-macro" shot with my long Tamron 200-500 mm lens, on our shaky upstairs deck. It's a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2010)

True dragonflies hold their wings horizontal at rest and true damselflies hold their wings vertical. These critters are in between.

No more time for photos. Julie and I are stepping out tonight for dinner and a concert with The David Grisman Quintet. Hot Dawg!

File.Name: ab13a_ab13-013.jpg
Notes:
65545557 This pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) is working a late-season native flower of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the composite Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2010)

The asters are still flowering on the north side - that is the south-facing side - of our canyon, but it's mostly shady here on the north-facing side where we live.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; California Aster; Sunflower
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: ab13b_ab13-008.jpg
Notes:
65545376 This is a little Pomace Fly in the family Drosophilidae, order Diptera perched on a leaf of a Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) plant in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the bridge over our creek. I reckon it's waiting for the Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) to appear. I am too! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2010)

See my photo from January 4, 2009 to see what the flies do to the Honey Mushrooms:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/45611894
ScientificName: Drosophilidae; Diptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Armillariella mellea
associatedTaxa: Drosophilidae; Diptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Armillariella mellea

File.Name: ab13c_ab13-030.jpg
Notes:
65544969 You might have to blink a few times to get this photo in focus!

These are the light-trails of the tiny gnats (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams after a rain. These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so. That's a young Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2010)

I've never seen these little flies that dance in the sunbeams in November before. It's early.

I set my camera to it's lowest ISO setting and took the photo at 0.3 sec @ f/22. You have to slow things down to get these action shots.

File.Name: ab14a_ab14-014.jpg
Notes:
65558835 This is the pretty gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see the fresh exit hole where the single adult gall wasp recently got away. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2010)

It occurs to me that these galls change color just like autumn leaves - they go from (sometimes) green to red to orange and finally to brown. Is the shrub breaking down the chlorophyll pigments and exposing other colors in the gall tissue? That twig in front looks chewed by a rodent - maybe a Dusky-Footed Wood Rat (Neotoma fuscipes)?
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Dusky-Footed Wood Rat
ScientificName: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Neotoma fuscipes
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Neotoma fuscipes

File.Name: ab14b_ab14-009.jpg
Notes:
65558202 Here is a Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) snuggling into a late-season flower of native composite Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family. (It might be S. virgata, but it's only about one foot tall.) (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2010)

This is a pretty little bee and a pretty little flower - the flower looks like it's wearing striped stockings on its styles!
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria exigua; Asteraceae; S. virgata
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria exigua; Asteraceae; S. virgata

File.Name: ab14c_ab13-016.jpg
Notes:
65557932 Here is a smoky-colored ("fuscous") little Bee Fly - family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera - working a late season composite flower of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2010)

Insects and flowers are both scarce as we slide into winter, but there are still a few of each in the woods.
ScientificName: fuscous; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: fuscous; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: ab15a_ab15-015.jpg
Notes:
65572912 Drinking from the cup of plenty!

These Honey Bees (Apis mellifera) are working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2010)

The shrubs started flowering early again this year. Last year, I found them even earlier, on November 7. In previous years I've typically found manzanita flowering in December and January.

The problem is that the hummingbirds depend on these flowers for a nectar boost in January. But if they too flower early, there's nothing for them. Is this what climate change looks like locally?

File.Name: ab15b_ab15-025.jpg
Notes:
65572217 ...and in with the new!

Here are new glandular flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. I found these flowering wild along West Camino Cielo today, so these flowers are not influenced by summer watering in our garden. I reckon they are the first flowers of the new rain year to appear in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2010)

File.Name: ab15c_ab14-029.jpg
Notes:
65571925 Out with the old...

These are among the last native flowers of the old rain year - California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium) in the Onagraceae plant family. I did no color correction on this photo. Well, I tried, but everything I did made it worse. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2010)

These are classic hummingbird flowers, but I suspect the hummers have moved on to the Manzanita flowers. See my [Next] two photo.

File.Name: ab16a_ab14-035.jpg
Notes:
65588110 This is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly with upper wings extended, just to show what the fuss is all about. The colors on the left side show the iridescence. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2010)

Alas, I captured it with my net and killing jar 30+ years ago. I believe this specimen dates back to 1978, but I can't make out the tags. I made a wood case with a glass top and gave it to my Mom and Dad for a Christmas present many years ago.

I found this specimen at my parent's house last week. The case is still sealed - which is why the specimen is in such good shape. It's a butterfly from the past...

"Over the mountains and through the woods, to grandmother's house we go..."

Hah, we're the grandparents now! We're heading up to the cabin in Oregon tomorrow. We'll have a week to get ready for kids and grandkids and Thanksgiving dinner. It will be cold with rain and snow, but I'm hoping for mushrooms to photograph. I'll be back in two weeks!

File.Name: ab16b_ab12-001.jpg
Notes: 65587642 Here is a pretty little Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) bird in one of the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees off our upstairs deck. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2010)

File.Name: ab16c_ab14-031.jpg
Notes: 65587522 Here is a pretty little Cobweb Spider aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae) in its tangled web on the Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in front of our house. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2010)
CommonNames: Cobweb Spider; Combfooted Spider; Greenbark Ceanothus

File.Name: ab16d_ab07-001.jpg
Notes: 65587384 These are little black-spored Inky Cap (Coprinus sp.) mushrooms in the woods. They are deliquescing - auto-digesting themselves into a mass of black ooze! (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2010)

File.Name: ab16e_ab07-018.jpg
Notes:
65587107 This is certainly an Amanita mushroom in the woods, but what kind? It has a yellowish cap with a striate margin, a membranous partial veil forming a ring on the stem, and a long slender stalk with a fraible, indistinct, white volva at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2010)

I guess it's one of the "hybrid swarm" between Amanita pantherina and Amanita gemmata. Both species grow nearby under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in a pristine part of our canyon that I call the Mushroom Woods. It's probably not edible, and may be quite poisonous.
Locality: Quercus agrifolia) trees in a pristine part of our canyon that I call the Mushroom Woods. It's probably not edible
ScientificName: Amanita; Amanita pantherina; Amanita gemmata; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Amanita pantherina; Amanita gemmata; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: ab16f_ab15-004.jpg
Notes:
65586690 I found several of these very large Russula mushrooms growing under a non-native Fir tree (Abies) by an old homestead in the mountains. I added my pocket knife for scale - it's about 4 inches long. Other mushrooms were at least one foot across! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2010)

The dark stains seem distinctive. Maybe this is the Blackening Russula (Russula albonigra) or kin? My mushroom book says "Better punted than hunted" - My mushroom book says "Better punted than hunted" - meaning don't eat it. The dry caps are not inviting.

File.Name: ab16g_ab15-019.jpg
Notes:
65586277 This seems to be the gall of a Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perdens) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2010)

It's interesting because it's growing on an unusual oak in the woods - "Shreve Oak" - which might be Quercus shrevei; or Q. parvula; or Q. parvula var. shrevei; or maybe Q. wislizenii var. frutescens, or even Q. wislizenii! Either way, it's growing on a local oak in the Fagaceae family.
ScientificName: Callirhytis perdens; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus shrevei; Q. parvula; Q. parvula; shrevei; Q. wislizenii; frutescens; Q. wislizenii; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis perdens; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus shrevei; Q. parvula; Q. parvula; shrevei; Q. wislizenii; frutescens; Q. wislizenii; Fagaceae

File.Name: ab16h_ab14-020.jpg
Notes:
65585736 Interesting "rosette gall" on a stem of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see the exit holes where the tiny creatures inside got away.

I don't believe it's listed in Ron Russo's fine book "Field Guide to Plant Galls of California and Other Western States". I don't know what critter made it. I don't believe I've noticed it before. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2010)

File.Name: ab30a_ab26-039.jpg
Notes:
65778335 Here is Julie preparing our freshly harvested mushroom bounty for our Thanksgiving table in Oregon last week...

Clockwise from the lower left, here are Lobster Mushrooms (Hypomyces lacifluorum), White Chanterelles (Cantherellus subalbidus), Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes), and two piles of "ordinary" Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius). The mushrooms were a feast by themselves, but we also had local Umpqua lamb for dinner. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2010)

We had a real Thanksgiving dinner with all our family - our kids and grandkids and Julie's sister all met at "K". I'll post a photo of us all tomorrow.
CommonNames: Lobster Mushrooms; White Chanterelles; Black Chanterelles; Horn of Plenty; Chanterelles
ScientificName: Hypomyces lacifluorum; Cantherellus subalbidus; Craterellus cornucopiodes; Cantharellus cibarius
associatedTaxa: Hypomyces lacifluorum; Cantherellus subalbidus; Craterellus cornucopiodes; Cantharellus cibarius

File.Name: ab30b_ab19-030.jpg
Notes: 65778263 These Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes) mushrooms were my favorite at our Thanksgiving dinner. The flesh is thin and tough, yet the flavor is superb and they keep some texture through cooking. They are hard to find in the woods because they look so much like dried leaves or a hole. We don't have these at home. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 November 2010)

File.Name: ab30c_ab20-011.jpg
Notes: 65778190 I found a few of these delicious White Chanterelle (Cantherellus subalbidus) mushrooms in the woods in Oregon. I thought they were better on our Thanksgiving table than the ordinary Chanterelles - thicker and more buttery. We had local Umpqua lamb for dinner, and added these mushrooms to the gravy and stuffing. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2010)

File.Name: ab30d_ab21-033.jpg
Notes: 65778039 There were good ol' Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms in the Oregon woods for Thanksgiving! We had some snow, but even after being frozen they were a delicious part of our Thanksgiving dinner. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)

File.Name: ab30e_ab19-027.jpg
Notes:
65777992 It seems strange, but these Lobster Mushrooms (Hypomyces lacifluorum) are really delicious - at least fresh off the grill. (I didn't care for them after they cooled down.) Strange, because it's a parasitic fungus that engulfs and changes the acrid wild Lactarius and Russula mushrooms in the woods. True to their name, they have a slightly fishy taste, like crab cakes. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 November 2010)

This specimen was a bit wormy - we didn't collect it for our Thanksgiving dinner table.

File.Name: ab30f_ab26-020.jpg
Notes: 65777779 This is our full mushroom basket for Thanksgiving in Oregon - which we celebrated last Friday. Those are White Chanterelles (Cantherellus subalbidus) on top, with more good stuff down deeper. See my [Next] photos for more info. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2010)

File.Name: ac01a_ab19-012.jpg
Notes:
65791912 I believe this is the Apricot Jelly Mushroom (Phlogiotis helvelloides). What a beautiful mushroom this jelly fungus is! It's said to be highly prized by some people, but more for its color and texture than its non-existent taste. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 November 2010)

Today's photos are some of the strange mushrooms I found near our cabin in Oregon last week. More tomorrow!

File.Name: ac01b_ab21-017.jpg
Notes: 65791855 I believe this strange mushroom is the White Coral Mushroom (Ramariopsis kunzei) - but cp. the Crested Coral (Clavulina crestata). It's said to be "harmless, fleshless, flavorless". I found it growing under mixed woods of conifers and Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana). (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)
CommonNames: White Coral Mushroom; Crested Coral; Oregon White Oak
ScientificName: Ramariopsis kunzei) - but cp. the Crested Coral (Clavulina crestata; Quercus garryana
associatedTaxa: Ramariopsis kunzei) - but cp. the Crested Coral (Clavulina crestata; Quercus garryana

File.Name: ac01c_ab21-002.jpg
Notes: 65791709 This is a strange Coral Mushroom (genus Ramaria) growing under an unusual Sugar Pine (Pinus lambertiana) in the Oregon woods. I don't know what kind of coral mushroom it is. It looks like it belongs under the ocean! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)

File.Name: ac01d_ab19-049.jpg
Notes: 65791634 This is a strange rubbery Toothed Jelly Fungus (Psuedohydnum gelatinosum) in the Oregon woods - with a little spider walking along the edge of the cap. The latin name means "false tooth fungus" - I'll show some real tooth fungi in a day or two. They are said to be edible with no taste but great texture. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 November 2010)

File.Name: ac01e_ab19-062.jpg
Notes: 65791517 These are strange Truncate Club Coral (Clavariadelphus truncatus) mushrooms growing under conifers in the Oregon woods. They are said to be delicious and sweet! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 November 2010)

File.Name: ac01f_ab27-021.jpg
Notes:
65791421 Our Thanksgiving dinner was a family event. Here's the crowd. From right to left, here are me and Julie, our son Ryan, Julie's sister Laurie with Fred the dog, and her partner Nick, and our other son Ewan and Roz holding the twins. The backdrop is our elegant cabin in the Oregon woods. That's three generations of us Kummels. Thanks Dad. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2010)

I got this photo using the self-timer so I could be in there too.

File.Name: ac02a_ab27-056.jpg
Notes:
65805434 These are strange Bird's Nest Fungi (Nidulariales) growing on a twig in the Oregon woods. The tiny mushrooms look like little birds' nests complete with eggs! The eggs are actually peridioles that hold spores. The force of a single rain drop in the "splash cup" is enough to distribute the eggs. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2010)

Today's photos are more of the strange and wonderful mushrooms I found in Oregon last week. I'll have more tomorrow!

File.Name: ac02b_ab21-015.jpg
Notes: 65805309 I'm not sure what these strange slender mushrooms are - maybe Clavariadelphus ligula or kin? The simple stalks are only a few mm thick. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)

File.Name: ac02c_ab21-014.jpg
Notes: 65805245 I believe these pure white graceful mushrooms are Fairy Fingers (Clavaria vermicularis). They have unbranched stems that are very brittle. They look a bit like the mushroom in my [Previous] photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)

File.Name: ac02d_ab21-021.jpg
Notes:
65805209 I'm not sure what kind of strange Coral Mushroom this is - maybe the White Coral Mushroom (Ramariopsis kunzei)? The stems are brittle. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)

This is probably the same mushroom I posted yesterday:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65791855

File.Name: ac02e_ab27-040.jpg
Notes:
65805161 Here is another strange Coral Mushroom (genus Ramaria) growing in the Oregon woods - maybe it's the Pink-tipped Coral Mushroom (Ramaria botrytis) or kin? Maybe an Elk stepped on this specimen? It's about the size of a cauliflower. I don't think any of the large coral mushrooms are dangerously poisonous, but it seems wrong to eat anything so remarkable! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2010)

As I found them, I thought this was a different from the coral I posted yesterday. That one was more yellow. Now I'm not sure.

File.Name: ac02f_ab19-022.jpg
Notes:
65805050 I'm not sure what this strange mushroom is, but there were lots of them in the Oregon woods. They appear to be Cup Fungi (Pezizaceae or kin). "Pig's Ears" would be a good name for it, but it's taken - I don't believe this is Discina perlata. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 November 2010)

A good thing about photographing mushrooms away from home is that I don't have to know what they are!

File.Name: ac03a_ab27-048.jpg
Notes:
65817626 I believe these attractive mushrooms are Cystoderma fallax or kin. I don't see a layer of "mealy granules" on the caps, but maybe the rain and snow washed them away? What a remarkable flaring "ring" on the stalk - it looks like they are wearing warm stockings for the cold! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2010)

I have "a few" more mushroom photos from our Oregon trip - I photographed 65 different kinds of mushrooms in our few acres of the Oregon woods. Remarkable diversity!

File.Name: ac03b_ab19-067.jpg
Notes: 65817352 These are pretty Shaggy-Stalked Parasol (Lepiota clypeolaria or kin) mushrooms growing under conifers (and Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Oregon woods last week. Lepiota mushroom caps usually have a dark center that breaks into scales as it expands, making them easy to recognize. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 November 2010)

File.Name: ac03c_ab20-029.jpg
Notes: 65817162 These are beautiful Cortinarius mushrooms coming up in the Oregon woods. You can see the spiderweb-like "cortina" veil of hairs that once covered and protected the immature gills. I guess this is in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap and rimmed basal bulb. It's probably not edible, but sure is handsome! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2010)

File.Name: ac03d_ab27-034.jpg
Notes: 65817022 These mushroom look ordinary enough from above, but look under the cap. Instead of gills, they have a layer of spines or "teeth" to hold their spores. I believe this is a Hedgehog Mushroom (Hydnum repandum) or kin. They are said to be delicious - better than chanterelles! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2010)

File.Name: ac03e_ab27-033.jpg
Notes:
65816883 I believe this interesting fungus is a Gemmed Puffball (Lycoperdon perlatum) mushroom growing under conifers in the Oregon woods. They develop a pore at the top to release spores. The dark spore mass inside looks like the inside of a vacuum cleaner bag! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2010)

We might have this same mushroom at home, or something similar. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65476836

File.Name: ac03f_ab20-016.jpg
Notes:
65816686 These pretty "varnished conk" mushrooms might well be the Oregon Conk (Ganoderma oregonense). I find them mostly growing low on small conifer trees that have been shaded out in the woods. The trees often break just above the fungus. (You can see a scar just above this fungus.) (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2010)

It's closely related to Ganoderma lucidum - Reishi or Ling Zhi, a fungus used in oriental medicine. The Oregon species may have the similar medicinal properties. Sources on the internet sell it.

File.Name: ac04a_ab21-011.jpg
Notes:
65829912 Here are beautiful British Soldier Lichens (Cladonia cristatella or kin) in the Oregon woods arising from a bed of moss. The little red "caps" resemble the red hats once worn by British army troops. I believe that the red globes at the top of the lichens are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)

Today's photos are all interesting lichens from our recent visit to the Oregon woods. Enough mushrooms!

I took my usual Saturday hike in the canyon today and got photos of ... more mushrooms! *Sigh*

File.Name: ac04b_ab21-010.jpg
Notes: 65829679 This is the Devil's Matchstick (Pilophorus acicularis or kin) lichen growing on a rock in the Oregon woods. I believe that the black "match heads" are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)

File.Name: ac04c_ab21-006.jpg
Notes: 65829363 Here is a forest of little Pyxie Cup Lichens in the Oregon woods. I have know idea what they are - maybe Cladonia chlorophaea or Cladonia pyxidata? What a pretty little world this is! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)

File.Name: ac04d_ab25-020.jpg
Notes: 65829142 Here is a pretty little Cladonia-type lichen in the Oregon woods. I don't know what kind it is. What a pretty little forest it makes with the moss! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2010)

File.Name: ac04e_ab20-037.jpg
Notes: 65829048 Lichens are dual-organisms - part fungus and part algae - but sometimes the fungal part tries to reproduce on it's own. I believe this is the fungal part of an Oregon Lung Lobaria (Lobaria oregana) lichen that fell to the ground trying to produce its ascospores alone, like a little cup fungus. Notice how white the lichen is. The lichen will not reproduce alone unless the fungal spores are disseminated together with the right algae. Apparently the fungal spores don't germinate by themselves, so it's a wasted effort. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2010)

File.Name: ac04f_ab25-016.jpg
Notes: 65828833 This is the Oregon Lung Lobaria (Lobaria oregana) lichen - with snow - hanging on a branch of Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana) as an epiphyte. It looks like monster skin! It's a common foliose-type lichen in the Oregon woods. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2010)
CommonNames: Oregon Lung Lobaria; Oregon White Oak

File.Name: ac05a_ab20-043.jpg
Notes:
65841749 I believe this beautiful creature is the Red Waxy Cap (Hygrocybe coccinea) mushroom. It's either that or the Scarlet Waxy Cap (H. punicea). Either way, it sure is pretty! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2010)

We're getting some real rain tonight! It's just what we need to keep the mushrooms going at home. I figure I need one more day to finish the Oregon photos - and that's leaving out half of the 65 different kinds of mushrooms I photographed in our few acres of the Oregon woods!
CommonNames: Red Waxy Cap; Scarlet Waxy Cap

File.Name: ac05b_ab21-019.jpg
Notes:
65841520 I'm guessing, but maybe these pretty mushrooms in the Oregon woods are the Woolly Pine Spike (Chroogomphus tomentosus)? They are not woolly at all, but everything else fits. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)

A good thing about photographing mushrooms away from home is that I don't have to know what they are!

File.Name: ac05c_ab27-059.jpg
Notes:
65841138 I don't know what this mushroom is, but I'll guess it's a Cortinarius in the subgenus Myxacium as both cap and stem are slimy. The inside yellow stem seems distinctive, but I can't find it in my books. It seems to have a "slime-veil" connecting the cap and stem. You can see many Springtail (order Collembia) insects on the stem of the mushroom in front. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2010)

I found this mushroom growing in the old scattered skeleton of an elk in the woods.

File.Name: ac05d_ab23-033.jpg
Notes: 65840820 Here is a handsome - and delicious - White Chanterelle (Cantherellus subalbidus) mushroom in the Oregon woods after it snowed on us. It's frozen - like the lettuce when the fridge is set too cold - but it thawed just fine for our Thanksgiving dinner! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2010)

File.Name: ac05e_ab25-029.jpg
Notes: 65840670 These Lobster Mushrooms (Hypomyces lacifluorum) are really delicious - at least fresh off the grill. I got this photo after it snowed - the mushroom is frozen! It's a parasitic fungus that engulfs and changes the acrid wild Lactarius and Russula mushrooms in the woods into something delicious. True to their name, they have a slightly fishy taste, like crab cakes. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2010)

File.Name: ac05f_ab24-002.jpg
Notes:
65840549 Here is my usual mushroom trail in the Oregon woods the day after it snowed on us two weeks ago. Yes, I did go for a mushroom walk that day! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2010)

It's a remarkably diverse forest - Douglas Fir, Western Hemlock, Grand Fir, Incense Cedar, Western Red Cedar, Sugar Pine, Pacific Yew, Bigleaf Maple, Madrone, Oregon Ash, Cottonwoods, and Willow - and that's just the trees!

File.Name: ac06a_ab20-024.jpg
Notes:
65855467 I don't know what this yellow mushroom is that I found in the Oregon woods - but it sure is pretty! It almost looks like an Amanita with that ring and (maybe) free gills. I'm glad there are still surprises in the woods! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2010)

This is the end of my Oregon mushroom photos. I got lots more - I took photos of 65 different fungi and only posted about half of them! But the mushroom in the canyon at home need attention too!

File.Name: ac06b_ab27-037.jpg
Notes: 65855365 Here's a pretty group of "LBMs" - Little Brown Mushrooms - growing from a mossy bed on a fallen conifer in the Oregon woods. I don't know what they are (or much care), but I guess the dark-spored genus Psathyrella. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2010)

File.Name: ac06c_ab21-024.jpg
Notes: 65855320 Here's a clutch of delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms in the Oregon woods. Some of these became part of our Thanksgiving dinner! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)

File.Name: ac06d_ab21-026.jpg
Notes: 65855279 Here are beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus) mushrooms in the Oregon woods - or maybe Chocolate Marasmius (M. fuscopurpureus or kin) mushrooms? I don't really care - they are beautiful! We have these at home too. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2010)

File.Name: ac06e_ab19-059.jpg
Notes: 65855232 These are the seed capsules of Indian Pipes (Monotropa uniflora) in the Orchidaceae plant family. It's a summer-flowering orchid in the Oregon woods. It's not a mushroom, but it only grows with fungal partners. The flower stems are pure white, but they develop these rich chocolate brown colors as they mature. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 November 2010)

File.Name: ac06f_ab27-049.jpg
Notes: 65854507 I'm not sure what this pretty mushroom is that I found growing in the Oregon woods. I guess it's another Hygrophorus - maybe Hygrophorus piceae? It looks like the Ivory Waxy Cap (H. eburneus), but it's not slimy at all. This mushroom is growing under conifers, but not spruce (genus Picea). (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2010)
ScientificName: Hygrophorus; Hygrophorus piceae; H. eburneus; Picea
associatedTaxa: Hygrophorus; Hygrophorus piceae; H. eburneus; Picea

File.Name: ac07a_ac04-042.jpg
Notes:
65867822 This clump of beautiful Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) is growing from the still-sprouting stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods by our house. The mushrooms are edible and tasty if you get them before the worms do. They get their common name from the color, not the taste. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2010)

My photos today are mushrooms from home, not from Oregon. I know, *more mushrooms* - but if you look close, you can see oak leaves under them instead of conifer needles. It's the time of year when the fungi rule the woods!

It's interesting to compare the Oregon mushrooms with our mushrooms in the Santa Barbara mountains. Oregon has more "strange 'shrooms", but we have more amanitas and boletes and "classic mushrooms" at home. Some mushrooms like Chanterelles are happy in both places.

File.Name: ac07b_ac04-024.jpg
Notes:
65867445 These are the beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushrooms growing in our woods. The caps are smooth and polished, with a greenish tinge. They have a ring on the stalk and a "death cup" volva at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2010)

This is one of the wild mushrooms you must learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous!

File.Name: ac07c_ac04-021.jpg
Notes:
65867205 Here are new Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2010)

I can't imagine why anyone would confuse them with delicious chanterelle mushrooms, but I suppose anything is possible. DON'T confuse them with chanterelles!

Both mushrooms are orange and sort of trumpet-shaped, but these are quite poisonous, causing intense sweating and stomach issues. The stems are tough and have a greenish tinge, and they always grow from wood, sometimes buried. But I've been fooled before - just for a moment!

Go on a mushroom walk with someone who knows. You won't confuse them after you've seen them both. Some varieties are said to glow in the dark, but I've never seen that here.

File.Name: ac08a_ac04-036.jpg
Notes:
65881881 The leaves stuck to the cap show that the cap is viscid or slimy when wet, and the intensely yellow pores that don't turn blue when handled show that this impressive mushroom is Boletus flaviporus. Like all Boletes, it has pores instead of gills below the cap. It's a west coast endemic. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2010)

My book says that "the stem is not truly reticulate..., but the tubes may form a slight raised network at the stalk apex" - which is a perfect description of this mushroom. My book further says it is "edible, but mediocre." I haven't tried it for the table.

File.Name: ac08b_ac05-014.jpg
Notes:
65881738 Honey Mushrooms and Jack-O-Lanterns aren't the only local mushrooms that grow in clusters!

These are a bunch of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms growing on the stump of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. They are framed by still-green Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens). The caps looked more yellow in person, but I guess the long exposure in dim light made them look brown. They are bitter-tasting and poisonous. Don't eat them. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2010)

Heh - there's a little Pomace Fly in the family Drosophilidae, order Diptera, perched on the next-to-highest cap to the right of center.
CommonNames: Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Coast Live Oak; Western Bracken Fern; Pomace Fly
ScientificName: Naematoloma fasciculare; Quercus agrifolia; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Drosophilidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Naematoloma fasciculare; Quercus agrifolia; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Drosophilidae; Diptera

File.Name: ac08c_ac05-018.jpg
Notes:
65881677 This is another stately cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from a hidden tree stump in the woods. The stump is completely obscured by the mushrooms. Judging from the leaves on the ground, it might be a native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2010)

These mushrooms are edible and tasty if you get them before the worms do. They get their common name from the color, not the taste. See my [Previous] photo for a closer shot.

File.Name: ac09a_ac04-031.jpg
Notes:
65893423 I hoped this might be the yellow form of Amanita muscaria when I found them along the road, but the volva - the cup at the base of the stem - is wrong. I'm pretty sure they are the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) mushroom. It's a large, satisfying mushroom. I usually find them under Madrone trees (Arbutus menziesii) in the Ericaceae plant family. There were madrones about 20 feet up the road embankment, and I reckon roots grow that far. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2010)

These mushrooms are pretty, but probably poisonous. They form "hybrids" with the definitely poisonous A. pantherina mushrooms in our woods. Don't eat them!
ScientificName: Amanita muscaria; Amanita gemmata; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; A. pantherina
associatedTaxa: Amanita muscaria; Amanita gemmata; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; A. pantherina

File.Name: ac09b_ac04-033.jpg
Notes:
65893044 I believe this is the Variegated Russula (Russula cyanoxantha) which is said to be edible and sweet - but maybe it's some other Russula that is spicy hot and poisonous if you eat more than a nibble! It's growing just up the hillside at home, so maybe I'll try it this weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2010)

I'm very conservative with the mushrooms I collect for the table. Chanterelles of course, honey mushrooms, an occasional agaricus, and a few special boletes are about it. But I sure do enjoy finding and photographing and studying them!

File.Name: ac09c_ac04-003.jpg
Notes:
65892742 Oregon has no monopoly on chanterelles!

I found these are delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms closer to home - along our driveway. They are a bit dried out from the dry weather, but that just concentrates the flavor. The mushrooms are remarkably resistant to just about all pests - insects, worms, and decay seem to have no effect on them. That must be one reason why they are local favorites. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2010)

It's interesting that these are one of the few mushrooms I found in Oregon that I also find at home.

File.Name: ac10a_ac04-001.jpg
Notes:
65911206 Here is a beautiful Agaricus mushroom in the woods along our driveway. The thick felt-like partial veil that protects the immature gills is just about done tearing free from the edge of the cap to form a skirt-like ring on the stalk. It looks delicious, but I suspect it is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom which is poisonous to many people. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2010)

I'm posting late because we had a staff pizza party for my school last night. The menu featured delicious handmade pizzas topped with fresh chanterelle mushrooms that we provided!

File.Name: ac10b_ac08- 004.jpg
Notes: 65911010 Here are two-colored Shaggy Mane (Coprinus comatus) mushrooms growing on the grass at school. The mushrooms start white with a brown hat, but they turn black as they "deliquesce" - literally digest themselves into slime! They are edible and delicious - if prepared fresh. (Los Olivos, Santa Ynez Valley, 8 December 2010)

File.Name: ac10c_ac04-023.jpg
Notes: 65910785 These handsome mushrooms are growing in the gravel along the road in our canyon. You can see the orange spore-stained hairs on the stem that mark this species as a Cortinarius. The rocks stuck to the cap are a sure sign that the cap is viscid when moist. That and the abrupt rimmed base of the stalk place it in in the subgenus Bulbopodium. There are lots of species. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2010)

File.Name: ac11a_ac10-056.jpg
Notes:
65917642 Pizza in the oven!

It's not just any oven. This is Linda and Doug's outdoor wood-fired stone pizza oven. They hosted our staff pizza party for my school last night. The little pizzas were fabulous! (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 December 2010)

I got this photo hand held, at 1/5 second and ISO 1600.

Sorry I didn't get any photos of the finished pizzas. I was too busy eating!

File.Name: ac11b_ac10-017.jpg
Notes: 65917544 Eric tosses the perfect pizza at our staff pizza party for my school last night. The little chef figurine on the table seems to agree! (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 December 2010)

File.Name: ac11c_ac10-029.jpg
Notes:
65917406 Yummy Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms - with butter and garlic! Julie prepared these fungi at our staff pizza party for my school last night to put on our pizzas. Of course, they are wild mushrooms from the woods that we brought from home. (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 December 2010)

We usually "dry sautÈ" these fine mushrooms to concentrate the flavor. Clean them with a knife instead of water - they're like sponges. Then slice them into a hot dry pan, and let them stew in their own juices to concentrate the flavor until almost dry. Add a bit of salt and fresh pepper and garlic and a splash of olive oil. Delicious!

Hah - I recognize that wooden spoon! I'm sure it was made in my Tools & Materials class at DMS. Was it Carly?

File.Name: ac12a_ac12-031.jpg
Notes:
65928698 Here are new flowers of a native male Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Silk-Tassel (Garryaceae) plant family. The strange flowers hang like pearls on a thread. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2010)

This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. The flowers of this particular shrub will only produce pollen.

It was unseasonably warm today, but there is rain in the forecast sometime after Wednesday. We've had 7-1/4 inch of rain so far this rain-year. It's a bit over normal, but I'm worried about what's coming (or not coming) in this maybe dry La NiÒa year. I want more mushroom weather!

File.Name: ac12b_ac12-019.jpg
Notes:
65928293 This isn't a great photo, but it's a interesting moment in our mountains that I want to record. Here is the first new butterfly that I've seen this year. It was perched high up in a Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia) shrub, holding its wings like a sail to catch the sun. I had to hold my tripod up in the air to get the photo at ISO 800, and it's a tight crop.

It was worth it. This is a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly. My book says that it is "One of the first non-hibernating butterflies to fly in the spring." Darn, it's not even officially winter yet! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2010)

Is this what climate change looks like locally?

File.Name: ac12c_ac11-040.jpg
Notes:
65927974 There are still mushrooms in the woods, though this warm weather is turning against them.

These are Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus) mushrooms. Unlike most of their kind, these mushrooms seem to have no latex when you cut the gills. But look around them. Here is a micro-world of tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) and Mycena mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2010)

I don't expect the micro-world to last past tomorrow if the weather stays this warm. It got to 70∫ on our shady side of the mountains, and at least 10∫ warmer on the sunny side. Pleasant - but it's not right for December!

File.Name: ac13a_ac11-056.jpg
Notes:
65942592 These are beautiful Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. This is the largest cluster I've see so far this year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2010)

DON'T confuse them with chanterelles! Both mushrooms are orange and sort of trumpet-shaped, but these are quite poisonous, causing intense sweating and stomach issues. The stems are tough and have a greenish tinge, and they always grow from wood, sometimes buried. Some varieties are said to glow in the dark, but I've never seen that here.

The green leaves in the lower-right are native Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) in the Ericaceae plant family. It really belongs in Oregon, but it survives here on the pass in a remnant population with the mushrooms. The birds usually beat me to the berries.
Locality: Vaccinium ovatum) in the Ericaceae plant family. It really belongs in Oregon
CommonNames: Jack-O-Lantern; Coast Live Oak; Wild Huckleberry
ScientificName: Omphalotus olivascens; Quercus agrifolia; Vaccinium ovatum; Ericaceae

File.Name: ac13b_ac11-054.jpg
Notes:
65942482 This is a Conk fungus - genus Ganoderma or kin. It's not really a mushroom as it's not at all fleshy, but it works just like a mushroom. Look at all the rusty spores it has dropped - they cover the leaves below and the cap above! It's a surprise to see these rusty spores that came from that pure white hymenium. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2010)

I don't know which kind of conk it is. It's growing from buried wood - maybe Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia), but Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) are also possible. All three leaves are on the ground.
CommonNames: Conk; Coast Live Oak; Tanbark Oak; Madrone

File.Name: ac13c_ac12-035.jpg
Notes:
65942062 At first glance, these look like tiny mushrooms growing on a bit of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) wood in the forest, with a leaf for scale - but I don't think so. I'm pretty sure they are one of nature's most extraordinary creatures - Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria). They might be my favorite organisms, though I don't find them very often. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2010)

Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part animal. They are Mycetozoa - which literally means "fungus-animals". They are really animals, not fungi.

Mycetozoa live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying wood. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes. They end up as these tiny little mushroom-like structures that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another.

File.Name: ac14a_ac12-010.jpg
Notes: 65955563 This photo shows the shredding bark of a native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I shot it "Contre-jour" - into the sun, with backlighting. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2010)

File.Name: ac14b_ac12-013.jpg
Notes: 65955376 These flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family look fully-formed with holiday colors, but they will wait for another month or two until they open for business next spring. It's an interesting strategy. Several native plants form their buds early, but most don't. I wonder if there's an advantage, or is this just how it's always been done? (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2010)

File.Name: ac14c_ac12-004.jpg
Notes:
65955027 These are brilliant red berries of Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. They are having a pretty good year - which is also good for the birds that depend on them! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2010)

I happen to have a stumper about these berries in the school newsnote this week:

Marc's stumper for the week: Drive in the country, and you will notice the brilliant ripe Christmas Berrry shrubs - aka Toyon or California Holly, the namesake of Hollywood. The cooked red fruit were a Chumash staple, though many Web sites list them as poisonous. I understand why foliage and seeds might be toxic - to protect the plant. But plants use extra resources to grow succulent fruit and berries. Surely the point is to attract critters (like us!) to eat them and spread the seeds. That's the stumper. Are there really poisonous fruit and berries? What's the advantage for the plants?

(I'll give my answer tomorrow!)
ScientificName: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Christmas Berrry; Toyon; California Holly
associatedTaxa: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Christmas Berrry; Toyon; California Holly

File.Name: ac15a_ac12-033.jpg
Notes:
65968779 Here is a new cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) coming up on an old stump along our driveway - where they always appear at this time of year. These young mushrooms look quite gray. They are just starting to show the honey colors that they will develop in a few days. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2010)

Look on the tallest mushroom in the lower tier, just to the right of center - there's a little Pomace Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera, thinking about laying her eggs that will become the larvae that turn these mushrooms into mush!

File.Name: ac15b_ac11-008.jpg
Notes: 65968499 These are tasty Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms in the woods! Be careful though - the mushroom caps start blue, but they soon fade to gray or tan like these. Yet they always have a "blewitt aura" that is recognizable with experience. We also have an inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that looks very similar - see my [Previous] photo. Caveat emptor! (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2010)

File.Name: ac15c_ac11-027.jpg
Notes:
65968371 Be careful when hunting for edible Blewitt mushrooms in the woods -see my [Next] photo - because these non-edible Cortinarius mushrooms look very similar! The young caps of both are blue, and so are the gills, but the gills of all Cortinarius species turn reddish brown as they mature. If in doubt, don't eat it! (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2010)

I think these might be part of the Cortinarius cylindripes group. Both cap and stem are very slimy when fresh, but they change in dry weather like we've been having.

File.Name: ac16a_ac11-033.jpg
Notes:
65981815 This is a "JAR" - "Just Another Russula" - as my mushroom book puts it. I guess from the rosy colors of its cap that these mushrooms are in the "Russula maculata" group. Sounds good, but that's really not saying much ID-wise. They have a mild taste, unlike some of their cousins that taste peppery-hot. They are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2010)

Russulas are easy to recognize - the stems snap like a piece of chalk as there is no fibrous tissue present like most mushrooms. There are many different kinds.

Oh boy, it feels like real rain coming in tonight. Quite a change from the balmy weather last weekend, and just in time for Christmas vacation from school!

File.Name: ac16b_ac04-027.jpg
Notes:
65981664 I guess these "little brown mushrooms" in the woods are the genus Inocybe - maybe the Corn Silk Mushroom (Inocybe sororia) or kin. I didn't notice any pungent smell like freshly husked corn though. It's probably quite poisonous, with a high concentration of the toxin muscarine. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2010)

These grow along the road in our canyon. They make relatively large "mushrumps" (mounds of leaves) for their small size.

File.Name: ac16c_ac05-011.jpg
Notes:
65981535 I guess these are more Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) coming up on a stump of Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. But they sure look different from the mushrooms in my [Previous] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2010)

These mushrooms are sprouting in an exposed place, and they are sun-cracked by the warm weather last weekend.

My book says that "it is among the most variable and cosmopolitan of the fleshy fungi, and in its innumerable guises will confound you time and time again." That's just the way it is when trying to identify mushrooms!

File.Name: ac17a_ac16-013.jpg
Notes:
65992845 Here are my after-school Hike Club girls posing with handfuls of yummy Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms that we collected in the woods yesterday. Then we went to our house in the woods to cook them up! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2010)

We're having a rain day! We've had about an inch so far - with lots more rain expected over the next week. It's a great way to begin a school vacation.

File.Name: ac17b_ac16-024.jpg
Notes:
65992528 These are yummy Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms stewing in their own juices. It's amazing how much juice these mushrooms create in a dry frying pan on the stove!

This is the mushroom haul by my after-school Hike Club kids yesterday. We hunted for mushrooms and then went to our house in the woods and cooked and ate them. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2010)

We usually "dry sautÈ" these fine mushrooms to concentrate the flavor. Clean them with a knife instead of water - they're like sponges. Then slice them into a hot dry pan, and let them stew in their own juices as shown to concentrate the flavor until almost dry. Add a bit of fresh pepper and garlic and a splash of olive oil. Delicious!

File.Name: ac17c_ac11-044.jpg
Notes: 65992383 This is part of a larger cluster of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms growing on the stump of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. Look in the upper-right. There is a little Pomace Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera, thinking about laying her eggs. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2010)

Notes:
66003375 Here are brilliant new flowers of a native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. Leaves and flowers are both very glandular. These flowers of part of the new rain year, not the old. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2010)

I got this photo last weekend when it was sunny and warm. Today, it's been rain rain rain all day without a break. We have 3+ inches (8+ cm) in our rain gauge so far, and the heaviest rain is expected tonight and tomorrow. Not bad for an expected-dry "La NiÒa" year!
Locality: 8+ cm) in our rain gauge so far

File.Name: ac18b_ac12-023.jpg
Notes: 66003222 These might be the very last flower heads of autumn-flowering native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains. I think the flowers have out-lived the insects this year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2010)

File.Name: ac18c_ac12-021.jpg
Notes:
66003035 Here are close to the last flowers of native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Asteraceae plant family. For some reason, I rarely find insects on these flowers - even when they are almost the very last flowers of last year's growing season. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2010)

The plant gets its name from its way of following roads and trails. Though often considered a weed, it is a native species that competes very well with naturalized weeds in disturbed areas.

File.Name: ac19a_ac19-014.jpg
Notes:
66014923 This is the creek that flows by Cold Springs Tavern, swollen with our rain this weekend. The trees in the creek are deciduous White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia). They've stood up through worse, but maybe it will come to that! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2010)

It's been raining for two days without stop. We've had 10 inches (25+ cm) of rain from this storm so far, and it's still raining. The forecast is that we might get another 8-15 inches before the storms are done!

The mushrooms and banana slugs are loving it!
Locality: 25+ cm) of rain from this storm so far

File.Name: ac19b_ac19-003.jpg
Notes: 66014547 Here are multiple clusters of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) coming up from the stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the rain today. They don't look soggy at all! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2010)

File.Name: ac19c_ac19-WCIR103531730.jpg
Notes:
66014238 We've had more rain than I ever hoped for in an expected-dry "La NiÒa" year, and here's why.

This is a NOAA Satellite image from 9:30 this morning. It show the "pineapple express" of storm clouds that extend out to Hawaii and beyond. Note the little storm off Oregon/Washington that is driving these storms towards us.

The heaviest rain is in that little plume to the south, and it's coming right at Santa Barbara from the south. This creates "orographic enhancement" as the clouds meet our east-west mountain barrier. San Marcos Pass usually leads the local rain totals!

It hasn't stopped raining all day. We've had 10 inches (25+ cm) of rain from this storm so far, and it's still raining. The forecast is that we might get another 8-15 inches before the storms are done! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2010)

File.Name: ac20a_ac20-011.jpg
Notes:
66028146 These are soggy Agaricus mushrooms in the woods along our driveway today. I don't know what kind they are - in this condition, who can tell? (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2010)

The rain slowed to a drizzle this afternoon, so I took a walk with my camera and a plastic trash bag to protect it. I didn't really expect to find new mushrooms in the middle of a rain storm. Mushrooms are creatures of the rain, but they don't usually fruit while it's raining because it's impossible to disperse their spores.

We're having highly unusual weather - especially in an expected-dry "La NiÒa" year! We've had close to 12 inches (30 cm) of rain so far from our "pineapple express" rain storm!

Don't miss the solstice eclipse of the moon tonight - IF you have clear skies! The eclipse runs from 10:33 to 2:01 PST, and there are 72 minutes of totality starting at 11:41 PST. I'll have my camera ready, but I really don't expect to see much with this constant rain.
Locality:

Notes:
66027810 I believe these are Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) mushrooms in the woods today. Like all boletes, these mushrooms have pores like a sponge instead of gills - like soggy sponges in this case! The pores stain blue when bruised, but they are not that mushroom. They are said to be edible, but I haven't tried them. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2010)

The rain storm slowed to a drizzle this afternoon, so I took a walk with my camera and a plastic trash bag to protect it. I didn't really expect to find new mushrooms in the middle of a rain storm. Mushrooms are creatures of the rain, but they don't usually fruit while it's raining because it's impossible to disperse their spores.

File.Name: ac20c_ac20-010.jpg
Notes:
66027619 The rain storm slowed to a drizzle this afternoon, so I took a walk with my camera and a plastic trash bag to protect it. I didn't go far, but I was surprised to find dozens of delicious new Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms along our road! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2010)

Mushrooms are creatures of the rain, but they don't like to fruit while it's raining because it's impossible to disperse their spores. (Don't ask me how they know, but they know!) But I keep my eye out for these 'shrooms in particular, and I'm sure they weren't there before the rain!
Locality: Don't ask me how they know

It shows the "pineapple express" of storm clouds that extend out to Hawaii and beyond. Note the little counter-clockwise storm off the Oregon coast that is driving all these storms towards us. The storms approach us from the south, which adds "orographic enhancement" as they meet our east-west mountain barrier. We expect (at least) another two days of rain. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2010)

This is highly unusual weather - especially in an expected-dry "La NiÒa" year! We've had close to 12 inches (30 cm) of rain so far from this storm!

File.Name: ac21a_ac21-009.jpg
Notes:
66040439 I tried several different exposures to capture the rain drops. This is 1/60 @ f/5 @ ISO 400. (I had to bump up the ISO to get a faster shutter speed.)

The tree is a beautiful Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) in the Ericaceae plant family. I was standing on the front porch. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2010)

Happy solstice day! It's winter for us, but maybe it's summer for you. It's the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. From now on, the sunlight days will each be a little bit longer for a while. I'm ready!

We had light rain today, but it never let up. It was a gloomy day, appropriate for the solstice. We stand at about 13 inches (33 cm) of rain since Friday. Not bad for an expected-dry "La NiÒa" year!

File.Name: ac21b_ac21-002.jpg
Notes: 66040114 I found this interesting little mushroom in the rain today growing from a cavity on a Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) log in an uncovered wood pile. I'm not sure what it is. You can see signs of a universal veil. The branched gills almost look reticulate. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2010)

File.Name: ac21c_ac20-016.jpg
Notes: 66040030 Here is a soggy Russula maculata (or kin) mushroom that I found along our driveway in the continuing rain today. I had my camera under a plastic bag! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2010)

File.Name: ac21d_ac21-NHIR-4.jpg
Notes:
66039821 This updated NOAA Satellite (composite) image from 4:00 this afternoon shows the next chapter in our continuing rain event. Santa Barbara is at the sharp bend in California. You can see that the "pineapple express" has dropped south and lost some steam, but now the storm off the coast of Oregon that has been causing all this rain is about to hit us. This storm is coming from the north, but it's sucking a tail of moisture from the south. This will add "orographic enhancement" as it meets our east-west mountain barrier. It will be an interesting night! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2010)

We had light rain today, but it never let up. We stand at about 13 inches (33 cm) of rain since Friday. Not bad for an expected-dry "La NiÒa" year!

File.Name: ac22a_ac22-053.jpg
Notes:
66052982 What do insects do in the rain? Well, some of them just hang out!

This is a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) that I found in the rain today. It was raining when I got this photo. It's hanging on a branch of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus). What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2010)

I got this photo near the bridge over our creek, and I've seen them here before. See my photos:
   2009 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/56940048
   2008 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/44454306

We've had close to 15 inches (38 cm) of rain in the past week! The big storm expected last night mostly went south of us, but I'm not complaining. Our total is now 22 inches for the rain year which begins in September. Not bad for an expected-dry "La NiÒa" year!

Tomorrow is forecast to be mostly sunny - our first clear day after 6 days of rain. I'll be ready with my camera!
Locality: 38 cm) of rain in the past week!  The big storm expected last night mostly went south of us
