File.Name: ac22b_ac22-005.jpg
Notes:
66052505 It's Banana Slug weather!

Here's a giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) investigating a soggy Russula mushroom in the woods today. Look close - it's picking up some Springtail (order Collembola) insect hitchhikers! I saw several more of these interesting terrestrial mollusks on my walk in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2010)

These critters move very slowly - unless you try to photograph them in the low light they prefer, and then they move way too fast for the slow shutter speeds you need!

(Sorry Nano - I don't think we have Velvet Worms here in California. Maybe this will do? I'll look for Slender Salamanders tomorrow!)

File.Name: ac22c_ac22-062.jpg
Notes:
66052296 Here is a strange Crested White Coral (Clavulina cristata) mushroom, growing from the leaves under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our house today. It's small - only an inch or two tall. It looks like a strange crystalline growth, or something from the ocean. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2010)

The rain let up today - at least it became off and on showers. We even had a few moments of sunshine. Tomorrow is forecast to be mostly sunny - our first clear day after 5 days of rain. I'll be ready with my camera!
CommonNames: Crested White Coral; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ac23a_ac23-008.jpg
Notes:
66064199 Slugs have teeth?

Well, sort of. This giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) does have tooth-like soft mouthparts to help it munch this soggy Lactarius mushroom in the woods today. You can see its "tooth prints" on the mushroom - they are more like gum prints! There are also a pair of hitchhiking Springtail (order Collembola) insects on its eye stalks. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2010)

These critters move very slowly - until you try to photograph them in the low light they prefer, and then they move way too fast for the slow shutter speeds you need! I bumped my ISO up to 800 to get this photo.

The weatherman promised mostly sunny today, but we were in a cloud all day. I guess it's good for the mushrooms!

Sorry Nano - I found no Salamanders today. In fact the last Slender Salamander (Batrachoseps attenuatus) I found in the woods was way back in 2006. I admit I usually don't look for them (under bark). I hope their population is ok.
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/12793892

File.Name: ac23b_ac22-040.jpg
Notes:
66063869 This is an impressive cluster of mushrooms that I found in the woods yesterday. They look like Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) on a crack in a living Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods - but my spidey-sense was tingling! (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2010)

Several things are wrong. The mushrooms seem to have yellow gills; I don't think I've ever seen Honey Mushrooms in a living tree; and when I picked a mushroom, it came away clean from the cluster.

I was thinking Big Laughing Mushroom (Gymnopilus spectabilis aka G. junonius), but I went back today to collect a specimen, and sure enough - it has white spores. So I guess it is just an unusual cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) after all - but I'm still not sure.

Identifying mushrooms is a bitch - like identifying clouds by their shape. Don't bet your life on it!
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Coast Live Oak; Big Laughing Mushroom; Honey Mushrooms

File.Name: ac24b_ac23-013.jpg
Notes: 66075256 I guess these are the fruiting bodies of the well-named "Split-Gill" (Schizophyllum commune) fungus in our woods on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree encrusted with lichens. I've seen these mushrooms before, but never so furry. I guess I've never seen them fresh. It looks like they're covered with gray carpet! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2010)

File.Name: ac24c_ac24-002.jpg
Notes:
66075196 Maggots for Christmas Eve? Well, why not! I photographed them today, and they are part of the natural life of our woods.

I'm pretty sure these are larvae of Drosophilid Flies - aka Pomace Flies - in the family Drosophilidae of the insect order Diptera. They are turning these Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) into slime. This is such a universal winter occurrence in our woods, that I assume the mushrooms somehow invite the flies to lay their eggs and create maggots, which will carry their spores away. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2010)

It's kind of unnerving to do a Google search on ["honey mushrooms" drosophila maggots] and the first three sites that come up are my photos! Biologists (who study these flies) should get outside more to see what their Drosophilid flies actually do in the world! *Sigh* I guess that's the difference between biology and natural history.
CommonNames: Drosophilid Flies; Pomace Flies; Honey Mushrooms

File.Name: ac25a_ac24-025.jpg
Notes:
66083287 Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

My Christmas photo features Band-Tailed Pigeons (Columba fasciata) on top of a beautiful Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family in the woods, with ripe red berries. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2010)

This photo reminds me of my Mom's old Christmas cards. Start here and keep clicking [Next]:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/12018042

These are wary native woodland pigeons, not common city pigeons. A flock of these native birds live in our canyon in the mountains. I got this photo from our deck yesterday with my long Tamron 200-500 mm lens.

It was a relentlessly gloomy Christmas day, but we're finally getting more rain. That's a real Christmas present!

File.Name: ac25b_ac22-031.jpg
Notes:
66082866 Santa brought us beautiful mushrooms for Christmas!

We don't have Amanita muscaria in our mountains - at least not the red ones with the white Christmas trim - they prefer pine trees, not oaks. But maybe this beautiful Russula maculata (or kin) mushroom will do for Christmas colors! There are lots of these in the woods after our recent rains. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2010)

File.Name: ac25c_ac22-047.jpg
Notes:
66082592 Santa brought us beautiful mushrooms for Christmas!

This is a strange "Witch's Butter" (Tremella mesenterica or kin) mushroom growing on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. It's looks like a yellow brain! (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2010)

I don't recall ever seeing Witch's Butter so large. The recent rain has been good for it.

File.Name: ac25d_ac22-049.jpg
Notes:
66082463 Santa brought us beautiful mushrooms for Christmas!

This is a slimy Cortinarius mushroom in the woods - it's almost too slimy to pick up. I believe it is part of the Cortinarius cylindripes clan. Both cap and stem are slimy, which places it in the subgenus Myxacium. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2010)

There may be a connection between Christmas and Santa Claus! See the writings of "Cosmic Jimmy" aka James Arthur. They have an odd way of disappearing and reappearing on the Web, but check out (for now):
   http://www.amanitashop.com/amanita-arthur/mankind1.htm

File.Name: ac26a_ac23-032.jpg
Notes:
66095632 Here are beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus) mushrooms growing in the woods among the fallen leaves and twigs of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The velvety caps and wide-spaced gills are especially handsome. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2010)

These little mushrooms have wiry stems and an interesting connection with lichens - they have a remarkable ability to dry out and then revive after new rain. You can wear them like earrings if you're ears are pierced!

File.Name: ac26b_ac23-039.jpg
Notes:
66095480 This must be a beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom in our woods. The caps are smooth and polished, with a yellow-green tinge. They have a "death cup" volva at the base. This one is just beginning to tear its "partial veil" (which covers the gills) free. The fragments of the veil will form the "ring" on the stalk. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2010)

This is one of the wild mushrooms you must learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous!

It's curious that this mushroom is said to have been introduced from Europe, yet I found this mushroom deep in the woods, far from any road.

File.Name: ac26c_ac22-035.jpg
Notes:
66095290 This appears to be a cluster of little white-spored Mycena mushrooms in the woods, probably growing from buried wood under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2010)

One monograph on the genus lists 218 species, and its surely incomplete! I'm happy not knowing what these are, though the are distinctive. Most of our little Mycenas grow one-by-one as saprophytes on leaves. These are different.

File.Name: ac27a_ac27-006.jpg
Notes:
66108155 Here is another one of the strange mushrooms in our woods. This is the Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum or kin) mushroom in the woods today - though this one seems to lack both ribs and a stalk. You can see the fertile spore-bearing surface inside the cup. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2010)

Darn - there seems to be a little brown slug in the center of this mushroom! I didn't notice when I took the photo.

As I type this, I'm also listening to a fabulous PBS TV program about the pianist Glenn Gould. Watch it if you can!

File.Name: ac27b_ac27-008.jpg
Notes:
66108011 This is not the first new flower of the rain year, but it's close! This is a cluster of new flowers of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family growing in the canyon. They are related to avocado. They're starting to open. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2010)

I like the geometry of these flowers.

File.Name: ac27c_ac27-023.jpg
Notes: 66107781 It's not quite the new year yet, but here is one of the new year's first insects. It's a furry bee-like Bee Fly - family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera. It perched (just for a moment) on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. I saw a few of these flies today. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2010)

File.Name: ac28a_ac27-033.jpg
Notes:
66121315 These large Agaricus mushrooms look like delicious Portobello (Agaricus bisporus) mushrooms, and maybe they are! But maybe not. They also look like the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom which is poisonous to many people. I need to understand the Agaricus mushrooms better. (Until then, I'll stick with Chanterelles!) (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2010)

Look at the mushroom on the left. You can see the "ring" of the mushroom just tearing free of the cap. The ring is the "partial veil" that protects the gills until they ripen. Some mushrooms also have a "universal veil" that covers the whole mushroom - not just the gills - before it opens.

*Sigh* - we have another rain storm settling in tonight, and possible snow tomorrow!
ScientificName: Agaricus; Agaricus bisporus; Agaricus hondensis; Agaricus
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Agaricus bisporus; Agaricus hondensis; Agaricus

File.Name: ac28b_ac28-013.jpg
Notes:
66121106 Here is a cluster of "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) growing in sandy soil at the base of a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree root in the woods. I'm not sure what they are, and I won't try to guess right now. Pretty though! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2010)

I'll have a better idea what they are (and more time) after a few more days.

File.Name: ac28c_ac27-016.jpg
Notes:
66120829 Here is another strange mushroom - the Common Club Coral (Clavariadelphus pistillaris or kin) mushroom growing in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. They are all stalk, related to the beautiful coral mushrooms, but they don't branch like coral mushrooms do. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2010)

Are they edible? My mushroom book says they are "Harmless. The taste and texture are reminiscent of stale rope.
CommonNames: Common Club Coral; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ac29a_ac29-016.jpg
Notes:
66132031 I'm pretty sure this handsome mushroom is the Woodland Amanita (Amanita silvicola). It has warts on its cap like the Pine Cone Amanita (A. magniverrucata, but the warts on the cap aren't so distinct. It has a very distinct ring on the stem, but no obvious volva. Edibility is unknown - be careful! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2010)

The little green plants are native perennial California Bedstraw (Galium californicum) in the Rubiaceae plant family.

It was very cold and windy today after our most recent storm dropped an inch of rain last night. I took my usual walk in the woods anyway, but the only things not blowing around were the mushrooms!
Locality:
Galium californicum) in the Rubiaceae plant family.

It was very cold and windy today after our most recent storm dropped an inch of rain last night. I took my usual walk in the woods anyway
CommonNames: Woodland Amanita; Pine Cone Amanita; California Bedstraw
ScientificName: Amanita silvicola; A. magniverrucata; Galium californicum; Rubiaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita silvicola; A. magniverrucata; Galium californicum; Rubiaceae

File.Name: ac29b_ac29-010.jpg
Notes:
66131685 Demon dentures?

No, I believe this is a Gilled Polypore (Lenzites betulina) growing from a fallen oak branch in the woods. It appears to be a gilled mushroom, but it's actually a polypore in the fungal order Polyporales. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2010)

Maybe this is an example of convergent evolution - a fungus that developed its gills independently of the rest of the Agaricales mushrooms?

File.Name: ac29c_ac24-003.jpg
Notes:
66131078 Here is another Agaricus mushroom in the woods today. I'm pretty sure this is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom. That tearing "partial veil" ring on the stem sure looks like felt! Looks tastly, but this kind of mushroom is poisonous to many people. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2010)

(The blue tint is a white-balance thing. It's hard to photograph mushrooms in such dim light. Many of my recent photos are 6-8 second exposures!)

File.Name: ac30a_ac30-005.jpg
Notes:
66144370 Here is a structurally perfect Coccora mushroom - the Italian name means cocoon - Amanita calyptrata growing in the woods today under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). This beautiful mushroom is soft to the touch and the eyes. Despite the Italian name, it is only known from the west coast of the US. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2010)

The mushroom is covered with a thick layer of the "universal veil" material that once covered the whole mushroom in an "amanita egg". You can see the veil remains both on the cap and in the "volva" at the base of the stem. The gills are still covered with their "partial veil" that will tear free and form the ring on the stem.

It's edible, but I generally avoid eating all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. But this really is a distinctive mushroom that I should try. My book describes it as having a "fishy" taste, which isn't too encouraging.

It's interesting that I find the same mushrooms every year in the same place. It's like the fungal mycelium has the woods laid out in a pattern. I could almost make a map...

It's been cold! The water froze over our classic hot tub last night, and the ice never never thawed today.

File.Name: ac30b_ac30-012.jpg
Notes:
66143980 This is a troop of some kind of Cortinarius mushrooms growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. I can tell the genus because they have the characteristic reddish-brown "cortina" of hairs on their stalks which have caught falling spores. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2010)

I believe these mushrooms are in the subgenus Phlegmacium, with a viscid cap and an equal stalk without a rimmed base. I tried keying them out in my book, but I get lost every time - the key has 138 couplets and there are over 1,000 species! It's best not to eat any Cortinarius anyway. The genus contains several poisonous species, and the species are very hard to tell apart.

Identifying mushrooms is a bitch - like identifying clouds by their shape...

File.Name: ac30c_ac27-040.jpg
Notes: 66143096 I've been finding quite a few of these large white Hygrophorus sordidus mushrooms in the woods this year. Hygrophorus mushrooms typically have gills that feel waxy when you rub them between your fingers, but these hardly feel waxy at all. They are fruiting under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. My book says that they are "edible but not choice". No thanks! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2010)

File.Name: ac31a_ac31-008.jpg
Notes:
66156489 This is a remarkable mushroom-like plant of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. These aren't flowers - just the bracts that will soon host flowers. This early-flowering native has red leaves with just a bit of green. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2010)

It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in this place. It looks mushroom-like coming up fully-formed from the earth, and it must have a fungal connection with its host plant.

We have another rain storm coming in tonight. They just keep coming - even in this expected-dry "La NiÒa" year!
Locality: Quercus berberidifolia) in this place. It looks mushroom-like coming up fully-formed from the earth

File.Name: ac31b_ac31-005.jpg
Notes:
66156434 These must be brilliant Russula emetica (or kin) mushrooms in the woods growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. This photo taken in deep shade doesn't do them justice. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2010)

They look beautiful, and they have an interesting hot spicy taste if you just nibble. But as the name suggests, they are quite poisonous. Not "drop down dead" poisonous, but "wish you were dead" stomach upset. There is a rumor that parboiling destroys the toxins.

What's that interloper brown-spored mushroom doing in the photo? Well, it was attached to the Russula I picked, so here it is. I don't know what it is.

File.Name: ac31c_ac31-029.jpg
Notes:
66156356 Don't believe everything you read on mushrooms!

I found this "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus satanas) mushroom along the road in the woods. It has impressive bulk - the cap is about 10 inches (25 cm) across! These bolete mushroom have a spongy spore-bearing surface instead of gills. It doesn't look very appetizing - in fact it's quite poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2010)

These mushrooms stain blue when you scratch or handle then, but they are not that other notorious blue-staining mushroom. It's fun to leave messages for other mushroom collectors to find!

These mushrooms were early last year, but this is the first one I've seen this year.

File.Name: 9101a-8c30-186.jpg
Notes:
45495423 Beautiful Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) waking up at the Ellwood Main Monarch Grove as part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. As they warm up in the sunlight they get a little frisky. (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2008)

I ignore most holidays - New Year's included - but the start of the new year means something. I've been counting my blessings today rather than making resolutions I won't keep.

I'm thankful for many things, like the U.S. election ;=) and the fact that nature is so amazing and fun to photograph. But I also give thanks to Fotolog for making it so easy - and compulsive! - to share my photos and get some feedback!

I had a stoke last summer, and I give Fotolog much credit for my ongoing recovery. It was a real challenge at first just typing my comments, but I've gotten so much better. Teaching is also going well - I even performed a (mock) Wedding Ceremony on the last day of school before vacation. Thanks!

File.Name: 9101b_8c28-075.jpg
Notes:
45494195 I found this cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on an oak leaf in the woods a few days ago. They have the most remarkable eyes - with eyelashes. And blue booties! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2008)

I give thanks that the spiders and insects and other criitters in the woods are so beautiful and so varied in form! I take endless joy in trying to photograph them. There's always something new.

And I have a new stumper. Insects have a 6-sided gleam in the eye. It has to do with the 6-sided "ommatidia" simple-eyes that make up the large compound eyes of these insects.

And when I shoot too near the sun, I get a 7-sided flare in my photos that matches the 7 petals on the diaphram of my favorite Nikon 105mm micro lens. It's a refraction thing.

So why does this spider - with simple eyes, not compound - have a 5-sided gleam in its eye?? (I'll get back to this stumper!)

File.Name: 9101c_8c30-134.jpg
Notes:
45493283 The scene of the "Gap Fire" - a wildfire that threatened our home on the top of the mountains last summer. This is looking over the power lines at the base of the pass towards the area known as the Playground. It still looks pretty barren. I guess we need more rain before the "fire-follower" plants will emerge. The banding on the rocks is interesting - it's the native geology, not passing clouds. (Goleta foothills, 30 December 2008)

I have a few photos of how the wildfire looked from our house on top of the mountains:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/37369803
and [Next] from there.

I give thanks that fire is a rejuvenating force as well as a destructive one - the hills will come back surprisingly fast!

Heh, I'm also pretty happy our house didn't burn down! In fact, no homes were lost in this wildfire, probably thanks to the massive air support and the avocado and lemon groves that acted as a buffer with the city below. The "Tea fire" in November was another story. This urban wildfire burned over 200 homes in the Santa Barbara foothills. All I can say is that they will come back too.

File.Name: 9101d_8c27-022a.jpg
Notes:
45491725 Ancient native American Chumash pictographs in a sandsone "cave" - really just an overhang. I got this photo near the Chumash bedrock mortar holes filled with rainwater that I show in my photo here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/45300951

The rock art is protected by a chain-link fence. I had to get real close and shoot through the fence. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2008)

I give thanks that the people who lived here before us left a record of their thoughts and dreams! I can only look at these fantastic designs and wonder...

File.Name: 9101e_8c26-159.jpg
Notes:
45490963 Pretty little "Fairy Fingers" (Clavaria vermicularis or kin) mushrooms growing in a natural compost pile of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2008)

I give thanks that mushrooms are so strange and varied in form! There's no reason for the fleshy fruiting bodies of fungi to be so darn interesting if they just deposit spores - but they are.

File.Name: 9101f_8c21-066.jpg
Notes:
45490478 The waxy buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family are fully formed now, though the flowers won't open until spring. It's an interesting strategy. Several plants form their buds early, but many don't. I wonder if there's an advantage, or is this just how it's always been done? (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2009)

I give thanks that nature is so interesting and so vast that there is always something new in the woods to photograph!

File.Name: 9102a-9101-252.jpg
Notes:
45535714 Aphid and Wasp sequence - 3 of 3

The tiny aphid-sized parasitic Wasp (Hymenoptera) in the [Previous] photo with abdomen ready, is about to make its move against its Milkweed Aphid (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) prey!

The "sting" was over in the blink of an eye. I missed it.

Maybe it's a Braconid Wasp (Braconidae) or a Crabronidae? I wonder why it chose the mature winged aphid as its prey - maybe that's how they get around? (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2009)

This is as close as I can get - full 1:1 macro outside in the garden, and a tight crop of the photo. Any closer requires studio conditions.
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Braconidae; Crabronidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Braconidae; Crabronidae

File.Name: 9102b-9101-253.jpg
Notes:
45535074 Aphid and Wasp sequence - 2 of 3

The tiny aphid-sized parasitic Wasp (Hymenoptera) in the [Previous] photo has decided on its prey - one of the Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) still living on a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. Maybe it's a Braconid Wasp (Braconidae) or a Crabronidae? (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2009)
CommonNames: Wasp; Milkweed Aphids; Butterfly Weed; Braconid Wasp
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa; Braconidae; Crabronidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa; Braconidae; Crabronidae

File.Name: 9102c-9101-250.jpg
Notes:
45534587 Aphid and Wasp sequence - 1 of 3

I can just imagine the conversation between these aphids: "Oh shit - here comes a wasp!" "Try to act nonchalant, or we'll end up as mummies like Fred over there!"

This photo shows a tiny aphid-sized parasitic Wasp (Hymenoptera) moving in on a pair of Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) still living on a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. Maybe it's a Braconid Wasp (Braconidae) or a Crabronidae? There were quite a few of these tiny wasps picking off the last of the aphids. The aphid husk in the lower-left shows the results of their work, with a big exit-hole on the rear of its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2009)

I just noticed that we actually have about 10 minutes of sun on the corner of the garden at midday! I'm usually off on my walk to sunnier sites, so I didn't know. The milkweeds look pretty sad at this time of year, but they still have a few aphids on them.
CommonNames: Wasp; Milkweed Aphids; Butterfly Weed; Braconid Wasp
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa; Braconidae; Crabronidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa; Braconidae; Crabronidae

File.Name: 9103a-9103-016.jpg
Notes:
45577243 The Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) are still "converging" in the canyon, though there are not as many of them as there used to be.

These ladybugs are shaking off the raindrops from a sprinkle last night. We only got a trace of rain, but it will help the mushrooms grow and keep the new seedling spring wildflowrs going. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2009)

File.Name: 9103b-9102-265.jpg
Notes:
45576909 Surprise! The caterpillars of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies are already active on this native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family.

These Variable Checkerspots are interesting because they overwinter as caterpillars, and I guess they come out on warm days. I don't expect these early caterpillars to last unless they know something about the weather that I don't know! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2009)

I have many photos of the adult butterflies that are usually on the wing during May and June. Try this one:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/37230228
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Bush Monkey Flower; Figwort; Variable Checkerspots

File.Name: 9103c-9103-011.jpg
Notes:
45576337 So that's why they're called MUSHrooms!

It's the end of these Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) on the edge of the garden. They got a repreive because of the cold weather we've been having, but their time has come. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2009)

The maggots are turning the mushrooms into a river of slime. I guess they are the larvae of Pomace Flies aka Small Fruit Flies in the family Drosophilidae in the order Diptera. This fly is in the same Drosophila genus as the critter still studied in genetics classes around the world. No other creature has contributed so much to our understanding of genetics! (Despite what Sarah Palin said!)
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Pomace Flies; Small Fruit Flies

File.Name: 9104a-9104-057.jpg
Notes:
45613102 Tiny mushroom in the woods today with with raindrops and my 0.5 mm tip pen for scale. The pen gave the mushroom a little "kiss" of color! I don't try to find mushrooms this small, but sometimes I notice them and try for a photo. I guess it is a Mycena mushroom. "Heh, I bet you say that about all the tiny white mushrooms!" (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2009)

Today's photos are all tiny things. *Sigh* It's the end of a wonderful vacation with nothing much to do than take photos. Back to school tomorrow!

File.Name: 9104b-9104-051.jpg
Notes: 45612414 The tiny Springtail (order Collembia) insects with dark eye-spots scatter as I move this White Russula (Russula albidula or kin) mushroom into the sun for a photo. There are gazillions of these little insects in the woods after our little rain yesterday. Not surprisingly, most of them go unnoticed. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2009)

File.Name: 9104c-9104-047.jpg
Notes:
45611894 The maggots in between the gills are turning our Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) into slime belore my eyes. I guess they are the larvae of Pomace Flies aka Small Fruit Flies in the family Drosophilidae in the order Diptera. They've advanced in a day! (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2009)

It's impossible to get a sharp photo of these critters without using flash - which I prefer not to do. They're squirming constantly during the 2 second exposure in low light at ISO 400. That's the point!
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Pomace Flies; Small Fruit Flies

File.Name: 9105a_9103-040.jpg
Notes: 45659021 This beautiful Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silicola) mushroom in the woods is just tearing its patial veil to show its gills to the world for the first time. The veil will collapse to form the distinctive "ring" on the stem. It's edible and tasty, but you must be very careful not to confuse it with a deadly Amanita or one of the "unpleasant" Agaricus species. The mushrooms bruise slightly yellow on the margin - you can see where I rubbed it. They have a sweet almond odor when fresh like this. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2009)

File.Name: 9105b_9103-028.jpg
Notes:
45658555 Half-eaten Boletus subtomentosus mushroom growing in the woods, showing the tooth-marks of the rodent who ate it - probably the Dusky-Footed Wood Rat (Neotoma fuscipes) who had a large nest nearby.

Rodents nibble many of our mushrooms before I get to! They have a knack for finding them first while still in the ground leaving only an empty hole! Yes the mushrooms are edible, but definitely not choice for flavor or texture. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2009)

File.Name: 9105c_9103-043.jpg
Notes:
45657789 These little Mycena mushrooms in the woods are probably the smallest mushrooms for which I know a name. They are Mycena acicula - or maybe Mycena oregonensis - instantly recognizable because of their tiny size and bright colors. They were growing from wood debris near an old forgotten stash of firewood. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2009)

I don't have a pen for scale in this photo, but the spines of the oak leaves are about 1 mm long. They really are tiny!

File.Name: 9106a_9103-030.jpg
Notes:
45698496 I think I finally have these Amanitas sorted out.

This is NOT the yellow-form of the Fly Agaric (Amanita muscaria var. formosa), but it IS the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) growing under a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family) tree. I've also found it under Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the same plant family. I have at least one photo ID wrong. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2009)

The structural feature that makes me so sure is that it has a "collar-like" rather than a "scaly" volva. I was confused by our A. pantherina mushrooms which also have yellowish caps - though not bright yellow - but apparently they intergrade with A. gemmata. Now I understand.

This mushroom is not edible. It intergrades with A. pantherina. It probably contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria - only more so.
CommonNames: Fly Agaric; Jonquil Amanita; Madrone; Heath; Big-berry Manzanita
ScientificName: Amanita muscaria; formosa; Amanita gemmata; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: Amanita muscaria; formosa; Amanita gemmata; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca

File.Name: 9106b_9103-036.jpg
Notes:
45697546 I think I finally have these Amanitas sorted out.

I believe this is the Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) coming up under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. BUT it has a yellowish cap which suggests that it is intergrading with the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) - see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2009)

Yipes - as if the mushrooms weren't hard enough to identify, I have to worry about them intergrading!

The mushroom is poisonous! It contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria at higher concentrations. Large doses can be fatal.
CommonNames: Panther Amanita; Coast Live Oak; Jonquil Amanita
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita gemmata
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita gemmata

File.Name: 9106c_9104-060.jpg
Notes: 45696857 I guess this an Entoloma ferruginans mushroom in the E. nidorosum group. It has "pink" spores and a surprising smell of bleach, like a swiming pool. I can't imagine eating it, though there are reports that it is edible. This mushroom seems to have a glow, even though it is growing where the sun never shines at this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2009)

File.Name: 9107a_8c31-234.jpg
Notes:
45746103 I found this "egg" of the Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. It has a yellowish cap which suggests that it is intergrading with the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) which is nearby. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2008)

This mushroom is poisonous! It contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria at higher concentrations. Large doses can be fatal.

It has a yellowish cap which suggests that it is intergrading with the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) which is nearby - see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2008)

This mushroom is poisonous! It contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria at higher concentrations. Large doses can be fatal.

File.Name: 9108a_8c31-229.jpg
Notes:
45782642 This is the same "egg" of the Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom as the previous photo. I sliced the "egg" open so you can see what will become the gills and the volva when the mushroom opens. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2008)

This mushroom is poisonous! It contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria at higher concentrations. Large doses can be fatal.

File.Name: 9109a_8c31-201.jpg
Notes: 45819744 A pair of pretty little dark-spored mushrooms - Coprinus or Psathyrella? - in the shady woods. Nothing special, and too insubstantial to eat, but they have a beautiful form! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2008)

File.Name: 9109b_8c31-214.jpg
Notes: 45819280 This is the same cluster of little white-spored Mycena mushrooms in the woods as my previous photo, a week later. The woods - and the mushrooms - are already drying out. I hope this isn't already the end of our mushroom season, and I hope this is not the harbinger of another drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2009)

File.Name: 9109c_8c26-165.jpg
Notes: 45818811 Cluster of pretty little white-spored Mycena mushrooms in the woods, probably growing from rotting wood under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree back when it was cold and wet. Compare my next photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2008)

File.Name: 9110a_9110-001.jpg
Notes:
45854772 I know this isn't quite sharp - there was a breeze this morning - but it's an action shot! This tiny Fly in the order Diptera has caught an even tinier fly (or midge) as prey for breakfast. Just looking at the antennae (the body looks wrong), I wonder if it is a little Robber Fly (family Asilidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2009)

This is a tight crop of a "macro photo" taken near 1:1 magnification. I guess I could use extention tubes like Nano is using to get even closer, though the real-world problems like the slight breeze seem an issue.

It was warm (60 degrees) and dry today. My Dad was visiting, and he reported that the Santa Ana winds are howling down south of us. Fortunately we don't get the Santa Ana winds here in the Santa Barbara mountains, but it's still a bad omen for the new year. It was a short mushroom season.
Locality: 60 degrees) and dry today.  My Dad was visiting

File.Name: 9110b_9110-005.jpg
Notes: 45854042 I guess this is a Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae in the order Hymenoptera) - a wasp without the narrow waist. I found it resting on a Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaf this morning. Maybe this is the critter that makes the leaf galls in our willows? (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2009)

File.Name: 9110c_9110-007.jpg
Notes:
45853645 Parasitized husks of Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. You can see the exit holes where a preditor/parasiite has burst out. Think of the movie Alien.

I guess the culprit is a little Braconid Wasp Braconidae) - maybe Lysiphlebus testaceipes or kin? It's tough being a late-season aphid! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2009)

I have a recent photo of the wasp in action:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/45535714
CommonNames: Milkweed Aphids; Butterfly Weed; Braconid Wasp

File.Name: 9111a_9111-037.jpg
Notes:
45890153 Here is a Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera framed by new grass blades. It's resting on a fallen leaf of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2009)

It's hard to grumble at a warm, perfect day without wind in mid-winter. But I worry that we're in for another drought year. We've only had about 7 inches of rain this year, and the forecast is for continued warm and dry. There are aleready "Red Flag" fire-weather warnings for Ventura county south of us. It's nice to find some early insects out though!

File.Name: 9111b_9111-019.jpg
Notes:
45889282 Me and my shadow...

These furry little Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) are so cute - they look like flying mice up close! They are even cuter in the air, but good luck getting the photo. They have a remarkable proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2009)

File.Name: 9111c_9111-032.jpg
Notes: 45888963 A little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) resting on a fallen oak leaf in the woods. It has the distinctive "spurious vein" between the radius and the media on its wings - barely visible in this photo, but clear in others. It stayed close to the ground instead of hovering. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2009)

File.Name: 9112a_9111-025.jpg
Notes:
45934170 Our Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - are getting frisky with the warm weather we've been having this week. Note the ladybug on top about to take wing. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2009)

This photo is not quite sharp - how could it be when they are all moving and the exposure is 1/20 second @ f/11 @ ISO 800!

It's hard to grumble at a warm, perfect day without wind in mid-winter. But I worry that we're in for another drought year. It's nice to find some early insects active though!

File.Name: 9112b_9111-022.jpg
Notes: 45933797 Another furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera). This one is resting on a leaf of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae family growing by the creek. They are so cute - they look like flying mice up close! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2009)

File.Name: 9112c_9111-029.jpg
Notes: 45933452 An early-season Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae in the order Hymenoptera) resting on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. The wasps are blacker-than-black - hard to capture in this contrasty light. They flick their wings in a distinctive way. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2009)

File.Name: 9113a_9111-046.jpg
Notes:
45973524 Beautiful Cortinarius fulmineus or kin mushroom - that's my generic name for all these yellow cortinarii with a basal bulb. I don't collect them for the table because I can't tell the many species apart! That's a good reason right there not to consider them for the table! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2009)

You can often tell a mushroom's spore color by looking at the cap. This one is definitely rusty-brown. What aerodynamics blows the spores back onto the cap?

File.Name: 9113b_9111-048.jpg
Notes:
45973298 I guess this is part of the Corinarius sodagnitus group. It's definately in the in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap and rimmed basal bulb. You can see a remnant of the now orange "cortina" veil of hairs that once covered and protected the immature gills. It's probably not edible. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2009)

I found this mushroom hidden deep under a very slight "mushrump" in the woods. That's the mound of leaves the mushrooms push up from below. Much of the knack of finding mushrooms consists of reading the mushrumps! The mushrooms are hiding deep in the leaves because of the warm weather we've been having.

And be careful! It's easy to confuse this with the edible Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom. They grow together here in the canyon.
ScientificName: Corinarius sodagnitus; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius; Clitocybe nuda
associatedTaxa: Corinarius sodagnitus; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius; Clitocybe nuda

File.Name: 9114a_9111-018.jpg
Notes:
45974032 A perfect spider web in the woods in a native shrub of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the canyon. No doubt it's some kind of Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae), but the spider is really tiny. You can see the little spider perched in the center of its web. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2009)

Heh - the web has color bands just like a CD!

File.Name: 9115a_9115-097.jpg
Notes:
46055459 I didn't do my after-school hike club today, so I had some time to kill after school. I stopped for my favorite short hike on the way home, near Lake Cachuma. The only insects on the wing were these tiny Flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams - but they are remarkable! Note the plumose antennae, like a fancy headdress. (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 January 2009)

I couldn't actually see any of the beauty of this insect. It only becomes clear through the camera's eye.

File.Name: 9115b_9111-023.jpg
Notes:
46055121 These Scavenger Flies in the family Sarcophagidae of the order Diptera never really disappear from the woods. They must overwinter as adults since they are always ready to emerge on a warm winter day. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2009)

We've had warm winter days in January before, but this is crazy weather! We've had high temperatures in the mid-80s (28+ degrees Celsius) all this week. This is supposed to be our cool, wet season. This will signal a disaster for the plant and animal life in our woods if it continues, but it certainly will be interesting to document what happens!
Locality: 28+ degrees Celsius) all this week.  This is supposed to be our cool

File.Name: 9115c_9110-010.jpg
Notes:
46054475 The Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis in the Salicaceae family) leaves have finally fallen in the canyon - they've been holding on late this year! As they fall, they bring the galls down with them.

I believe this is the gall of a Willow Tube Gall Midge fly in the genus Iteomyia of the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. The galls have a little opening at the apex, and only occur on the undersides of the leaves. This one seems to be yearning to open! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2009)
CommonNames: Arroyo Willow; Willow Tube Gall Midge
ScientificName: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Iteomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae; Iteomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: 9116a_9116-120.jpg
Notes:
46094404 This is another tiny little Fly (order Diptera) on an branch in the woods near Lake Cachuma. I guess this is the same kind of fly as my photo yesterday at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/46055459

The antennae are very different, but I think the flies are the same. I guess that's the male yesterday with the plumose antennae, and the female today with the skimpy antennae. I visited my books, and this seems backwards to me. Aren't the females supposed to find the males, so shouldn't they have the more elaborate antennae? Maybe the males' antennae are just for show, like a peacocks's tail feathers? (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 January 2009)

Today's photos are all tiny critters - really too tiny to photograph outside of the studio with the wind and other distractions. (Nano will disagree!) But they're always worth a shot!

File.Name: 9116b_9103-021.jpg
Notes: 46093983 Tiny little Fly (order Diptera) on an oak leaf in the woods. I don't know what to say about it except that it's different for the tiny little fly in the [Next] photo. There's great diversity in the micro-world! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2009)

File.Name: 9116c_8c31-243.jpg
Notes: 46093821 A really tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on an oak leaf in the woods. For scale, the spines of the oak leaves are about one millimeter long. You can't quite make out the wonderful eyes these spiders have, but it's pretty cool how the spider's shadow brings out the structure of the leaf! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2009)

File.Name: 9117a_9117-028.jpg
Notes:
46127379 The 80+ degree weather (25+ Celsius) is bringing out the butterflies early. This is a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly in the canyon today. Actually I've been seeing them for several weeks, but I couldn't get them to pose for a photo until today! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2009)

This butterfly is pretty tattered. I believe they overwinter as adults so that they can fly early in spring.

The was a bit of breeze today. Maybe that's the first sign that this stagnant warm weather will end, and bring back the rain and mushrooms that we should be having.

File.Name: 9117b_9117-022.jpg
Notes:
46126837 Pretty Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly "puddling" - getting moisture or salts - from a sandstone outcrop by the creek today. It has intense blue colors on the upper-side of it's wings, but it usually sits with wings folded. You can get a glimpse of the blue color in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2009)

My book calls it "one of the first nonhibernating butterflies to fly in the spring" - I guess that's true!

File.Name: 9117c_9117-036.jpg
Notes:
46126539 There are too many kinds of Moths in the same Lepidoptera order as the butterflies - and "moth" is such a dull name!

This pretty little diurnal (day-flying) Moth has perfect camouflage against the sandstone rock except for those red "underwings" that it usually shows. It is small - about the size of a pencil eraser. I reckon it's too small to be a true Underwing Moth (genus Catocala). These moths are fairly common - meaning I've seen several this spring! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2009)

File.Name: 9118a_9118-109.jpg
Notes:
46161831 This is the same kind of tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) that made the CD-sized web I photographed a few days ago:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/45974032

I guess it's a "Garbage line spider" (Cyclosa) which leaves a trail of its victims in a vertical line down the web (not visible here). It has an interesting bottle-shaped extension to its abdomen. Geek that I am, I keep thinking of the Mandelbrot Set lurking in the center of the web. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2009)

It's hard to capture these tiny spiders without over-exposing them. I took this photo at -1.3 stops and it's still too bright.

File.Name: 9118b_9117-055.jpg
Notes:
46160692 Drinking from the cup of plenty!

This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is working new flowers of native Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. The shrubs started flowering early this year, but some plants are just starting to flower. I guess these shrubs are on a different clock! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2009)

File.Name: 9118c_9117-025.jpg
Notes:
46160018 This is another photo of the California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly in the canyon yesterday. This time it's disguised as an oak leaf! With their wings folded together, they are almost impossible to see unless you spot them land. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2009)

It's a Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae family. All insects have six legs, but look closer at this butterfly - it's perching on just four legs. Brushfoot butterflies have their front legs reduced T-Rex style, so they only have four functional legs. Simplify, simplify...

File.Name: 9119a_9119-119.jpg
Notes:
46202639 This is a Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera framed by new grass blades. The wasps are already active thanks to the unseasonably warm weather we've been having. I took this photo near our well head by the creek. There's some moisture in the soil due to leakage. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2009)

The good news is that there's "likely rain" (a 60% chance) on Wednesday! And tomorrow is (finally) Inauguration Day!

I don't think I've ever promoted a music video before on my f'log, but Pete Seeger singing "This Land" yesterday at the inauguration concert brought tears to my eyes:
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Xg0wiOHc9tI

finally) Inauguration Day!

File.Name: 9119b_9119-122.jpg
Notes: 46201647 A little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) resting on some kind of "Mexican Sage" (Salvia sp.) in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2009)

File.Name: 9119c_9119-112.jpg
Notes: 46201433 Here is the same kind of tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) taken from the other side of the web showing the underside of the spider. I believe this is a "Garbage line spider" (Cyclosa). The "stars" are wonderful, like a sci-fi movie! (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2009)

File.Name: 9120a_9118-057.jpg
Notes:
46245520 These are new berries of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum), a shrub in the Grossulariaceae family - see my [Previous] photo. The berries turn deep blue when they are ripe, but the immature berries are red-colored. The berries are very glandular. You can see the little pin-head glands stuck in them like cloves on a ham. They taste bitter until they are ripe, then bland. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2009)

This photo is shot "contre-jour" as the French call it - "against daylight". To me it's just backlight, shooting into the sun. I love the effect!

File.Name: 9120b_9118-064.jpg
Notes:
46244893 There seem to always be new flowers on the native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrubs in the Grossulariaceae family - during the winter months of course.

This plant is in our garden. It pre-existed our house, and it's now nearly 12 feet tall. It is the mother-shrub that feeds our winter insects and humingbirds during the winter months when nothing else is flowering. I got my first photo of the flowers on September 27, 2008 this year - before the first rain! - and it's still going strong! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2009)

File.Name: 9120c_9117-009.jpg
Notes:
46244253 The French have a name for this lighting - Contre-jour, "against daylight". To me it's just backlight, shooting into the sun. I love the effect!

This is a new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae family in the canyon shot contre-jour. It's very beautiful. Actually I don't have much choice in mid-winter. We live on the north-side of the mountains, and almost lose the sun in winter except for sunbeams in the woods. The sun is just starting to come back. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2009)

File.Name: 9121a_9118-072.jpg
Notes:
46287070 Despite a week of 80 degree (25+ celsius) temperatures, there are still mushrooms in the woods! I just have to look closer for "mushrumps" - the mound of leaves the mushrooms push up from below.

I believe these are part of the Cortinarius sodagnitus group - or maybe the Cortinarius glaucopus group . They have beautiful violet colors when fresh. You can see the remnant of the "cortina" that once covered the gills with spider web-like hairs. They tear free and fall as rusty-brown spore-stained hairs on the stems. You might confuse them with the edible Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom. Be careful collecting for your table! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2009)

The forecast is for much-needed rain tonight. I hope it happens to keep the mushrooms coming and the woods alive!
ScientificName: Cortinarius sodagnitus; Cortinarius glaucopus; Clitocybe nuda
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius sodagnitus; Cortinarius glaucopus; Clitocybe nuda

File.Name: 9121b_9118-076.jpg
Notes:
46286459 A pair of beautiful Cortinarius mushrooms in the woods. I guess they are part of the Cortinarius sodagnitus group, but they've already lost most of their color.

You can see the remnants of the "cortina" that once covered the gills with spider web-like hairs on the stems and picked up some color from the rusty-brown falling spores. You can also see a natural spore print where one mushroom leaned on another.

It is the fate of all cortinarius mushrooms to turn brown and indistinct as they age. I don't collect then for the table because I can't tell the many species apart. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2009)

File.Name: 9121c_9118-077.jpg
Notes:
46285907 A week of 80 degree temperatures (25+ celsius) hasn't completely stopped our mushrooms!

These are the well-named Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) mushroom in the woods under the oaks. The cracks on the cap are like a fractal hot cross bun. All boletes have a sponge-like layer of tubes under the cap instead of gills. Some are delicious, but this kind is not worth collecting for the table. It's ok to admire it though! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2009)

File.Name: 9122a_9118-082.jpg
Notes:
46323628 Male staminate flowers of native Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah fabaceus in the Cucurbitaceae family) growing in the mountains across the road from the Gap Fire last summer. The plants seem unusually robust. Maybe it's all those good smoke chemicals from the wildfire? (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2009)

We've only had about 1/4 inch of rain so far. Hopefully there's more coming!

Update 23 January 2009 - I now believe this is Marah Macrocarpus. That's why it seemed so striking! This is usually a coastal plant, so it unusual to find it high in the mountains like this.

File.Name: 9122b_9118-093.jpg
Notes: 46323068 New staminate male catkins of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Silk-Tassel (Garryaceae) family. This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants, and the males usually appear first. The flowers are interesting rather than beautiful, but they have their charm. You can see the stamens hanging out from the flower stalks. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2009)

File.Name: 9122c_9117-056.jpg
Notes:
46322834 Pretty flowers of a California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family growing in the woods. The flowers are interesting. They are born in little involucres almost like buckwheats. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2009)

Have you ever accidentally eaten a "Bay Leaf" in a stew? *Yuck* Our native bay trees are like that only more so. The trees are related to avocados, and they look like it. Feel stuffed up? Crush a leaf a smell it!

File.Name: 9123a_9117-043.jpg
Notes:
46362869 This is a Lepidoptera, but it's a moth rather than a butterfly. You can tell because the antennae do not have knobs on the end.

This pretty little diurnal (day-flying) Moth has perfect camouflage against the sandstone rock except for those red "underwings" that it usually shows. It is small - not much larger than a pencil eraser. I reckon it's too small to be a true Underwing Moth (genus Catocala). These moths are fairly common - meaning I've seen them several times this spring. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2009)

We had better rain today - about 1 1/4 inch total, maybe 3 cm. We only stand at about 50% normal, so every bit of rainfall helps!

File.Name: 9123b_9117-045.jpg
Notes:
46362600 Here you can see the beautiful blue upper wings of a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly resting on a leaf of Wood Mint (Stachys bullata in the Mint (Labiatae) family). It's not quite sharp, but it's good enough! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2009)

I have another recent photo of the butterfly with wings closed at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/46126837

File.Name: 9123c_9118-089.jpg
Notes: 46362364 I now believe these are the pure white flowers and tendrils of native Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah macrocarpus in the Cucurbitaceae family) growing in the mountains across the road from the Gap Fire last summer. It is M. macrocarpus and not M. fabaceus as I previously thought. This is usually a coastal plant, so it is unusual to find it high in the mountains like this. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2009)
ScientificName: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus
associatedTaxa: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus

File.Name: 9124a_9124-020.jpg
Notes:
46394670 It's a rain day. Here are raindrops on a new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae family in the canyon. I usually say the flowers remind me ballet dancers with petticoats in the air - this is a very wet ballet dancer! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2009)

Good rain today - it rarely got above a drizzle, but we've had 2-1/4 inches (about 6 cm) since Thursday. Every little bit helps in a drought year!

File.Name: 9124b_9124-006.jpg
Notes: 46394300 It's a rain day. Here are raindrops on a grass blade in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2009)

File.Name: 9124c_9124-003.jpg
Notes: 46394182 It's a rain day. Here are raindrops on Prickley Pear (Opuntia sp.) cactus spines in the garden, reflecting the cactus. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2009)

File.Name: 9125a_9125-090.jpg
Notes:
46431015 Curious Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) growing on a pile of leaves by the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2009)

It (or they) are remarkable critters that are part fungus and part protist. They (or it) live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying leaves. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" that makes tiny little mushroom-like structures like these that release spores that will disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying leaf pile to another.

I'll have a photo of the plasmodium stage tomorrow!
Locality: or it) live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying leaves.  When the weather is right

File.Name: 9125b_9125-042.jpg
Notes:

File.Name: 9126a_9125-046.jpg
Notes:
46475474 A raft of tiny Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushrooms sprouting from the fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the soggy woods yesterday. The are small but tough. These little mushrooms with wiry stems have a connection with lichens - they have a remarkable ability to dry out and then revive after each new rain. File.Name: 9126b_9124-023.jpg
Notes: 46475245 The rain brought out these new Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods! Here they are dropping their veils - the protective skin that cover the immature gills. File.Name: 9126c_9125-091.jpg
Notes:
46475064 This is the plasmodium stage of the same curious Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) that I found growing on a pile of leaves by the garden yesterday. I guess they're networking! See my [Previous] photo.

They are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part protist. They live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying leaves. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form this visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes that ends up as the tiny little mushroom-like structures in my [Previous] photo. Then they release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying leaf pile to another. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2009)

File.Name: 9127a_9126-158.jpg

File.Name: 9204a_9131-056.jpg
Notes:
46824623 Yum - Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms from the woods for dinner! It's one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2009)

These mushrooms bring $15-$20 a pound in local markets - more in restaurants - and a large specimen like this one was can weigh a pound or more. This makes me nervous - like putting little price tags and barcodes on the banana slugs and butterflies and native lilies in the woods. Be a naturalist and find your own!

We always "dry saute" these fine mushrooms to concentrate the flavor. Clean them with a knife instead of water - they're like sponges. Then slice them into a hot dry pan, and let them stew in their own juices to concentrate the flavor until almost dry. Add a bit of fresh pepper and garlic and a splash of olive oil. Delicious!

File.Name: 9209b_9208-046.jpg
Notes:
47013175 An interesting Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) that I found streaming in the woods yesterday. It was a rainy day - perfect for these little slime molds creatures. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2009)

These are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part protist. They live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying wood. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form this visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes as you can see in the photo. They will end up as tiny little mushroom-like structures that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying log or leaf pile to another.

File.Name: 9213a_9201-101.jpg
Notes:
47165291 Beautiful Coccora mushroom - the Italian name means cocoon - Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma - in the woods under native Madrone trees (Arbutus menziesii in the Heath (Ericaceae) family). I took this photo a week ago before the recent rains. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2009)

This mushroom shows all the classic mushroom anatomy that you need to know before you start eating wild mushrooms. It has a sac-like white cup or volva at the base, and white tissue on the cap that are part of the universal veil that once enclosed this mushroom like a soft egg shell. You can also see the partial veil membrane that once covered the gills pulling free from the cap to leave a skirt or ring on the stem.

Some mushrooms with these same characteristics of universal and partial veil are deadly poisonous, so I make it a point not to collect any Amanita mushrooms for the table. Despite it's toxic relatives, this native mushroom is distinctive with that thick soft felt-like veil that gives it a cocoon look. It is said to be edible and delicious. Maybe I'll try it sometime.
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

47164583 This photo is rich with memories for me, despite the flash-burn.

This is a huge girder under the east end of Cold Springs Bridge in our mountains. I brought my after-school DMS Hike Club kids here last Thursday. We sit under the roadway that rocks when cars and trucks pass over. We also "spit write" our names on the dusty girders. Spit on your finger and write in the dust, don't lick! This is "spit-writing" on the dusty girders dating back to hike club kids in 1992 that is still visible. Treebeard was here, and my sons Ewan and Ry, along with Mike, Kekoa, Arif, Richard, Gabe, Matt, and Cory! There was major earthquake-recontruction on the bridge a few years ago, but our spit-names survived. Wow, that's history! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2009)

The tradition continues! See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/dms/46110874

"Ewan rules" it says here back in 1992. He is also the father of my new grandkids. We're going to visit Ewan and his wife Roz in Portland next week!

47198053 This may be the tinest critter in the woods for which I know a proper name.

It's a tiny Mycena acicula mushroom coming up in an old woodpile. It doesn't really matter if it's edible - it is so tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2009)

File.Name: 9222a_9219-144.jpg
Notes:
47504100 Little Kelvin clutching a finger as he sleeps. (Portland, Oregon, 19 February 2009)

I figure babies clutch our fingers so we will keep them. It works!

Julie and I flew to Portland, Oregon last week to be with our new grandkids, Kelvin and Ottokar. I'll get back to the usual insects and mushrooms and slime molds tomorrow!

File.Name: 9222b_9218-115.jpg
Notes:
47503878 Kelvin and Ottokar are my new grandkids! They're easy to tell apart so far, as Otto (in the front) has some baby jaundice - a bit too much "Billy Rubin". He's also larger and more ferocious so far! (Portland, Oregon, 18 February 2009)

Julie and I spent the week in Portland, Oregon visiting as proud grandparents!

File.Name: 9222c_9218-139.jpg
Notes: 47503743 My new grandson Kelvin - one of the twins - clutching his mom's finger. (Portland, Oregon, 18 February 2009)

File.Name: 9222d_9219-147.jpg
Notes:
47503643 Grandma Julie holding baby Kelvin durng our visit to Portland, Oregon last week to visit our new twin grandsons. (Portland, Oregon, 19 February 2009)

Our son Ewan is a musician as you can tell from the photo. The kids already dig music, especially gospel songs on the autoharp.

File.Name: 9222e_9219-160.jpg
Notes: 47503450 Grandpa Treebeard ("GT" for short) holding little Kelvin in Portland, Oregon last week. My new grandkids! (Portland, Oregon, 19 February 2009)

File.Name: 9222f_9217-007.jpg
Notes:
47503135 My kids Ewan and Roz handing off their new twin babies in Portland, Oregon last week. (Portland, Oregon, 17 February 2009)

Kelvin and Ottokar - they're easy to tell apart so far, as Otto (in the back) has some baby jaundice - a bit too much "Billy Rubin," not a worry.

Julie and I spent the week visiting as proud grandparents!
Locality: in the back) has some baby jaundice - a bit too much "Billy Rubin

File.Name: 9223a_9222-075.jpg
Notes:
47542254 It's a leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family, with raindrops. These lupines hold raindrops so perfectly.

Now look closer... Inside the large center raindrop is a trapped spider. I guess by the shape of its body that it's a "Garbage Line Spider" (Cyclosa). I watched it hypnotically for a long time as it spun slowly around and around. How did it get stuck here? I'll bet it was having the same thoughts! (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2009)

It was fun visiting my new grandkids in Portland, but it's good to be home doing what I do best - finding new photos in the woods!
CommonNames: Canyon Lupine; Pea; Garbage Line Spider

File.Name: 9223b_9222-063.jpg
Notes:
47541746 I guess this a female Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) perched in a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the creek.

But look closer... At the top of her abdomen (the bottom in the photo) you can see the spider's epigynum which is it's sexual part. It looks just like a human male part - the sort you see in drawings in high school classes. It warms my heart that nature has a sense of humor! (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2009)

File.Name: 9223c_9222-067.jpg
Notes: 47541458 For some reason, the Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) were interested in this one new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae family in the canyon. There were many other flowers without ants. I wonder what they saw (or smelled) in that particular flower? (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2009)

File.Name: 9224a_9222-086.jpg
Notes:
47583100 This is the strange maze of a "daedaloid" (maze-like) mushroom in the woods - maybe the Gilled Polypore (Lenzites betulina)? - growing from a fallen oak log in the woods.

Now look closer... Can you see the little beetle on top trying to navigate the maze? Of course walking over it is cheating! (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2009)

File.Name: 9224b_9222-059.jpg
Notes:
47582830 This is a brain-like Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) streaming in the woods. I got a photo of these critters a week ago, and they're still doing their stuff. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2009)

These are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part protist. They live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying wood. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form this visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes as you can see in the photo. They will end up as tiny little mushroom-like structures that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying log or leaf pile to another.

File.Name: 9224c_9222-050.jpg
Notes:
47582593 Stuck in the rain...

This is a handsome Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera that was stuck on a garden leaf during the rain on Sunday. I guess it decided to go out, and then found it was was too cold to do anything. I've been there too!

This wasp was still perched on the same leaf when I got back from my walk 3 hours later. It was gone the next morning. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2009)

File.Name: 9225a_9214-023.jpg
Notes: 47622499 I found this beautiful - and very slimy - Cortinarius cylindripes (or kin) mushroom in the woods last weekend. Maybe it's edible, and maybe not. My mushroom book says "Better eyed than fried; the snot-like universal will deter most." (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2009)

File.Name: 9225b_9222-093.jpg
Notes: 47622195 I found this beautiful red Russula mushroom in the soggy woods on Sunday growing with the native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens). I guess it is the Emetic Russula (Russula emetica - or maybe R. rosacea?). It's not edible, but it has a spicy hot taste and I like to nibble. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2009)
ScientificName: Russula; Agrostis pallens; Emetic Russula (Russula emetica; R. rosacea
associatedTaxa: Russula; Agrostis pallens; Emetic Russula (Russula emetica; R. rosacea

File.Name: 9225c_9222-087.jpg
Notes: 47621931 Another photo of the "daedaloid" (maze-like) mushroom in my[Previous] photo - maybe the Gilled Polypore (Lenzites betulina)? It's growing from a fallen oak log in the woods. That's another specimen in the lower left. These bracket fungi grow whole crops of algae on their caps. It is the way with life, that one species depends on another. (San Marcos Pass, 22 February 2009)

File.Name: 9226a_9226-137.jpg
Notes:
47699761 I guess (by the process of elimination) that this critter is a Burrowing Bee (family Andenidae) in the superfamily Apoidea of the Hymenoptera, but I really don't know. It has unusually long antennae! It's resting on new flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 February 2009)

I got this photo in Tequepis Canyon during my after-school DMS Hike Club - I wasn't equipped for macro shots, but I did the best I could hand-held with my zoom lens. Hike club was followed by a Natural History Society meeting. Long night!

* (Update 1 March 2009) Thanks to my friend and fellow photographer Lynn Watson, I now believe that this is a Long-horned bee (genus Eucera) of the family Apidae. That name makes more sense! See Lynn's photo of the same critter at BugGuide,
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/253546
Locality: genus Eucera) of the family Apidae. That name makes more sense! See Lynn's photo of the same critter at BugGuide
CommonNames: Burrowing Bee; Milk Maids; Toothwort; Mustard; Long-horned bee
ScientificName: Andenidae; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cardamine californica; Brassicaceae; Eucera; Apidae
associatedTaxa: Andenidae; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cardamine californica; Brassicaceae; Eucera; Apidae

File.Name: 9227a_9227-200.jpg
Notes:
47700265 A funny thing happened over the last two busy weeks - busy with a trip to visit my new grandkids, and then busy with school grades and comments and a Natural History Society meeting last night - it's spring!

Here are glorious new flowers of a native Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primrose (Primulaceae) plant family after school today near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 February 2009)

File.Name: 9227b_9227-203.jpg
Notes: 47700070 Here are new flowers of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family growing near Lake Cachuma. The flowers are unusually blue/lilac colored - they are usually white. The spring flowers cover the shrubs - sometimes whole hillsides - with a white bloom like snow. The individual flowers are remarkable up close. I got this photo after school today near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 February 2009)

File.Name: 9228a_9228-008.jpg
Notes:
47730883 Slugs have teeth?

Well, sort of. This is a giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) terrestial mollusk slurping up the decaying remains of a clump of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods today. It does have tooth-like soft mouthparts to help slurp up the goo. The goo smells like a diaper pail. Yes, it's pretty disgusting, but to each his own! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)

File.Name: 9228b_9228-054.jpg
Notes:
47730302 Here's what our Slime Molds (Myxomycetes) are up to. They've just about finished streaming - though you can still see a network of streams. Now they're forming new spore-bearing, mushroom-like structures. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)

I've been tracking these slime molds for a few weeks. Usually they develop faster. See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/47582830 (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/47013175 (9 Feb. 2009)

These are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part protist. They live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying wood. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes. They end up as tiny little mushroom-like structures like this photo shows that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying log or leaf pile to another.
Locality:
