These are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part protist. They live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying wood. When the weather is right

File.Name: 9228c_9227-187.jpg
Notes: 47729622 Interesting walnut-sized flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family growing near Lake Cachuma. These plants usually hide under the chaparral, but this area near Lake Cachuma burned a few years ago to give them light. The perennial roots used to be collected locally for export to China as a medicinal herb. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 February 2009)

File.Name: 9301a_9301-060.jpg
Notes:
47764437 I caught this pair of Pacific Tree Frogs (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) underwater this morning in the embrace known as "amplexus". It's defined as "pseudocopulation" because fertilization in frogs is external. The male holds the female, but egg and sperm meet outside of the body. The female releases her eggs, which the male frog then covers with a sperm solution. It looks like they still have a moment of closeness! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2009)

The frogs have been singing every night, but I usually don't see them. They stop singing and become invisible whenever I get close! Their "pond" is just an old bathtub that Julie equipped with a cheap submersible pump to recirculate the water. You should do the same - frogs need our help right now, and the chorus of frog song at night is wonderful!

File.Name: 9301b_9301-086.jpg
Notes: 47762928 Look close! This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has caught an interesting Fly (order Diptera) for breakfast. It's perched among the new umbel flowers of native Tauschia arguta in the Parsley (Apiaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2009)

File.Name: 9301c_9228-029.jpg
Notes:
47762215 This native Land Snail (Gastropoda) is a distant cousin to the giant Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) in my [Previous] photo. I only found the shell in the woods on my walk yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)

This snail has a flat shell that turns clockwise. It is NOT one of the nasty introduced Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in town and at school. We don't have those snails at home.

I rarely notice the live native snails in the woods. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=9497119
CommonNames: Land Snail; Pacific Banana Slug; Garden Snails

File.Name: 9302a_9301-072.jpg
Notes:
47805322 This is the beautiful but deadly "Destroying Angel" (Amanita ocreata) mushroom pushing up a "mushrump" in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2009)

It lives up to its name. The mushroom has a mostly white cap and free gills. The cap is not "striate" with lines from the gills underneath. It has the remnants of a ring on the stem that are the remains of the "partial veil" membrane that once covered the gills and tore apart as the cap expanded. You can't see the sacklike "volva" or "death cup" that sheaths the base of the stalk like a broken eggshell in this photo - and that's been the bane of many mushroom collectors! I'll post another photo tomorrow that shows it.

This mushroom looks ordinary, but it is deadly poisonous with one of the most lethal natural toxins known. It is elegant and beautiful. I love the name. It's not dangerous to handle or photograph!

File.Name: 9302b_9228-044.jpg
Notes:
47804293 Remarkable Pine Cone Amanita (Amanita magniverrucata aka A. strobiliformis) mushroom pushing up a "mushrump" in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The warty cap is impressive, but I'm more impressed with the warty volva at the base. I wouldn't taste any white amanita! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)

This beautiful mushroom looks so solid and architectural! My books say it lasts for weeks without decaying. It may be a source of interesting antibiotics.
CommonNames: Pine Cone Amanita; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Amanita magniverrucata; A. strobiliformis; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Amanita magniverrucata; A. strobiliformis; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 9302c_9301-071.jpg
Notes:
47803413 A beautiful - and tasty! - Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom pushing up a "mushrump" in the woods yesterday. I picked it this afternoon to cook up at school tomorow as a treat for my Hike Club kids. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2009)

This seems to be Chanterelle time. I found half a dozen over the weekend, and I noticed them for sale in the local market today for $25 a pound. This one mushroom must weigh at least half a pound.

This makes me nervous - it's like putting little price tags and barcodes on the banana slugs and butterflies and native lilies in the woods. Be a naturalist and find your own!

Of course, you should never trust what you read and see on the internet for your mushroom ID! Find a local expert (meaning someone still alive) you trust for your first forays! Start with relatively safe mushrooms like this.

File.Name: 9303a_9228-023.jpg
Notes:
47842644 Me and my shadow!

This early-season American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly is "puddling" - getting moisture or salts - on the same sun-facing sandstone rock outcrop as my [Previous] photo. I was surprised to see it last weekend. I don't know if it overwinters or is an early migrant. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)

These early butterflies all look the same - until you look closer at the patterns of white...

File.Name: 9303b_9228-028.jpg
Notes: 47842580 This beautiful California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly is "puddling" - getting moisture or salts - on a sun-facing sandstone rock outcrop in the canyon. I believe these butterflies overwinter as adults so that they can fly early in the spring. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)

File.Name: 9303c_9301-076.jpg
Notes:
47842532 This is another shot of the beautiful but deadly "Destroying Angel" (Amanita ocreata) mushroom in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2009)

It has pure white free gills. You can see the remnants of a ring on the stem that are the remains of the "partial veil" membrane that once covered the gills and tore apart as the cap expanded. You can also see the sacklike "volva" or "death cup" that sheaths the base of the stalk like a broken eggshell - that's the "universal veil" that originally enclosed the mushroom like an egg shell. Many Amanitas keep remnants of this veil on the cap as warts or a patch, but not this one.

This impressive mushroom is deadly poisonous, but it is elegant and beautiful. It's not dangerous to handle or photograph!

File.Name: 9304a_9214-037.jpg
Notes: 47881532 A troop of tiny spore-bearing Moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes growing on a rock in the canyon. Mosses are commonplace, but they have a strange lifecycle with alternating haploid and diploid forms that look very different. I guess we do too, but we don't usually think of it that way! Open your botany textbook to see how strange they really are. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)

File.Name: 9304b_9228-050.jpg
Notes: 47881295 A troop of the tiny sporophytes of native Liverworts (Marchantia or Asterella?) - related to mosses - on a sandstone outcrop still wet from recent rain. I don't see these "flower" every year. We're still well below normal rainfall this year, but the recent rains - including an inch, 2+ cm today - have been well-spaced to keep the woods wet this spring. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)

File.Name: 9304c_9228-047.jpg
Notes:
47881032 The final new butterfly I managed to photograph last weekend is a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) perched like a solar panel on a leaf of native Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. This buitterfly is a "Blue" and you can see some blue on the upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)

I also saw a Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) and a Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly flutter by, but I couldn't get a photo. It's spring!
CommonNames: Spring Azure; Big-berry Manzanita; Heath; Mourning Cloak; Sara Orangetip
ScientificName: Celastrina ladon; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Nymphalis antiopa; Anthocharis sara
associatedTaxa: Celastrina ladon; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Nymphalis antiopa; Anthocharis sara

File.Name: 9305a_9301-080.jpg
Notes:
47917085 Elegant new flower of a native Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) wildflower in the Iris (Iridaceae) family growing in the woods near home. It's our only native Iris. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2009)

It's spring, and the wildflowers are coming out! It's not the best wildflower year - we've had less than 60% of normal rainfall - but the spacing of the rains has been good for the wildflowers, so far. Where are the insect pollinators for these new flowers?

File.Name: 9305b_9301-084.jpg
Notes:
47916860 Remarkable new flower head of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. The red flowers are visible among the red leaves below. The two stamens are bundled inside the flowers - you can see the outlines in silhouette.

This early native plant has mostly red leaves with just a bit of green. It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs. You usually have to crawl under the chaparral to find it, but this patch was exposed by brush-clearing on the Gap Fire last summer. There were lots of flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2009)

File.Name: 9305c_9301-064.jpg
Notes:
47916519 Perfect new flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. It's pretty much right from my camera with no fiddling with contrast or saturation. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2009)

I guess it's a hummingbird flower, but it also offers the broad lower lip of the flower as a landing platform for large bees. The flowers are tasty, and the fragrant leaves are good for tea.

File.Name: 9306a_9228-017.jpg
Notes: 47951243 Strange Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum or kin) mushrooms in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. Note the white branching ribs on the outside of the cups. These mushrooms are common along trails in our canyon, but probably not edible. I'm not tempted. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)

File.Name: 9306b_9228-035.jpg
Notes: 47951014 Strange Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushroom in the woods, the first I've seen this year. I call it the "Brain Mushroom" because that`s what it looks like. Said to be edible, but I'm not tempted. Sometimes when you touch the cap, it shoots off a little cloud of spores! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2009)

File.Name: 9306c_9301-089.jpg
Notes: 47950844 The beautiful new spring flowers of native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primrose (Primulaceae) plant family have finally come up to the mountains! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2009)

File.Name: 9307a_9307-044.jpg
Notes:
47978556 I'm pretty sure this beautiful mushroom in the woods today is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis). Like all Agaricus mushrooms, it has chocolate-brown spores and a thick felty ring on the stem, and it lacks the "death cup" volva at the base of the stem. It looks delicious, but it is poisonous to many people. It has an unpleasant phenol-like taste. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2009)

It was windy today whenever the sun was shining. At least the mushrooms weren't blowing around!

File.Name: 9307b_9307-013.jpg
Notes:
47977504 This is "The Blusher" (Amanita rubescens) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2009)

The cap has white warts left from the universal veil, and it developes reddish "blushing" colors like this. It is said to be edible when cooked - and highly esteemed in France - but I'm not tempted by any white Amanitas - the genus that includes our most deadly fungi, see my [Previous] photo. That could be a fatal mistake!

File.Name: 9307c_9307-048.jpg
Notes:
47976854 To paraphrase William Burroughs, "Nobody owns life, but anyone who can pick this mushroom owns death."

This is the Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom I found in the woods today. It's remarkable to hold this mushroom knowing that it is deadly poison, but it is not dangerous to hold it or photograph it! You can see the "death cup" - really the saclike volva - at the base of the stipe. It's one of our most beautiful mushrooms - pristine white, like finding a styrofoam cup in the woods. Enjoy it, but don't eat it! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2009)

File.Name: 9308-112.jpg
Notes: 48013368 Nasty-looking Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) with impressive mandibles perched on a leaf of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum in the Grossulariaceae family). Is he sticking his tongue (etc.) out at me? Maybe that's the insect equivalent to giving the finger! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2009)

File.Name: 9308a_9308-099.jpg
Notes: 48013233 Handsome preditory long-horned Lacewing insect in the order Neuroptera working new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2009)

File.Name: 9308b_9308-085.jpg
Notes: 48012734 Little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) getting a mouthfull of pollen from a new flower of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2009)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Snowball; Hoary-leaved Ceanothus; Buckthorn

File.Name: 9308c_9308-090.jpg
Notes:
48047107 Ride 'em cowboy!

This giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) terrestial mollusk is rearing up like a bucking bronco! They only have the breathing hole on the right side. These critters seem to move slow - until you try to photograph them in the low light they prefer, and then they move way too fast for the slow shutter speeds you need! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2009)

File.Name: 9309a_9307-064.jpg
Notes: 48047028 New umbel flowers of native Tauschia arguta in the Parsley (Apiaceae) plant family. The plant is sometimes known as the Southern Tauschia, but it deserves a better proper name. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2009)

File.Name: 9309b_9308-114.jpg
Notes: 48044190 I believe this is a different Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) perched on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the woods this weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2009)

File.Name: 9309c_9307-032.jpg
Notes:
48168448 I believe this wildflower is the real Death Camas (Zigadenus venenosus) in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family. As both common and scientific names suggest, they are good for photos, but not to eat! (Figueroa Mountain, 11 March 2009)

I had some time to kill after school on Wednesday, so I went for a drive up Figueroa Mountain - even though it was cloudy and windy as a dry storm moved in. The Death Camas is doing well, and seems to be spreading. I only find it growing on the serpentine mineral soils which inhibit many other plants. It's unusual in our mountains - it's cousin Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) is more common.
CommonNames: Death Camas; Lily; Chaparral Death Camas

File.Name: 9310a_9301-092.jpg
Notes: 48201177 Intense deep-blue/indigo native Larkspur - I guess Delphinium patens but cp. D. parryi - in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family growing in the woods near Lake Cachuma yesterday. (Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2009)

File.Name: 9311a_9308-075.jpg
Notes:
48229190 Beautiful new flower head of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. The red flowers are visible among the red leaves. There are two stamens bundled inside each flower - you can see the outlines in silhouette. (Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2009)

This early native plant has mostly red leaves with just a bit of green. It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs - probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum).

File.Name: 9312a_9308-074.jpg
Notes:
48257902 This uncommon local wildflower is native California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifrage (Saxifragaceae) plant family. The pretty little flowers hide in the north-facing canyons of the mountains. I found this one in the woods near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2009)

Fotolog has been cranky lately with uploads, but that suits me just fine. The weather has been crummy, and I'm busy with my DMS Science Fair project on plant galls, due a week from Friday on March 27. I'm the teacher, but I always do my own project.

File.Name: 9313a_9311-152.jpg
Notes:
48290127 Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending little Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on this branch of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. Whew, that's a lot of latin! (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2009)

The aphids are really tiny. It's seems like the ants are invidually placing them, a few ants to each colony. There are dozens - maybe hundreds - of colonies in this one shrub.

It was tricky getting this photo in the low-light shade against the bright sky. I had to stop-down my exposure 3 full f/stops. I held my breath and took the photo with time-delay.
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: 9314a_9313-201.jpg
Notes: 48320206 More interesting Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) in the woods. These ants are working a strange "flower" of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae family. Usually this plant has large "sunflower" type flowers with ray and disk flowers, but this flower is different. Has it been galled? (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2009)

File.Name: 9315a_9313-195.jpg
Notes:
48351653 Shy Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) swimming in the frog pond on Kinevan Ranch, located on the other side of San Marcos Pass. Julie and I used to live in a nearby cabin many years ago, and we had our kids here. One of my kids' earliest memories is going to the frog pond and saying "Shhhh"... moments later the frogs would start their deafening chorus!

I went back to the old digs today with my after-school Hike Club kids. This frog was floating just out of reach. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2009)

File.Name: 9316a_9313-198.jpg
Notes: 48383692 This pretty Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) was perched on a leaf in the woods last weekend trying to warm up on a cold morning. I guess it might be an Andrenid Burrowing Bee (Andrenidae). (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2009)

File.Name: 9317a_9315-036.jpg
Notes:
48383398 This is a lucky shot of a Cynipid Gall Wasp in the Cynipidae family of the order Hymenoptera). I was trying to get a photo of the new catkin flowers of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata in the Fagaceae family). The new staminate catkins look like bunches of bananas! I only noticed this little wasp on the computer screen when I was looking at my photos. This is a very tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2009)

I happen to be working on my science fair project on insect galls this weekend. (I'm the teacher, but I always do my own project.) Expect to see lots more gall photos next week as I go through my archives!

File.Name: 9318a_9315-028.jpg
Notes: 48382845 New frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) unrolling in the woods near home. This is our "summer fern". Most other native ferns have been open all winter. (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2009)

File.Name: 9319a_9319-129.jpg
Notes:
48417095 Beautiful new flowers of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum), aka Brodiaea for the squeamish, in the Lily (Liliaceae) plant family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 13 March 2009)

It was drizzly and overcast today - not a photo day, and there's a chance of snow tonight! Just as well. I've been working on my science fair project on "Plant Galls of San Marcos Pass" due next Friday. Yipes!

File.Name: 9320a_9314-007.jpg
Notes: 48416718 Perfect blue flowers of Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii var. integrifolia) in the woods near Lake Cachuma. This native flower is part of the dificult Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family. Nothing else has a color like this! (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2009)

File.Name: 9320b_9313-185.jpg
Notes: 48416580 New catkins of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis in the Salicaceae family) by the creek. At least they were new a week ago! (San Marcos Pass, 14 March 2009)

File.Name: 9320c_9314-001.jpg
Notes:
48449750 Pretty insect galls of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii aka Cynips douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidaceae in the family Fagaceae). (San+ Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)

File.Name: 9321a_9313-172.jpg
Notes:
48139004 Interesting new flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family on the Pass. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2009)

I've been finding these native plants near Lake Cachuma in an area that burned a few years ago, but these are the first ones I've found for many years near home. The plants are growing in a place that was cleared (and "masticated") last summer as part of a fire break. I've been finding many "fire-follower" plants in this mechanically cleared place. I guess many of the fire-follower plants don't actually need fire to sprout, but just a bit of clearing and sunshine.

The perennial roots used to be collected locally for export to China as a medicinal herb.
Locality: and "masticated") last summer as part of a fire break.  I've been finding many "fire-follower" plants in this mechanically cleared place.  I guess many of the fire-follower plants don't actually need fire to sprout

File.Name: 9321c_9314-020.jpg
Notes: 48079365 New flowers and leaves of deciduous native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Maple (Aceraceae) family growing along San Jose Creek a mile from home. It was windy, but at least I documented the emergence of these interesting flowers. It's our only native maple, though we also have native Box Elder (Acer negundo var. californicum) growing along the river. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2009)

Everything is now printed for my science fair project on "Insect Galls of San Marcos Pass" due on Friday. I hope the kids are working as hard on their projects as I am on mine!

(San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2008)

File.Name: 9326c_8907-050.jpg
Notes:
48653938 Here is my poster board for the DMS Science Fair. The subject is my collection of plants galls found (mostly) near my home on San Marcos Pass. I take the photos and write the text, but Julie makes the display look beautiful. I also had an Excel database and a Powerpoint slideshow of 350 photos. Whew, it's all done! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2009)

It was a fun science fair - the kids came through again!

This is my last DMS science fair - at least as the person responsible. I've been the person in charge of 26 annual DMS science fairs, and I'm happy to pass it on to Eric!

I'm semi-retiring next year. I'll still do electives and hike club and take school photos, but I'll be done with academic classes, grades, conferences, and all that stuff. Yahoo!

Notes:
48656560 Calling all insects - be careful!

Yellow on yellow - this Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is perched on a Daffodil (Narcissus sp.) in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2009)

I suspect these spiders are color shifters.

File.Name: 9328a_9328-032.jpg
Notes: 48656137 These interesting native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) were very interested in a new bud of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods today. I wonder why? (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2009)

File.Name: 9328b_9328-027.jpg
Notes: 48655899 Not a great photo, but a great butterfly! This is a beautiful green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly resting on native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the woods today. I think of this as a "tag photo" - I got you on this date! (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2009)

File.Name: 9328c_9328-012.jpg
Notes:
48690719 This Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is perched on a Daffodil (Narcissus sp.) in the garden with its prey. I reckon the color-shifting spider recently moved from the yellow flowers to this white one? (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2009)

I'm heading off to the amazing Santa Cruz Island for a week of camping with the 7th grade DMS students. I'll be back in a week with new photos!

File.Name: 9329a_9328-036.jpg
Notes: 48690222 These new flowers of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family look like perfect little origami boxes! (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2009)

File.Name: 9329b_9328-020.jpg
Notes: 48689943 Beautiful new flowers of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha in the Buttercup Ranunculaceae) family). It's a woody perennial vine. This plant is growing on the edge of the garden at home. It's the first time it's flowered in 10+ years! (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2009)

File.Name: 9329c_9329-041.jpg
Notes: 48897919 Beautiful Purple Needle Grass (Nassella pulchra) in full flower. It's a native grass that is showing up in the grasslands on the eastern end of Santa Cruz Island. Given enough time, I hope the natives will become dominant again. After all, they are native! (Santa Cruz Island, 31 March 2009)

File.Name: 9404a_9331-030.jpg
Notes: 48897535 This Island Monkey Flower (Mimulus flemingii) in the Figwort Scrophulariaceae) family is endemic to several of the Channel Islands across the Santa Barbara Channel. I found it on our school trip to Santa Cruz Island last week. It's remarkable that it doesn't grow anywhere else. (Santa Cruz Island, 31 March 2009)

File.Name: 9404b_9331-043.jpg
Notes:
48910607 Treebeard and entwife Julie on top of 1808 foot high El Montanon peak - the highest point on the east end of Santa Cruz Island. This was literally the high point of a wonderful school camping trip last week on Santa Cruz Island in the Channel Islands National Park. (Santa Cruz Island, 31 March 2009)

We've been doing a spring backback trip with my school for many years, and this photo is a tradition - except it's not my usual backpacking photo. Julie fell on the hike down the mountain and seriously slashed her knee. She had to leave the trip early.

Check out:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/32713658
and follow the link from there.

Whew, it's been a busy couple of weeks. First the DMS science Fair, then the camping trip to the islands, and tomorrow we're flying up to Portland, Oregon to visit our grandkids over spring break. I'll be back next weekend with new photos of the twins!
